title: #eng-sre: 1740431902
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up: before we do broad rollout of incident-bot v2, we need to update a handful of runbooks. Beta folks had good instincts but a lot of "where do I click" / "what does this signal mean" in the thread. Can we list what needs updates + assign owners here? (tracking under ENG-5431)

Miles Thornton: +1. GA checklist should include "runbooks updated + linked from cards". In beta retro we had ppl misread config diffs as global when it was per-tenant.

Aisha Bello: Dumping the runbooks/standards I think are gating:
- Incident bot triage playbook (first 5 min): how to use cards, when to trust cached vs live, what to do when empty
- Recent deploys + config diff debugging: how to interpret rollout phases, partial propagation, canary vs full
- GPU saturation triage: map GPU util / queue depth / KV cache pressure / scheduler backpressure -> mitigations
- Saturation signals standardization doc: metric names/labels/thresholds (we need to align the bot text with the standard)
- Diagnostic signal <-> SLO burn mapping: which links to open for latency vs 5xx vs streaming disconnect patterns
- Incident context collection guidelines: payload field names + deep link format so every card is consistent
- Permissions/audit: what scopes are required, break-glass steps, and what gets logged

Jacob Stein: :eyes: on the GPU saturation triage doc. It’s currently written for runtime folks, not oncall. Needs a "if bot says X, do Y" section + copy/paste console links.

Allison Grant: I can take incident-context-collection-guidelines + make sure the deep-link format matches the cards. Also add the "query budgets" guidance (what we do when telemetry is slow) so oncall doesn’t keep smashing refresh.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll own the recent deploys runbook updates. Main gap is rollout phase semantics (canary healthy but only in us-east, etc.) + how to verify with deploy timeline if bot’s deploy registry is behind.

Miles Thornton: I’ll update the incident-bot triage playbook (first 5 min). Need to include:
- what the 3 cards are (Deploy / Config diff / Saturation)
- what “degraded mode” looks like (cached context + links)
- when to page platform vs telemetry if context is missing
- how to use the Slack actions (expand diff, open dashboards, copy commands)

Dylan Brooks: Can I help with the playbook section on Slack UX + failure modes? We learned a bunch from the timeout handling work (ack deadline, retries, idempotency). I can add screenshots/gifs if that’s useful.

Miles Thornton: yes pls. send me whatever you have from the UX mock thread.

Aisha Bello: I’ll take diagnostic-signal-slo-mapping (latency + 5xx + streaming disconnects). We should make sure the “recommended next links” in the bot match the doc 1:1. Also will add paging/alert hints (e.g. when to treat GPU util as symptom vs cause).

Kevin Osei: For permissions/audit runbook: I can draft the "what gets audited" + retention + how to request access. But we should have Naomi/Marcus sign off on wording (don’t want folks thinking break-glass is casual).

Naomi Feldman: Yep. I’ll review and add the "customer data handling" disclaimers (redaction, do not paste raw diffs into public channels, etc.). Also clarify that config diffs are tenant-safe but still should be treated as sensitive.

Rafael Mendes: Great. Can we turn this into a concrete checklist w/ owners + due date? Proposing we aim to have docs merged before GA rollout window (targeting next Wed).

Allison Grant: Works. I’ll have a PR up by EOD Fri for guidelines + link formats.

Jacob Stein: I can update GPU saturation triage by Monday morning. Need input from Logan/Nadia on the exact KV cache pressure thresholds we show (the numbers changed recently).

Aisha Bello: I’ll sync w/ Allison on naming so we don’t say "queue depth" in one place and "scheduler queue" in another.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy runbook by Monday EOD.

Miles Thornton: Triage playbook draft by Tuesday lunchtime.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll send Miles the Slack UX notes today + can review the playbook draft for accuracy.

Rafael Mendes: Summarizing owners:
- Incident bot triage playbook (first 5 min): Miles (+ Dylan for Slack UX sections)
- Recent deploys + config diff debugging: Connor
- GPU saturation triage: Jacob
- Incident context collection guidelines + deep link formats + query budget guidance: Allison
- Diagnostic signal <-> SLO burn mapping: Aisha
- Permissions/audit + break-glass + handling guidance: Kevin (draft) + Naomi (review/sign-off)

Rafael Mendes: Also: can someone sanity check the saturation signals standardization doc vs what bot prints today? We had minor mismatch in label names.

Allison Grant: I can do that as part of guidelines update. If it’s bigger than text tweaks I’ll flag Logan.

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll add a small "terminology" section to SLO mapping doc too.

Rafael Mendes: Perfect. Please drop links to the PRs in ENG-5431 when ready. GA gate = all runbooks updated + referenced from bot cards.

Miles Thornton: ack. will also add a short "if card empty" troubleshooting snippet at the bottom w/ common causes (missing scope, telemetry timeout, service registry lag).

Kevin Osei: +1 and include "don’t retry 50x" guidance. Bot load-shedding will kick in and you’ll just get cached anyway.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: I’ll update the rollout checklist doc to include this. Thanks everyone.

title: #eng-sre: 1740342217
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Heads up: trying to close out GA readiness for Dedicated Burst this week. Can folks reply w/ status on the final checklist items + any remaining blockers?

Sean Gallagher: Checklist (please confirm ✅ / 🚫):
1) Runbooks complete + linked from alerts (burst pool overload, circuit breaker, denial spikes)
2) Alerts tuned (noise acceptable) + paging policy agreed
3) Game day executed + outcomes documented
4) Rollback plan + kill switches validated (per-tenant, per-region/hw allowlist, global circuit breaker)
5) SLO regression gate enabled (auto-reduce/disable burst when non-burst p95 regresses)

Rafael Mendes: ✅ Runbook: burst pool overload mitigation is in place and reviewed w/ oncall. I added a “first 10 minutes” section + comms template to Support/CS.
Link: confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/burst-pool-overload-mitigation-runbook

Rafael Mendes: One note: we should add a short FAQ for “why are we returning 429 vs queueing” since that’s already come up in beta. But not a GA blocker.

Allison Grant: On alerts: mostly ✅ but want 1 more pass on label cardinality for denial reason breakdown. In staging the “reason” label is ok, but the “tenant_id” label leaked into one panel query and blew up.

Allison Grant: If we’re keeping tenant-level alerting, it has to be via recording rules / pre-aggregations, not ad-hoc queries. Sergio and I can patch today.

Sergio Costa: ✅ Dashboards are in and linked from runbook. I’ll push an update to remove tenant_id from any default dashboard queries.
Grafana: “Dedicated Burst - Pool Health” + “Dedicated Burst - Denials & Reasons”

Sergio Costa: Alert status:
- burst_pool_saturation_high (page) ✅
- burst_denial_rate_spike (ticket) ✅
- nonburst_p95_latency_delta (page) ✅ but threshold might be a touch sensitive (see below)

Peter Holtz: For rollback plan: ✅ feature flags + kill switches verified in prod canary env.
- per-tenant enable flag works
- per-region/hw allowlist works (denylist override works too)
- global circuit breaker flips routing to baseline only within ~30s

Peter Holtz: We should explicitly document “what customers see” when CB trips. Right now it’s clean: baseline routes keep serving, burst requests get 429 with the new denial code.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet side ✅ on safety caps. Current caps match what we agreed in sizing policy for us-east H100 + eu-west L40S pools.

Vanessa Ortiz: One risk: eu-west supply is still tighter than we want. I’d prefer GA enablement there stays behind a stricter pool utilization gate until we’re a week stable in us-east.

Keiko Yamamoto: Guardrails ✅ cooldown + fairness are in. The telemetry smoothing hotfix is already rolled into the current build.

Keiko Yamamoto: Ask: confirm we’re ok with default cooldown=5m and max burst ratio=2.0x for GA? We can tune later but need to lock for docs.

Ethan Park: Control plane ✅. Validation rules + audit events are live. Support tooling has the debug endpoint (redacted) so we can explain admit/deny.

Ethan Park: Only thing I’m waiting on is final sign-off on the denial reason code mapping to HTTP responses (429 vs 503). We’re consistent w/ ADR: 429 for capacity/guardrail denials, 503 for internal admit controller errors.

Lauren Bishop: From Product ✅ with the staged rollout plan Vanessa mentioned. For GA announcement we’ll message “GA in us-east first; eu-west follow after stability gate.”

Lauren Bishop: Re cooldown / 2.0x: ✅ agree for GA defaults. Enterprise tiers can override with approvals.

Talia Benson: Game day status: ✅ executed yesterday.
Scenarios we ran:
- synthetic burst spike (3 tenants) + verify non-burst p95 delta
- induced telemetry lag + confirm no starvation (hotfix held)
- admit controller timeout + routing fallback behavior
- global CB trip + rollback drill

Talia Benson: Notes are here: confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/burst-capacity-game-day-plan (updated with outcomes + action items). Only open action is Allison/Sergio dashboard query cleanup.

Allison Grant: On SLO gate (non-burst protection): ✅ gate is enabled, but I’m slightly worried about sensitivity.
Current: if non-burst p95 latency delta > 12% for 10m in a region/hw class, auto-reduce burst caps by 50%; if > 20% for 5m, disable burst.

Allison Grant: In the game day we got a brief ~13% delta from a load test artifact (client retry storm). It recovered quickly but would have triggered a reduction. Not sure if that’s desired.

Peter Holtz: That’s probably ok? Reducing burst briefly under retry storms is acceptable. But we should make sure it doesn’t flap.

Sean Gallagher: Agree w/ Peter: better to err conservative on GA. But we should add hysteresis / minimum hold time to avoid toggling.

Keiko Yamamoto: We already have a 15m “hold” after a reduction before we consider increasing caps again. For full disable we require manual re-enable unless the “auto re-enable” flag is set (default off). So no flapping there.

Sergio Costa: I’ll update the alert annotations to include “hold time behavior” so oncall doesn’t panic when it stays reduced.

Rafael Mendes: Also added to runbook: “If SLO gate triggers, check retry rates + request backlog; coordinate w/ Support on customer comms.”

Ethan Park: Re HTTP mapping: +1 to the current behavior. If it’s a guardrail denial, 429 w/ denial_reason is the right signal to clients.

Sean Gallagher: OK. Action items before I sign off:
1) Allison/Sergio: remove tenant_id cardinality from default dashboards + confirm alerts only use safe labels
2) Rafael: add quick FAQ snippet (429 vs queue) to runbook (non-blocking but would like before announcement)
3) Vanessa/Lauren: confirm GA is us-east first w/ eu-west gated (sounds agreed)

Allison Grant: ✅ Will patch dashboard queries + recording rules today. Will reply when merged.

Sergio Costa: ✅ same, I’ll push the grafana json update + double-check the alert queries don’t include tenant_id.

Rafael Mendes: ✅ I can add the 429/queue FAQ in the next hour.

Vanessa Ortiz: ✅ Confirmed. Fleet is good w/ us-east GA first. eu-west will remain allowlist-only until utilization gate holds for 7 days.

Lauren Bishop: ✅ Confirmed on messaging/rollout. I’ll update the GA brief + sales/CS talking points.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Assuming the dashboard patch lands today, I’m marking eng-sre GA readiness ✅. Please drop links in-thread once merged so we have an audit trail.

title: #eng-sre: 1740037715
preview:
Allison Grant: hey all — need to lock initial alert thresholds for the new cache dashboards before we turn them on in prod. goal is “regression signal w/o paging on workload mix changes”.

Allison Grant: current candidate alerts:
- prefix cache hit rate drop
- KV cache reuse effectiveness drop
- eviction pressure sustained high
- wasted prefill ratio spike

Allison Grant: question: what should page vs ticket, and what dimensions do we alert on (global vs region vs model)?

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should be super conservative on paging for anything cache-efficiency related. it’s a guardrail SLO, not a customer SLA.

Aisha Bello: agree. hit-rate swings a *lot* with prompt length / traffic mix. if we page on “hit rate down” alone we’re going to hate ourselves.

Sergio Costa: fwiw I can wire 2 severities:
- SEV2 page (oncall)
- SEV4 ticket (triage in hours)

Sergio Costa: but need crisp predicates to avoid false positives.

Neha Kapoor: we should only page when there’s strong evidence it’s a platform/runtime regression (not traffic mix). multi-signal gating.

Allison Grant: ok. what gating do we want?

Aisha Bello: recommend:
1) require enough volume (otherwise noise): e.g. `reqs > 500/min` for the evaluated slice
2) hit-rate drop must be *absolute* + *sustained* (avoid short spikes)
3) confirm with eviction/pressure or miss-reason shift

Sean Gallagher: + check “telemetry missing” separately. if metrics go to zero due to ingestion/cardinality drop, we should detect that and page (because dashboards go blind).

Logan Wright: +1 telemetry-missing is actionable and usually a pipeline/config issue. cache-efficiency dips are often “real” but not urgent.

Allison Grant: makes sense. for efficiency alerting: global only? or per region?

Sean Gallagher: I’d do:
- ticket on global + per region
- page only on global (or 2+ regions) to reduce flappy pages

Neha Kapoor: yup. paging on a single region hit-rate dip will be constant during capacity shifts.

Sergio Costa: ok. let me propose something in promql terms. rough sketch:

Sergio Costa: ```
# 15m rolling hit-rate for prefix cache (weighted)
hr = sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_hits_total[15m]))
     /
     sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_lookups_total[15m]))

# 15m wasted prefill ratio
wpr = sum(rate(redwood_cache_wasted_prefill_tokens_total[15m]))
      /
      sum(rate(redwood_prefill_tokens_total[15m]))

# pressure (gauge)
press = avg_over_time(redwood_cache_kv_memory_pressure[10m])

# req volume
qps = sum(rate(redwood_requests_total[5m]))
```

Sergio Costa: thresholds TBD.

Aisha Bello: for thresholds, we should use “drop from baseline” not absolute numbers. some models are inherently lower hit rate.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also note we *cannot* baseline per route/tenant in metrics without cardinality explosion. but we can baseline at {deployment_mode, region, model_family} maybe.

Caleb Johnson: reminder: ingestion guards will drop series if someone sneaks tenant_id/route_id. so alert queries should stick to allowed labels.

Allison Grant: ok: {deployment_mode, region, model} is allowed? or model_family only?

Nadia Rahman: model is allowed but it’s “model_id_allowlist” (curated list). for the first pass we recommended model_family in dashboards, model_id only when high-volume.

Sean Gallagher: for alerting, do model_family. if we do model_id we’ll end up with dozens of alerts firing at once during a rollout.

Neha Kapoor: +1. pager should not explode.

Allison Grant: how do we handle legit workload mix shifts? e.g. a big customer starts sending novel prompts, hit rate drops, wasted prefill rises, no eviction pressure.

Aisha Bello: that’s exactly why we should gate paging on pressure/evictions *or* a miss-reason shift to “version_mismatch/hash_mismatch/policy_disabled”. those are regressions, not workload.

Logan Wright: we’ll have miss-reason counters: `redwood_cache_prefix_misses_total{reason=...}` and similarly for KV.

Sergio Costa: so page condition could be:
- hit rate down AND
- (pressure high OR eviction rate up OR miss_reason in {version_mismatch, policy_disabled} grows)

Sean Gallagher: sounds right. but careful: policy_disabled can be an operator action too. maybe ticket not page.

Allison Grant: ok, maybe:
- page if hit-rate drop + pressure high (suggests real capacity/eviction regression)
- ticket if miss_reason shifts (could be rollout/config)

Neha Kapoor: I like that. page when infra/runtime is shedding cache unexpectedly.

Aisha Bello: concrete numbers (initial, conservative):
- page: global prefix_hr drops by >12% absolute vs 7d same-hour baseline for 30m AND press > 0.85
- ticket: global prefix_hr drops by >10% absolute vs baseline for 60m (no pressure requirement)

Aisha Bello: baseline can be computed in the alerting stack? or are we doing static thresholds only?

Sergio Costa: we don’t have fancy baselining in prom rules today without recording rules gymnastics. simplest is static thresholds per deployment mode.

Sean Gallagher: then static, but high thresholds. and we can iterate.

Allison Grant: what are reasonable static thresholds? we’ve seen hosted chat hover ~0.35–0.55 depending on model and time of day.

Aisha Bello: static global seems risky.

Logan Wright: could approximate baseline using 7d offset if we’re ok with the complexity:
`hr_now` vs `hr_past = (same query offset 7d)`

Sergio Costa: yes, Prometheus supports `offset`. example:

Sergio Costa: ```
hr_now = sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_hits_total[15m]))
         /
         sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_lookups_total[15m]))

hr_7d = (sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_hits_total[15m] offset 7d))
         /
         sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_lookups_total[15m] offset 7d)))

alert_if = (hr_now < hr_7d - 0.12)
```

Sergio Costa: we can add `and on() qps > X` and `and on() press > 0.85`.

Sean Gallagher: that’s better. note: offset breaks if retention gaps / new metrics. but acceptable for initial.

Neha Kapoor: make sure the “telemetry missing” alert doesn’t depend on offset (it’ll be broken during rollout).

Allison Grant: agreed. telemetry missing should be a simpler “expected series present” check.

Caleb Johnson: for telemetry missing, best is to alert on ingestion drop at the aggregator layer (rate of samples) rather than on the cache metric itself. but we can do both.

Allison Grant: ok propose:
1) PAGE: Cache telemetry drop (metrics pipeline) — if cache metrics sample rate drops >80% for 10m in prod
2) PAGE: Cache pressure regression — if press >0.90 for 20m AND eviction_rate up >2x vs 7d offset
3) TICKET: Cache efficiency regression — if prefix_hr_now < prefix_hr_7d - 0.10 for 60m (no page)

Aisha Bello: +1, but for (2) ensure we page only when traffic is non-trivial, otherwise memory pressure during low load is weird.

Sean Gallagher: also (2) should be per deployment_mode maybe. dedicated clusters can run hotter.

Sergio Costa: yep we can label-scope:
`{deployment_mode="hosted"}` etc.

Connor O'Brien: from releases POV: please add a “mute during canary window” note in the runbook. we’ll have real changes during staged rollout.

Allison Grant: good call. will add to runbook + create an “alert suppression” note for `runtime-cache-telemetry` rollout label.

Observability Bot: reminder: dashboard PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability-pack/pull/612 includes alert stubs. reviewers: @Allison Grant @Aisha Bello @Sean Gallagher

Sean Gallagher: we should explicitly decide: cache efficiency alerts should *not* page until we’ve observed for a week. can we ship them as “disabled by default” (ticket-only)?

Neha Kapoor: +1. enable telemetry-missing paging immediately, but keep efficiency regression as ticket-only during burn-in.

Allison Grant: ok. action items:
- Sergio: update PR-612 rules to:
  - enable telemetry-missing paging
  - enable cache-pressure paging (with qps gate)
  - efficiency regression -> ticket only
- Aisha/Sean: review thresholds once we have 48h of prod data
- Allison: add runbook section “expected workload shifts vs regressions”, and note canary suppression

Sergio Costa: ack, will push updates today.

Aisha Bello: I’ll draft the “qps gate” and suggested numbers. thinking `qps > 50` global, `> 10` per region.

Sean Gallagher: works. also add note: if hit-rate dip coincides w/ model rollout, file ticket to Runtime first (version_mismatch likely).

Logan Wright: + and if miss_reason=label_drop or unknown spikes, ping Telemetry (could be cardinality guard config).

Allison Grant: cool. :thumbsup: will follow up in PR comments.
text:
Allison Grant: hey all — need to lock initial alert thresholds for the new cache dashboards before we turn them on in prod. goal is “regression signal w/o paging on workload mix changes”.

Allison Grant: current candidate alerts:
- prefix cache hit rate drop
- KV cache reuse effectiveness drop
- eviction pressure sustained high
- wasted prefill ratio spike

Allison Grant: question: what should page vs ticket, and what dimensions do we alert on (global vs region vs model)?

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should be super conservative on paging for anything cache-efficiency related. it’s a guardrail SLO, not a customer SLA.

Aisha Bello: agree. hit-rate swings a *lot* with prompt length / traffic mix. if we page on “hit rate down” alone we’re going to hate ourselves.

Sergio Costa: fwiw I can wire 2 severities:
- SEV2 page (oncall)
- SEV4 ticket (triage in hours)

Sergio Costa: but need crisp predicates to avoid false positives.

Neha Kapoor: we should only page when there’s strong evidence it’s a platform/runtime regression (not traffic mix). multi-signal gating.

Allison Grant: ok. what gating do we want?

Aisha Bello: recommend:
1) require enough volume (otherwise noise): e.g. `reqs > 500/min` for the evaluated slice
2) hit-rate drop must be *absolute* + *sustained* (avoid short spikes)
3) confirm with eviction/pressure or miss-reason shift

Sean Gallagher: + check “telemetry missing” separately. if metrics go to zero due to ingestion/cardinality drop, we should detect that and page (because dashboards go blind).

Logan Wright: +1 telemetry-missing is actionable and usually a pipeline/config issue. cache-efficiency dips are often “real” but not urgent.

Allison Grant: makes sense. for efficiency alerting: global only? or per region?

Sean Gallagher: I’d do:
- ticket on global + per region
- page only on global (or 2+ regions) to reduce flappy pages

Neha Kapoor: yup. paging on a single region hit-rate dip will be constant during capacity shifts.

Sergio Costa: ok. let me propose something in promql terms. rough sketch:

Sergio Costa: ```
# 15m rolling hit-rate for prefix cache (weighted)
hr = sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_hits_total[15m]))
     /
     sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_lookups_total[15m]))

# 15m wasted prefill ratio
wpr = sum(rate(redwood_cache_wasted_prefill_tokens_total[15m]))
      /
      sum(rate(redwood_prefill_tokens_total[15m]))

# pressure (gauge)
press = avg_over_time(redwood_cache_kv_memory_pressure[10m])

# req volume
qps = sum(rate(redwood_requests_total[5m]))
```

Sergio Costa: thresholds TBD.

Aisha Bello: for thresholds, we should use “drop from baseline” not absolute numbers. some models are inherently lower hit rate.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also note we *cannot* baseline per route/tenant in metrics without cardinality explosion. but we can baseline at {deployment_mode, region, model_family} maybe.

Caleb Johnson: reminder: ingestion guards will drop series if someone sneaks tenant_id/route_id. so alert queries should stick to allowed labels.

Allison Grant: ok: {deployment_mode, region, model} is allowed? or model_family only?

Nadia Rahman: model is allowed but it’s “model_id_allowlist” (curated list). for the first pass we recommended model_family in dashboards, model_id only when high-volume.

Sean Gallagher: for alerting, do model_family. if we do model_id we’ll end up with dozens of alerts firing at once during a rollout.

Neha Kapoor: +1. pager should not explode.

Allison Grant: how do we handle legit workload mix shifts? e.g. a big customer starts sending novel prompts, hit rate drops, wasted prefill rises, no eviction pressure.

Aisha Bello: that’s exactly why we should gate paging on pressure/evictions *or* a miss-reason shift to “version_mismatch/hash_mismatch/policy_disabled”. those are regressions, not workload.

Logan Wright: we’ll have miss-reason counters: `redwood_cache_prefix_misses_total{reason=...}` and similarly for KV.

Sergio Costa: so page condition could be:
- hit rate down AND
- (pressure high OR eviction rate up OR miss_reason in {version_mismatch, policy_disabled} grows)

Sean Gallagher: sounds right. but careful: policy_disabled can be an operator action too. maybe ticket not page.

Allison Grant: ok, maybe:
- page if hit-rate drop + pressure high (suggests real capacity/eviction regression)
- ticket if miss_reason shifts (could be rollout/config)

Neha Kapoor: I like that. page when infra/runtime is shedding cache unexpectedly.

Aisha Bello: concrete numbers (initial, conservative):
- page: global prefix_hr drops by >12% absolute vs 7d same-hour baseline for 30m AND press > 0.85
- ticket: global prefix_hr drops by >10% absolute vs baseline for 60m (no pressure requirement)

Aisha Bello: baseline can be computed in the alerting stack? or are we doing static thresholds only?

Sergio Costa: we don’t have fancy baselining in prom rules today without recording rules gymnastics. simplest is static thresholds per deployment mode.

Sean Gallagher: then static, but high thresholds. and we can iterate.

Allison Grant: what are reasonable static thresholds? we’ve seen hosted chat hover ~0.35–0.55 depending on model and time of day.

Aisha Bello: static global seems risky.

Logan Wright: could approximate baseline using 7d offset if we’re ok with the complexity:
`hr_now` vs `hr_past = (same query offset 7d)`

Sergio Costa: yes, Prometheus supports `offset`. example:

Sergio Costa: ```
hr_now = sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_hits_total[15m]))
         /
         sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_lookups_total[15m]))

hr_7d = (sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_hits_total[15m] offset 7d))
         /
         sum(rate(redwood_cache_prefix_lookups_total[15m] offset 7d)))

alert_if = (hr_now < hr_7d - 0.12)
```

Sergio Costa: we can add `and on() qps > X` and `and on() press > 0.85`.

Sean Gallagher: that’s better. note: offset breaks if retention gaps / new metrics. but acceptable for initial.

Neha Kapoor: make sure the “telemetry missing” alert doesn’t depend on offset (it’ll be broken during rollout).

Allison Grant: agreed. telemetry missing should be a simpler “expected series present” check.

Caleb Johnson: for telemetry missing, best is to alert on ingestion drop at the aggregator layer (rate of samples) rather than on the cache metric itself. but we can do both.

Allison Grant: ok propose:
1) PAGE: Cache telemetry drop (metrics pipeline) — if cache metrics sample rate drops >80% for 10m in prod
2) PAGE: Cache pressure regression — if press >0.90 for 20m AND eviction_rate up >2x vs 7d offset
3) TICKET: Cache efficiency regression — if prefix_hr_now < prefix_hr_7d - 0.10 for 60m (no page)

Aisha Bello: +1, but for (2) ensure we page only when traffic is non-trivial, otherwise memory pressure during low load is weird.

Sean Gallagher: also (2) should be per deployment_mode maybe. dedicated clusters can run hotter.

Sergio Costa: yep we can label-scope:
`{deployment_mode="hosted"}` etc.

Connor O'Brien: from releases POV: please add a “mute during canary window” note in the runbook. we’ll have real changes during staged rollout.

Allison Grant: good call. will add to runbook + create an “alert suppression” note for `runtime-cache-telemetry` rollout label.

Observability Bot: reminder: dashboard PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability-pack/pull/612 includes alert stubs. reviewers: @Allison Grant @Aisha Bello @Sean Gallagher

Sean Gallagher: we should explicitly decide: cache efficiency alerts should *not* page until we’ve observed for a week. can we ship them as “disabled by default” (ticket-only)?

Neha Kapoor: +1. enable telemetry-missing paging immediately, but keep efficiency regression as ticket-only during burn-in.

Allison Grant: ok. action items:
- Sergio: update PR-612 rules to:
  - enable telemetry-missing paging
  - enable cache-pressure paging (with qps gate)
  - efficiency regression -> ticket only
- Aisha/Sean: review thresholds once we have 48h of prod data
- Allison: add runbook section “expected workload shifts vs regressions”, and note canary suppression

Sergio Costa: ack, will push updates today.

Aisha Bello: I’ll draft the “qps gate” and suggested numbers. thinking `qps > 50` global, `> 10` per region.

Sean Gallagher: works. also add note: if hit-rate dip coincides w/ model rollout, file ticket to Runtime first (version_mismatch likely).

Logan Wright: + and if miss_reason=label_drop or unknown spikes, ping Telemetry (could be cardinality guard config).

Allison Grant: cool. :thumbsup: will follow up in PR comments.

title: #eng-sre: 1739995402
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Need to lock down the SLO regression gate semantics for burst before we call GA-ready. Specifically: what exactly trips the gate + what counts as a rollback trigger vs “auto-throttle burst”.

Sean Gallagher: Context: PR-23988 adds “disable/reduce burst when non-burst latency regresses”. We need agreed thresholds and windows so we don’t flap.

Peter Holtz: +1. Also routing needs deterministic behavior. If the gate trips mid-flight, we should be explicit: does it return BURST_DENIED (w/ reason) or do we hard-disable the whole region/hw class flag.

Jordan Lee: Current implementation (policy engine rules) supports 3 actions:
1) `burst_mode=normal`
2) `burst_mode=throttled` (reduce max burst ratio + tighten per-tenant token bucket refill)
3) `burst_mode=disabled` (deny all burst admissions)

Jordan Lee: It’s keyed by (region, hw_class) and can be overridden by global circuit breaker.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. But we need paging / rollback criteria. If it hits `disabled`, do we automatically revert the feature flag allowlist or just stay disabled until oncall re-enables?

Keiko Yamamoto: From infra side: auto-disable burst is fine, but don’t auto-change pool sizes / allocations. We should treat disable as “stop borrowing from burst pool”, not “move workloads”.

Allison Grant: For metrics: the cleanest “neighbor impact” signal we have is non-burst tenant p95/p99 latency deltas vs baseline. But baseline definition is tricky (diurnal + model mix).

Mina Haddad: Routing can supply a simpler signal too: “non-burst request queueing time” and “tail amp” at gateway. But I’d prefer SRE gate uses 1-2 metrics max.

Sean Gallagher: Proposed: gate is based on Dedicated non-burst SLO burn. If error budget burn rate spikes, we throttle/disable burst. That ties to what we actually care about.

Allison Grant: Agree, but we need to pick the SLO. Is it per-tenant SLO or aggregate “non-burst cohort” SLO per region/hw?

Sean Gallagher: Aggregate per (region, hw_class) for “non-burst cohort”, otherwise cardinality explodes and you’ll have noisy single-tenant outliers.

Allison Grant: OK. We can compute cohort as “Dedicated tenants where burst_enabled=false OR burst_in_use=false” (careful: tenants with burst enabled but currently not bursting still count as neighbors).

Jordan Lee: Implementation note: policy engine only gets a small set of signals pushed in by telemetry aggregator. If we need cohort logic, it should happen in telemetry layer and be shipped as a single scalar.

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane can’t do per-tenant joins at decision time.

Peter Holtz: Also: any gate signal must be stable under telemetry lag. We saw in beta the lag spikes caused unfair denials. I don’t want lag to trigger “disable burst” unless it’s truly impacting neighbors.

Keiko Yamamoto: Can we explicitly add a telemetry-lag guard: if lag > N seconds, we don’t transition from normal->disabled automatically (or we require longer window)?

Jordan Lee: Yes. I can add a condition: `if telemetry_lag_seconds > 30 then max_action = throttled` (never disabled). Or require 2 consecutive windows.

Rafael Mendes: Let’s define rollback trigger vs auto gate:
- Auto gate = throttled/disabled (no human) when neighbor SLO is threatened.
- Rollback trigger = we revert the rollout flag / disable feature in that region/hw and page oncall.

Rafael Mendes: Otherwise folks will interpret “disabled” as “we rolled back”.

Sean Gallagher: Exactly. So what constitutes “rollback trigger”?

Sean Gallagher: I suggest rollback triggers are:
1) non-burst cohort p95 latency regression > 15% sustained for 10m AND error-budget burn > 10x
2) any sustained increase in non-burst 5xx rate above 0.5% for 5m (routing meltdown)
3) admission controller timeouts > 1% for 5m (since that can cascade into routing latency)

Sean Gallagher: Auto gate triggers (no page) can be milder:
- burn > 4x for 5m => throttled
- burn > 8x for 5m => disabled

Allison Grant: For burn we should use the same multi-window burn rate approach we use elsewhere (e.g., 5m and 1h). Otherwise short spikes will flap.

Allison Grant: e.g. “burn_rate_5m > 8 AND burn_rate_1h > 2” to disable.

Peter Holtz: +1. We already have that pattern in routing circuit breakers.

Mina Haddad: Question: when burst is disabled, what does client see? 429? Or is it treated as “no extra capacity” and they just run at baseline throughput (queue on their dedicated pool)?

Peter Holtz: They only hit burst when they exceed baseline. If disabled, admission denies, routing should fall back to baseline routing and apply existing per-tenant backpressure. For Dedicated, that’s typically 429 with `Retry-After` once they hit their own queue limit.

Jordan Lee: Denial reason codes already include `SLO_GATED_NONBURST_REGRESSION` (from PR-23795). We can ensure it maps to the customer-visible error body.

Ethan Park: Control plane can also surface “burst temporarily unavailable due to fleet safety gate” in Console.

Rafael Mendes: Need to be crisp in runbooks: “Gate is active” vs “Feature rolled back”.

Sean Gallagher: Yes, runbook will call it “SLO gate tripped” and include decision tree.

Keiko Yamamoto: Threshold numbers: 15% p95 regression seems aggressive. Some workloads jitter 10-12% daily. Can we do an absolute + relative? Like p95 delta > 25ms AND > 15%.

Allison Grant: I like adding absolute floor. For small p95 (e.g., 60ms), 15% is 9ms which is noise.

Sean Gallagher: OK. For rollback trigger, we can do:
- `p95_delta_ms > 25` AND `p95_delta_pct > 15%` for 10m.

Sean Gallagher: For auto throttle/disable, we can rely on burn (since it already normalizes).

Jordan Lee: Where do we compute `p95_delta`? Burn rate is already computed on SLO, but p95 delta is separate.

Allison Grant: Telemetry pipeline can produce “p95_nonburst_latency_ms” and “p95_nonburst_latency_baseline_ms” (baseline from trailing 24h same hour-of-day), then emit delta.

Peter Holtz: Baseline from same hour-of-day might be too slow to react to step changes (like deploy). But that’s fine for rollback trigger, since we’re also looking at error rate / burn.

Rafael Mendes: Can we keep rollback trigger solely on burn + error rate? Baselines are always contentious.

Sean Gallagher: I’d rather have a human-comprehensible latency condition in the rollback trigger. Burn rate alone is hard to explain to CS when they ask “why did you roll back.”

Mina Haddad: For explainability, if gate trips, we should annotate in Grafana: “burst disabled by SLO gate” w/ the metric values.

Allison Grant: We can add alert annotations + a counter metric `burst_slo_gate_transitions_total{from,to,region,hw}`.

Jordan Lee: Already in PR-23822 we have some of that, but not transitions. I can add transitions with low-cardinality labels (from/to + region + hw only).

SLOBot: Reminder: SLO burn alerts should include both windows (5m, 1h) and link to runbook https://runbooks.redwood.dev/burst/slo-gate (draft)

Peter Holtz: Another edge: “disable” should have a cooldown to prevent flapping when SLO recovers. Otherwise it’ll re-enable and immediately re-trip.

Jordan Lee: Current state machine: once in disabled, it stays disabled until it sees burn_rate_5m < 1 AND burn_rate_1h < 1 for 30m. (configurable)

Rafael Mendes: That’s good, but we should make “re-enable” manual for GA? At least during early rollout.

Sean Gallagher: For GA I’d prefer automatic recovery but with a page on rollback triggers only. For beta/early GA we can set recovery window longer (e.g., 60m) + keep a feature flag kill switch.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. Auto recovery helps us avoid waking people up for transient events.

Allison Grant: Another guard: don’t allow transitions if telemetry lag > 30s (as mentioned). Telemetry lag itself should alert separately.

Sean Gallagher: Summarizing proposal:
- Auto throttle:
  - Condition: `burn_5m > 4` AND `burn_1h > 1.5` for non-burst cohort SLO
  - Action: `burst_mode=throttled` (reduce max burst ratio; keep fairness)
- Auto disable:
  - Condition: `burn_5m > 8` AND `burn_1h > 2`
  - Action: `burst_mode=disabled`
  - Guard: if `telemetry_lag_seconds > 30`, max action is throttled
  - Cooldown / recovery: require both burns < 1 for 60m to recover
- Rollback trigger (pages and requires human ack + likely region/hw rollout rollback):
  - (A) `p95_delta_ms > 25` AND `p95_delta_pct > 15%` for 10m AND `burn_5m > 10`
  - OR (B) non-burst 5xx > 0.5% for 5m
  - OR (C) admission controller timeouts > 1% for 5m

Sean Gallagher: Any objections?

Peter Holtz: Looks solid. For (C), define timeout metric precisely: `burst_admission_rpc_errors_total{type=timeout}` / requests. And scope to region/hw.

Jordan Lee: I’ll update policy rules + add telemetry-lag guard. Need Allison to confirm metrics names.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync w/ Logan on the exact Prometheus series. Also will add “transition counter” and Grafana annotation.

Rafael Mendes: Runbook action for rollback trigger should be explicit:
1) flip region/hw allowlist off (feature flag)
2) keep global breaker available if the flag propagation is slow
3) notify CS + post in #incidents if customer-facing

Rafael Mendes: I’ll add to runbook draft and link from the alert.

Mina Haddad: Routing side: when `burst_mode=throttled`, we should bias routing to baseline first to avoid oscillations. I’ll make sure Ravi/Elena see this.

Ethan Park: Control plane: we’ll surface current burst mode + last transition reason in Console. That’ll help support.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Let’s treat these numbers as “GA defaults” but keep config-tunable per region/hw (not per tenant). Please don’t create customer-specific thresholds.

Keiko Yamamoto: :thumbsup: +1. Region/hw tunable is fine.

Jordan Lee: I’ll post a PR comment in PR-23988 with this exact spec so reviewers align.

Allison Grant: I’ll also add a note about label cardinality (no tenant_id labels) since people keep asking.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks all. Next step: once merged, we test in game day with synthetic burst + induced latency and confirm the gate trips to throttled first, then disabled, and doesn’t flap.

Sean Gallagher: :white_check_mark: done. Closing the loop once we have dashboards updated.

Peter Holtz: Another edge: “disable” should have a cooldown to prevent flapping when SLO recovers. Otherwise it’ll re-enable and immediately re-trip.

Jordan Lee: Current state machine: once in disabled, it stays disabled until it sees burn_rate_5m < 1 AND burn_rate_1h < 1 for 30m. (configurable)

Rafael Mendes: That’s good, but we should make “re-enable” manual for GA? At least during early rollout.

Sean Gallagher: For GA I’d prefer automatic recovery but with a page on rollback triggers only. For beta/early GA we can set recovery window longer (e.g., 60m) + keep a feature flag kill switch.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. Auto recovery helps us avoid waking people up for transient events.

Allison Grant: Another guard: don’t allow transitions if telemetry lag > 30s (as mentioned). Telemetry lag itself should alert separately.

Sean Gallagher: Summarizing proposal:
- Auto throttle:
  - Condition: `burn_5m > 4` AND `burn_1h > 1.5` for non-burst cohort SLO
  - Action: `burst_mode=throttled` (reduce max burst ratio; keep fairness)
- Auto disable:
  - Condition: `burn_5m > 8` AND `burn_1h > 2`
  - Action: `burst_mode=disabled`
  - Guard: if `telemetry_lag_seconds > 30`, max action is throttled
  - Cooldown / recovery: require both burns < 1 for 60m to recover
- Rollback trigger (pages and requires human ack + likely region/hw rollout rollback):
  - (A) `p95_delta_ms > 25` AND `p95_delta_pct > 15%` for 10m AND `burn_5m > 10`
  - OR (B) non-burst 5xx > 0.5% for 5m
  - OR (C) admission controller timeouts > 1% for 5m

Sean Gallagher: Any objections?

Peter Holtz: Looks solid. For (C), define timeout metric precisely: `burst_admission_rpc_errors_total{type=timeout}` / requests. And scope to region/hw.

Jordan Lee: I’ll update policy rules + add telemetry-lag guard. Need Allison to confirm metrics names.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync w/ Logan on the exact Prometheus series. Also will add “transition counter” and Grafana annotation.

Rafael Mendes: Runbook action for rollback trigger should be explicit:
1) flip region/hw allowlist off (feature flag)
2) keep global breaker available if the flag propagation is slow
3) notify CS + post in #incidents if customer-facing

Rafael Mendes: I’ll add to runbook draft and link from the alert.

Mina Haddad: Routing side: when `burst_mode=throttled`, we should bias routing to baseline first to avoid oscillations. I’ll make sure Ravi/Elena see this.

Ethan Park: Control plane: we’ll surface current burst mode + last transition reason in Console. That’ll help support.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Let’s treat these numbers as “GA defaults” but keep config-tunable per region/hw (not per tenant). Please don’t create customer-specific thresholds.

Keiko Yamamoto: :thumbsup: +1. Region/hw tunable is fine.

Jordan Lee: I’ll post a PR comment in PR-23988 with this exact spec so reviewers align.

Allison Grant: I’ll also add a note about label cardinality (no tenant_id labels) since people keep asking.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks all. Next step: once merged, we test in game day with synthetic burst + induced latency and confirm the gate trips to throttled first, then disabled, and doesn’t flap.

Sean Gallagher: :white_check_mark: done. Closing the loop once we have dashboards updated.

title: #eng-sre: 1739718502
preview:
Aisha Bello: for the quant profile canary next week, we need to lock alert thresholds. Specifically `fallback_rate` + any quality guardrail signals we’re emitting. What do we want to page on vs just warn?

Connor O'Brien: +1. canary is 1% traffic on 2 routes (Acme hosted + 1 dedicated). We need a “hit kill-switch” moment that’s unambiguous.

Logan Wright: can we align on metric names so we don’t build alerts on the wrong series? I’m seeing:
- `quant_profile.resolution_outcome` = applied|fallback|disabled
- `quant_profile.fallback_reason` = incompatible|load_error|policy_error|unknown
and then dashboard computes fallback_rate as fallback / (applied+fallback). Is that what we’re using?

Caleb Johnson: tracing side is tagging `quant_profile_id` + `resolution=applied|fallback|disabled`. For alerting we should use the aggregated counters not traces, but we can link traces in the runbook.

Ravi Menon: reminder: fallback is “safe” by design, but if we see fallback > ~0.1% it usually means compat matrix mismatch or control plane cache staleness. In canary I’d prefer tight thresholds.

Jordan Lee: careful though: there’s expected fallback if customers try to enable a profile on a route that spans mixed GPU SKUs. But for the canary routes we control those pools (dedicated is pinned, hosted canary route is region-pinned).

Aisha Bello: yep, for canary routes we’ll filter alert by `route_id in {…}` and `deployment_tier`. So it’s not global noise.

Nadia Rahman: what about separating “fallback due to incompatibility” vs “fallback due to errors”? Error fallback should be *much* tighter since it signals a runtime or registry issue.

Connor O'Brien: +1. if load_error spikes, that’s “rollback now” territory.

Selene Huang: quality guardrail signals: what do we have online that’s trustworthy enough to page? Offline evals gate publishing, but online canary should still watch for drift.

Sana Farid: we have two online signals wired for canary:
1) `quality_guardrail.block_event_rate` (from the lightweight prompt sentinel checks) — basically “this output tripped a guardrail rule.”
2) `quality_guardrail.regression_score` (aggregated z-score vs baseline for the canary route, updated every ~10 min). It’s noisy at low N.
If you page on (2) at 1% traffic you’ll get false positives.

Aisha Bello: ok so maybe page on guardrail block rate (1) but keep regression_score as warn-only?

Sana Farid: that’s my recommendation. For paging: require both (a) minimum sample size and (b) sustained increase.

Caleb Johnson: we can do a “burn-in” delay too (don’t page until 20 min after enablement) unless error rates go crazy.

Connor O'Brien: we already have the release checklist step “watch dashboards for 30 min” so burn-in makes sense.

Logan Wright: proposal for thresholds (route-scoped):
- Fallback rate WARN: >0.5% for 10m
- Fallback rate PAGE: >2.0% for 5m
- Fallback rate PAGE immediately if `fallback_reason=load_error|policy_error` >0.2% for 5m
Thoughts?

Ravi Menon: 2% for 5m seems high for canary. If we’re falling back 2% we’re basically not testing the profile anymore + might be hiding a bigger issue.

Jordan Lee: agree. For canary I’d set PAGE at 1% for 5m, with a stricter threshold for load_error.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also add a “applied_rate” alert? If applied_rate drops unexpectedly (e.g. kill-switch accidentally on) we should know.

Aisha Bello: applied_rate drop is probably a WARN (since it’s not an outage) but it will confuse the canary analysis.

Connor O'Brien: can we align on a single “stop the line” trigger list for oncall?
1) elevated 5xx
2) load_error fallback
3) quality guardrail blocks
4) p95 latency regression
Fallback due to incompatibility alone might be config issue, but still should stop if it’s sustained.

Selene Huang: quality guardrail blocks: what’s the baseline rate? we need a threshold relative to baseline not absolute.

Sana Farid: baseline (no quant) varies per route but generally <0.05% block events. For canary we can page if:
- block_event_rate >0.2% for 15m AND sample_size >= 2k outputs
That’s ~4x baseline and should be meaningful.

Caleb Johnson: 2k outputs at 1% traffic might take a while depending on route QPS. For Acme we’ll hit it; for others maybe not.

Aisha Bello: we can add a second condition for fast-moving regressions:
- block_event_rate >0.5% for 5m AND sample_size >= 200
This catches “something is really wrong” without waiting.

Sana Farid: that’s fine as long as sample_size gating is enforced.

Logan Wright: ok, summarizing an alert plan:
A) `quant_profile.fallback_rate` (route-scoped)
- WARN: >0.5% for 10m
- PAGE: >1.0% for 5m
B) `quant_profile.fallback_rate` by reason
- PAGE: `fallback_reason in {load_error, policy_error}` >0.1% for 5m
C) `quality_guardrail.block_event_rate` (route-scoped)
- WARN: >0.2% for 15m AND outputs >= 2k
- PAGE: >0.5% for 5m AND outputs >= 200
D) `quality_guardrail.regression_score`
- WARN only: score > 3.0 for 30m AND outputs >= 5k (no paging)

Connor O'Brien: looks good. add runbook text: what to do for each page (disable route setting first, then kill-switch if needed).

Aisha Bello: + link to the rollback procedure. I’ll wire alert annotations with the exact commands/Console path.

Ravi Menon: can we include a panel/alert for “fallback loop” detection? We had that bug once.

Jordan Lee: after the fix, fallback loop manifests as high policy eval count + elevated `policy_error`. Your (B) should catch it.

Caleb Johnson: I’ll add a trace search link in the alert: `quant_profile.resolution=fallback AND fallback_reason=policy_error` with example request_id.

Nadia Rahman: also can we track cost deltas during canary? not alerting, but at least a panel so PM doesn’t ask mid-rollout.

Aisha Bello: yep, not paging on cost, but I’ll add a dashboard row for `cost_per_1k_tokens` delta baseline vs quant route.

Connor O'Brien: awesome. action items:
- Aisha: create alerts in Grafana + add sample_size gating, route filters, and annotations
- Logan/Caleb: confirm metric names + labels match what dashboards expect
- Sana/Selene: sanity check quality thresholds vs baseline for Acme route
We’ll review in release sync tomorrow.

Aisha Bello: ack. I’ll post screenshots once alerts are in staging.

title: #eng-sre: 1739481144
preview:
Rafael Mendes: seeing migration lock timeouts again in staging during the private upgrade e2e. anyone else looking at this?

Rafael Mendes: symptom: `redwood-migrate` helm hook job fails, upgrade aborts.

Sean Gallagher: yep got paged by the staging smoke checks. do we have logs handy?

Sahana Iyer: paste from the e2e job (upgrade 1.19.0-rc3 -> 1.19.0-rc4). failing on control-plane DB migration:
``` 
Job: redwood-private-control-plane-migrate
...
ERROR: canceling statement due to lock timeout
DETAIL:  Process 21492 waits for ShareLock on transaction 778192; blocked by process 21301.
CONTEXT:  while updating tuple (12,3) in relation "deployments"
STATEMENT:  ALTER TABLE deployments ADD COLUMN rollout_policy jsonb;
```

Sahana Iyer: it retries once (k8s job backoff), then fails again. total run ~4m.

Michael Nguyen: that `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` should be quick, but it does take an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock briefly. lock_timeout firing means we’re waiting for other transaction to finish.

Michael Nguyen: question: do we still have staging canary traffic pointed at the same DB during the e2e window?

Ethan Park: staging has the canary control plane always on (for dev testing). it shares the same `private-config` postgres instance i think.

Sean Gallagher: so migration is competing with long-running txns from canary. that’s not great.

Rafael Mendes: what are our current timeout defaults for migrations? is lock_timeout set super low?

Michael Nguyen: in the migration container we set:
- `lock_timeout = '5s'`
- `statement_timeout = '15m'`

Michael Nguyen: intent was “fail fast + tell operator what to do” vs hanging forever in an upgrade.

Allison Grant: 5s is… pretty aggressive. in busy envs, it’ll trip constantly even if nothing is actually wrong.

Sean Gallagher: agree, but also don’t want to block upgrades for 30m. we need a safer default + clear mitigation.

Connor O'Brien: from release eng: we need staging to be representative. if staging always has background traffic, lock_timeout=5s will make the upgrade train look flaky.

Rafael Mendes: for customer envs, do we expect “no control plane writes” during upgrade? we should, but real world will have lingering sessions.

Michael Nguyen: even if we ask customers to schedule a window, you still get:
- idle-in-transaction sessions from dashboards/scripts
- long running SELECTs
- app pods with pooled connections holding open txns longer than expected

Michael Nguyen: safe mitigation is: quiesce writers, then re-run migration.

Sean Gallagher: can we add a precheck to detect “long running transactions” / “idle in transaction” and hard-block before we start apply?

Michael Nguyen: yes. we can query `pg_stat_activity` + `pg_locks` as a preflight.

Michael Nguyen: something like:
- warn if any `state = 'idle in transaction'` older than 60s
- warn if any xact age > 5m
- block if there are blockers holding locks on our core tables (deployments, models, routes) during precheck

Sahana Iyer: would be nice if the precheck output includes the actual PID + query so operator can kill it.

Michael Nguyen: yep. we can show:
- pid
- usename
- application_name
- xact_start age
- query (truncated)

Rafael Mendes: for *staging right now*, immediate mitigation: can we scale down the canary control plane before the e2e upgrade?

Ethan Park: I can do that. we can have the e2e job do:
`kubectl -n private-system scale deploy/redwood-private-control-plane --replicas=0`
then run upgrade, then scale back.

Sean Gallagher: that’s fine for CI, but we still need a product default.

Allison Grant: also worth adding a metric/log line: “waited X seconds for lock on <table>” so we can see patterns. right now we only see the failure.

grafana-bot: (auto) Related dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.example/d/private-upgrades-staging/migrations (panel: "Migration job failures")

Connor O'Brien: what’s the proposal for defaults?

Michael Nguyen: suggestion:
- bump `lock_timeout` from 5s -> 2m
- add retry loop around *each* migration statement with exponential backoff up to ~5m total
- keep `statement_timeout` at 15m (or 30m) so we don’t hang forever

Michael Nguyen: rationale: most “legit” blockers clear in < 1-2m once we quiesce, and on busy systems you want time to drain.

Sean Gallagher: 2m seems reasonable. also: if after 5m we still can’t lock, we should fail with “manual intervention required” not keep trying.

Rafael Mendes: +1. and error message should be explicit: “lock timeout means another session holds a lock; check pg_stat_activity; recommended action: put CP into maintenance / scale down writers; re-run upgrade apply”.

Allison Grant: can we add a runbook snippet (copy/paste) for operators?

Michael Nguyen: draft commands:
```sql
-- find blockers / long txns
SELECT pid, usename, application_name, state,
       now() - xact_start AS xact_age,
       left(query, 120) AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE datname = current_database()
  AND xact_start IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY xact_start ASC;

-- lock waits
SELECT a.pid, a.usename, a.application_name, a.state,
       now() - a.xact_start AS xact_age,
       l.locktype, l.mode, l.relation::regclass,
       pg_blocking_pids(a.pid) AS blocking_pids
FROM pg_locks l
JOIN pg_stat_activity a ON a.pid = l.pid
WHERE a.wait_event_type = 'Lock';
```

Michael Nguyen: then (operator choice) `SELECT pg_terminate_backend(<pid>);` for the offending session.

Sean Gallagher: we should be careful recommending terminate. but for Private operator-run it’s fine with a warning + “ensure you know what you’re killing”.

Connor O'Brien: action items:
1) bump lock_timeout defaults (Michael)
2) add precheck for long txns (Michael + installer precheck framework owner)
3) in CI e2e, scale down canary during upgrade window (Ethan + Sahana)
4) add runbook + troubleshooting section (Kira/Elliot but I can file)

Rafael Mendes: + add a mitigation step for on-call: if staging migration fails with lock timeout, first verify canary scaled down, then rerun job.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll update the e2e pipeline today. also can add a failure artifact dumping `pg_stat_activity` if the job fails (so we stop guessing).

Allison Grant: if you dump that, I can wire it into the support bundle / CI artifacts + link from the grafana panel.

Michael Nguyen: I’ll put up a PR for lock_timeout=2m + improved error text. precheck might be a follow-up PR (depends on DB creds availability in preflight).

Sean Gallagher: pls also add “lock_timeout value” to the migration job logs at startup. makes debugging easier.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. cc me on the PR, I want to make sure the operator messaging is clear + matches the on-call playbook.

Ethan Park: I’ll coordinate the staging canary scale-down schedule. we should probably not share DB between canary and upgrade tests long term.

Connor O'Brien: yep, longer term we should isolate staging upgrade DB, but for now let’s get the defaults + precheck in so customers don’t hit this.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup:

```sql
       pg_blocking_pids(a.pid) AS blocking_pids
FROM pg_locks l
JOIN pg_stat_activity a ON a.pid = l.pid
WHERE a.wait_event_type = 'Lock';
```

Michael Nguyen: then (operator choice) `SELECT pg_terminate_backend(<pid>);` for the offending session.

Sean Gallagher: we should be careful recommending terminate. but for Private operator-run it’s fine with a warning + “ensure you know what you’re killing”.

Connor O'Brien: action items:
1) bump lock_timeout defaults (Michael)
2) add precheck for long txns (Michael + installer precheck framework owner)
3) in CI e2e, scale down canary during upgrade window (Ethan + Sahana)
4) add runbook + troubleshooting section (Kira/Elliot but I can file)

Rafael Mendes: + add a mitigation step for on-call: if staging migration fails with lock timeout, first verify canary scaled down, then rerun job.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll update the e2e pipeline today. also can add a failure artifact dumping `pg_stat_activity` if the job fails (so we stop guessing).

Allison Grant: if you dump that, I can wire it into the support bundle / CI artifacts + link from the grafana panel.

Michael Nguyen: I’ll put up a PR for lock_timeout=2m + improved error text. precheck might be a follow-up PR (depends on DB creds availability in preflight).

Sean Gallagher: pls also add “lock_timeout value” to the migration job logs at startup. makes debugging easier.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. cc me on the PR, I want to make sure the operator messaging is clear + matches the on-call playbook.

Ethan Park: I’ll coordinate the staging canary scale-down schedule. we should probably not share DB between canary and upgrade tests long term.

Connor O'Brien: yep, longer term we should isolate staging upgrade DB, but for now let’s get the defaults + precheck in so customers don’t hit this.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup:

title: #eng-sre: 1739475822
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Want to run a game-day next week to simulate an audit sink outage (ingestion path + exporter) and confirm:
- service behavior (no admin workflows blocked)
- buffering/retry works as expected
- alerts fire w/ correct routing + runbook links
- we can explain data loss/dup semantics clearly

Proposed window: Tue 10:00–11:00 PT. Anyone hard conflict?

Rafael Mendes: +1. If we do Tue, I can facilitate.
We should explicitly test both "sink unreachable" and "sink slow/backlog" scenarios.

Aisha Bello: Tue 10–11 PT works. I want to validate the new alerts from observability-pack (drop rate + delivery latency) actually page the right on-call.

Kevin Osei: Works. For the outage simulation: are we doing it in staging only, or can we do a constrained prod shard? (I’d strongly prefer staging first.)

Sean Gallagher: Staging first. If we learn nothing + need confidence, we can schedule a separate prod canary later.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboards, I can set up a “expected vs observed event_types” view during the game-day. Need to know which tenant/project we’re hitting so I can filter.

Nadia Rahman: Reminder: the audit ingestion pipeline uses the async buffer in the gateway + control plane. If sink is down, we expect:
- events queued up to N (configurable)
- then spill to disk (private) / drop w/ metric (hosted) depending on mode
We should confirm which mode staging matches.

Allison Grant: Please include a check for cardinality blow-ups under retry. We saw a near-miss where error strings included full URLs and got super high-card.

Neha Kapoor: Can someone define “success criteria” in the thread so we don’t end with “seems fine”?

Sean Gallagher: Good call. Draft success criteria:
1) Sensitive admin actions still succeed while sink is down (no user-visible 5xx attributable to audit)
2) Audit pipeline alerts fire within 5 min of outage (latency + drop/backlog)
3) On-call runbook is sufficient to triage + mitigate (buffering, exporter retries, backfill guidance)
4) After recovery, backlog drains and delivery latency returns < 2 min p95 within 15 min
5) We can identify duplicates via event_id and recommend de-dupe strategy

Anything missing?

Caleb Johnson: Add traceability: confirm request_id/trace_id/admin_action_id propagate into emitted audit events even under retry/backfill. That’s one of the big investigation needs.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also “no secrets in logs” check: make sure we don’t accidentally log auth headers or key material during exporter failures.

Kevin Osei: For simulating outage, we can:
- blackhole the sink endpoint via network policy (preferred)
- or misconfigure exporter target to 503
- or stop the consumer in staging
I can own the actual “break it / fix it” steps if someone gives me admin access for the staging namespace.

Connor O'Brien: FYI we have a release train Tue 11:30 PT. As long as we end by 11 it’s fine. Please avoid changing anything that might land in release branch.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s keep it “ops knobs only” (network policy / scaling) and no code changes.

Aisha Bello: For alerts, what should be paging vs ticket-only? I think:
- drop rate > 0.1% for 5m => page (enterprise impact)
- delivery latency p95 > 5m for 10m => page
- exporter target failures => ticket unless sustained 30m
Agree?

Marcus Lin: Joining late here. Agree on staging game-day.
Strong preference that we treat sustained exporter failures as page for enterprise tiers, even if the app is fine. Audit logs are a control.

Aisha Bello: Got it. I’ll adjust thresholds/route by tier if possible.

Logan Wright: Re: dashboards, I can put a temporary panel on the standard on-call dashboard:
- ingestion backlog size
- observed vs expected event_types (staging tenant)
- “missing_events” detector counts
Will remove after if we don’t want clutter.

Allison Grant: Please don’t remove immediately—GRC will ask for evidence screenshots later. Maybe keep for 2 weeks.

Rafael Mendes: Agenda proposal:
10:00 Kickoff (roles, comms channel, stop conditions)
10:05 Baseline: verify alerts green, dashboards linked, runbook open
10:10 Execute outage scenario A (sink unreachable)
10:20 Perform 6–8 sensitive actions (RBAC change, API key rotate, retention setting update, export config change)
10:30 Verify alerts + metrics + no app failures
10:35 Restore sink
10:45 Verify drain + duplicates behavior
10:55 Retro notes + action items

Sean Gallagher: Love it.
Roles:
- Facilitator: Rafael
- Break/Fix operator: Kevin
- Alert observer: Aisha
- Dashboard observer: Logan
- App action driver: Nadia (can run the checklist)
- Scribe: Sean

Any objections?

Nadia Rahman: I can drive actions. I’ll need a scripted checklist w/ exact endpoints so we hit the same paths every time.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll paste a checklist draft here tomorrow (based on the sensitive-actions taxonomy). If anyone has “must include” actions, reply.

Kevin Osei: Must include: audit log export config change + disable/enable export target. That’s where we saw weird retry loops.

Caleb Johnson: Must include: at least one action that triggers an async job (e.g., “rotate key” that queues follow-up). Correlation IDs tend to get lost there.

Neha Kapoor: Stop conditions: if staging impact spreads beyond our tenant or we see unexpected blast radius (e.g., shared exporter), we roll back immediately.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also, comms: can we run this in #eng-sre and also post a heads-up in #eng-platform and #eng-security?

Sean Gallagher: I’ll post heads-up morning-of + 15 min before start.

Aisha Bello: Can someone link the runbook we’re validating? I have an older draft.

Rafael Mendes: Current draft is in Confluence:
https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Audit+Log+Pipeline+Incident+Runbook

Allison Grant: Also link the delivery guarantees ADR so we’re aligned on expected behavior:
https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR+-+Audit+Event+Delivery+Guarantees

Kevin Osei: Quick question: during outage, are services allowed to drop audit events once buffer full? I want us to explicitly observe what happens and document for customer comms.

Marcus Lin: Yes, depending on mode. Goal is to be honest: at-least-once where feasible, but some paths may be best-effort if sink is down long enough.
We should make sure metrics make drops obvious.

Logan Wright: I can add a panel for “audit_events_dropped_total” (by service) and “buffer_full_total”. If those counters don’t exist in staging, that’s an action item.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Action items before Tue:
- Sean: draft sensitive actions checklist + share (by EOD tomorrow)
- Kevin: confirm how to blackhole sink in staging + required perms
- Aisha: confirm alert routing + thresholds for staging
- Logan: prep dashboard panels + filters
- Nadia: confirm creds for doing RBAC/key/retention/export actions in staging

Redwood Incident Bot: Reminder set by Sean Gallagher: "Audit sink outage game-day (staging)" scheduled for Tue 10:00 PT. Temporary war room channel: #war-audit-gameday-2025-02-??

Connor O'Brien: pls avoid creating new channels w/ "war-" prefix unless it’s an actual incident. Maybe "#gameday-audit-sink"?

Sean Gallagher: Good catch. I’ll create #gameday-audit-sink and invite relevant folks.

Rafael Mendes: Last ask: during the game-day, can we capture 2–3 screenshots (dash + alert + runbook step) for evidence? Olga will want it.

Sean Gallagher: Yep, I’ll do screenshots + store in the project folder.

Aisha Bello: I’ll also export the alert history after the test (timestamps, who got paged).

Kevin Osei: I’ll write up the exact kubectl/netpol steps I use so we can repeat later in prod canary.

Sean Gallagher: Perfect. Thread stays open for checklist suggestions.

title: #eng-sre: 1739471188
preview:
Aisha Bello: hey — can someone drop the Grafana links for the provision-to-ready dashboards? also before we flip alerts on, want to sanity check which labels are “safe” (cardinality) for the new metrics

Rafael Mendes: +1. I’m getting asked by oncall what to look at when nodes stuck in Validating. I have the SLO doc but no dashboard URL yet.

Sergio Costa: dashboards are in the observability pack but not sure everyone has the folder bookmarked. give me 2 mins, will paste links.

Miles Thornton: also re: labels — we should align w/ Allison’s guidance. last thing we need is per-node tags blowing up TSDB right before we enable alert rules.

Allison Grant: yup, please do NOT ship/alert on metrics with `node_id`, `instance_id`, `hostname`, `k8s_node`, etc. For provision-to-ready we should be able to get everything we need with coarse dims.

Aisha Bello: current proposal (from PR) looked like it had `node` in tags. that’s a no-go if it’s per-node.

Sergio Costa: ok links:
- Provision-to-ready overview (P50/P95, success rate): https://grafana.prod.redwood.ai/d/prov2ready-overview/provision-to-ready-overview?orgId=1
- Step breakdown (latency per stage): https://grafana.prod.redwood.ai/d/prov2ready-steps/provision-to-ready-step-breakdown?orgId=1
- Failures heatmap (region/gpu): https://grafana.prod.redwood.ai/d/prov2ready-failures/provision-to-ready-failures?orgId=1
- Quarantine counts + reasons: https://grafana.prod.redwood.ai/d/prov2ready-quarantine/provision-to-ready-quarantine?orgId=1

Rafael Mendes: tysm. do these require the new “Fleet Readiness” permission group? couple folks on my rotation get 403.

Sergio Costa: yeah, some are behind the new folder perms. INT ticket is open for access. for now, if anyone’s blocked, ping me or file INT and tag Aisha.

Sean Gallagher: re: alerts — hard requirement: we do not page while dashboards are behind 403s for oncall. make sure the access request path is in the runbook.

Aisha Bello: ack. will add a section + link to INT-1042 once that’s done.

Miles Thornton: back to metric dims: what are we actually emitting right now?

Sergio Costa: from the instrumentation PR, these are the main ones:
- `fleet_provision_time_to_ready_seconds` (histogram)
- `fleet_provision_step_duration_seconds{step=...}` (histogram)
- `fleet_provision_step_failures_total{step=..., reason=...}` (counter)
- `fleet_readiness_state_transitions_total{from=...,to=...}` (counter)

Sergio Costa: label candidates we currently have wired in some places: `region`, `gpu_sku`, `pool_id`, `image_channel`, `step`, `result`

Allison Grant: `region`, `gpu_sku`, `image_channel`, `step`, `result` are fine.

Allison Grant: `pool_id` is the big question. if it’s truly “one per customer pool” and we have lots, it’s going to be high-card over time. can we replace with something coarser like `pool_type` (dedicated/shared) + maybe `pool_tier`?

Aisha Bello: for alerting I don’t need per-pool. I need “is the system broken” and “is rollout broken in us-east/a100” type signals.

Miles Thornton: same for SLO. SLI should be aggregated w/ `region` and `gpu_sku` at most.

Rafael Mendes: + for triage it’s nice to filter by pool, but that can be logs/traces. metrics should stay low-card.

Sergio Costa: we can keep `pool_id` out of Prom metrics and instead include it in trace/span attributes + logs. dashboard can have a drill-down link to logs query by pool.

Allison Grant: +1. rule of thumb: metrics = aggregation, logs/traces = per-entity.

Aisha Bello: can we write down the allowed label set so we don’t have to relitigate per PR?

Allison Grant: suggested “allowed” list for provision-to-ready metrics:
- allowed: `region`, `gpu_sku`, `image_channel` (candidate/stable), `step` (bounded enum), `result` (success/failure/timeout), `reason` (bounded enum, keep tight), `state` (bounded)
- not allowed: `node_id`, `hostname`, `instance_id`, `k8s_node`, `pool_id`, `pool_name`, `customer_id`, `request_id`

Allison Grant: and for `reason`: please cap it to a known set. don’t dump raw error strings into labels.

Sergio Costa: makes sense. right now `reason` was coming from a mapper, but there are a couple “unknown:<raw>” fallbacks. I’ll remove those and map to `unknown` only.

Miles Thornton: any other risk areas? histograms can also explode if we put a lot of label combos on them.

Allison Grant: yes — histograms + high label combos is worst case. keep label set minimal on histos. counters can handle slightly more, but still prefer the same rules.

Aisha Bello: ok proposal for alerts gating:
1) confirm no per-node labels
2) confirm `pool_id` not present in Prom metrics
3) confirm dashboard perms for oncall
4) then enable alert rules in “ticket mode” for 1 week before paging

Sean Gallagher: +1. and add a rollback lever: if metrics cardinality spikes, we disable the exporter/instrumentation immediately.

Sergio Costa: we already have a config flag in the agent to disable per-step metrics emission. I’ll document it + add to the alert tuning notes.

Rafael Mendes: what’s the easiest check for “are we blowing up cardinality” once this lands?

Allison Grant: in Grafana, use the TSDB status panel + Prom query like:
`count(count by(__name__, region, gpu_sku, step, result) ({__name__=~"fleet_.*"}))`
If that number jumps unexpectedly after a deploy, we revert.

Aisha Bello: cool. action items:
- Sergio: confirm dashboards accessible + post final links in runbook; remove `pool_id` + raw `reason` fallback
- Allison: drop the “allowed labels” list into the observability standards page (or send me text to paste)
- Miles/Aisha: keep alerts in ticket-only until above is green

Sergio Costa: ack. I’ll also add a note to the PR: “do not add pool_id/node_id labels” so it’s explicit.

Miles Thornton: thanks. once the label set is fixed, I’ll update the SLO doc to reference the aggregated metric (no pool dimension).

Aisha Bello: perfect. leaving alerts disabled for now; will revisit after the instrumentation follow-up lands.

4) then enable alert rules in “ticket mode” for 1 week before paging

Sean Gallagher: +1. and add a rollback lever: if metrics cardinality spikes, we disable the exporter/instrumentation immediately.

Sergio Costa: we already have a config flag in the agent to disable per-step metrics emission. I’ll document it + add to the alert tuning notes.

Rafael Mendes: what’s the easiest check for “are we blowing up cardinality” once this lands?

Allison Grant: in Grafana, use the TSDB status panel + Prom query like:
`count(count by(__name__, region, gpu_sku, step, result) ({__name__=~"fleet_.*"}))`
If that number jumps unexpectedly after a deploy, we revert.

Aisha Bello: cool. action items:
- Sergio: confirm dashboards accessible + post final links in runbook; remove `pool_id` + raw `reason` fallback
- Allison: drop the “allowed labels” list into the observability standards page (or send me text to paste)
- Miles/Aisha: keep alerts in ticket-only until above is green

Sergio Costa: ack. I’ll also add a note to the PR: “do not add pool_id/node_id labels” so it’s explicit.

Miles Thornton: thanks. once the label set is fixed, I’ll update the SLO doc to reference the aggregated metric (no pool dimension).

Aisha Bello: perfect. leaving alerts disabled for now; will revisit after the instrumentation follow-up lands.

title: #eng-sre: 1739412204
preview:
Rafael Mendes: we should treat Private upgrades like a first-class "change event" from an SRE POV. proposing a small set of SLO-ish signals + alerts, and also annotate upgrade windows in dashboards so we stop guessing "was this during an upgrade?" 

Rafael Mendes: draft list:
- upgrade success rate (N-1 -> N) by environment type (VPC vs on-prem)
- precheck failures by category (storage, RBAC/OIDC, DNS/TLS, artifact availability)
- DB migration duration + failure count
- rollback invoked count + reason
- error budget burn during upgrade windows (api 5xx, control-plane disconnect)

Sean Gallagher: +1. if we can’t get full SLO right away, at least alerts with clear paging semantics.

Sean Gallagher: suggestion: 2 tiers
1) "operator actionable" alerts during upgrade window (warn/page based on customer tier)
2) "platform regression" alerts (paging regardless) if we see spikes across multiple envs after a release train cut

Allison Grant: on annotations: easiest path is Grafana annotations via Loki or a webhook. we already have a pattern from deploy annotations in hosted. we can mirror that.

Allison Grant: question: do we have a canonical "upgrade window" start/stop event we can rely on? installer or control-plane?

Ethan Park: installer has the best context (it knows when prechecks start, when it actually begins applying plan). control-plane sees upgrade rollouts but not always the initial attempt.

Devon Price: we can have installer emit an "upgrade.lifecycle" event into the same pipeline as deploy events. doesn’t have to be fancy: start, prechecks_pass, backup_complete, apply_start, apply_complete, rollback_start, rollback_complete.

Rafael Mendes: yes exactly. also want annotations to include version (from->to) and env id (cluster name or private deployment id).

Connor O'Brien: release eng angle: we need to label these annotations with the release train version too (private platform build + installer build). otherwise it’ll be hard to correlate.

Allison Grant: agreed. annotation tags could be like:
`private_upgrade`, `from=1.18.3`, `to=1.19.0`, `installer=0.42.1`, `train=2025.02.2`, `env=vpc|onprem`, `customer_tier=enterprise` (optional)

Sahana Iyer: for CI + staging: can we also annotate e2e upgrade jobs? it’d help when we see flaky metrics in staging dashboards.

Allison Grant: yep. for staging, we can have CI job send same event type with env=staging + job url.

grafana-bot: (auto) FYI: Grafana annotation API docs https://grafana.redwood.example/docs/annotations

Sean Gallagher: alert proposals (paging candidates):
- migration stuck: `db_migration_duration_seconds > 900` (or p95 > threshold) during upgrade window
- control plane disconnected: `private_control_plane_connectivity == 0` for > 2m during upgrade apply
- helm hook job failures: `kube_job_status_failed{job=~".*preupgrade.*"} > 0`

Sean Gallagher: non-paging / ticket:
- precheck failure rate > X% over 7d (signals docs gap or false positives)
- rollback frequency > baseline post-release

Rafael Mendes: + add "upgrade aborted" count. we see a lot of "operator hit ctrl-c" when it looks stuck.

Devon Price: we can differentiate "aborted" vs "failed" in the state machine if we store it in the upgrade state file + emit event.

Connor O'Brien: be careful: some customers run in fully airgapped mode. annotation emission must not require external network. need to support: (a) they have our observability stack connected, or (b) offline capture for support bundle.

Allison Grant: right. we can do both:
- if observability endpoint configured, emit events
- always write a local "upgrade_events.jsonl" artifact that gets picked up by support bundle

Ethan Park: control-plane also emits audit log events (start/stop etc). those are more compliance-oriented, but we can reuse fields. might avoid two different schemas.

Sean Gallagher: good point. for SRE dashboards we don’t need the full audit payload, but aligning names/fields is good.

Rafael Mendes: re: how to scope alerting to upgrade windows — we should gate with an "upgrade_in_progress" metric per env. otherwise we’ll page on migration duration in normal ops.

Allison Grant: implementation detail: installer emits a gauge metric `private_upgrade_in_progress{env_id,...}=1` while it’s applying. but metrics from installer are tricky because it’s not a long-running service.

Allison Grant: alternative: emit events, then have a small "upgrade-event-exporter" in cluster turn that into a time series + annotations. or (simpler) rely on Helm chart jobs setting a configmap/lease that exporter reads.

Sean Gallagher: I’d rather avoid a new service right now. can we get enough with annotations only + alert on job failure counts (those are already in kube-state-metrics)?

Allison Grant: yes for v1. annotations + kube job failures + app error rate spikes should cover most incidents.

Sahana Iyer: + prechecks are before any in-cluster changes. annotations marking precheck start/fail would still be useful for support tickets.

Rafael Mendes: ok so v1 scope:
- annotations: precheck_start, precheck_fail/pass, backup_start/complete, apply_start/complete, rollback_start/complete
- alerts (paging): preupgrade job failures, migration job failure, control-plane disconnect during apply
- alerts (ticket): precheck failure rate by category, rollback rate by version

Connor O'Brien: can we also add a dashboard panel that overlays error rate with upgrade annotations? (similar to hosted deploy dashboard)

Allison Grant: yes, I’m already working with observability-pack PR for "private upgrade dashboards". will include a standard "Upgrade timeline" row and panels for migrations / precheck outcomes.

incident-bot: (reactions: :eyes: x3)

Sean Gallagher: once we have that, update on-call playbook: "if you see error spike, check upgrade annotation; if upgrade in progress, follow upgrade mitigation runbook; else treat as normal incident".

Rafael Mendes: +1. I’ll open a small follow-up task to wire annotation emission to installer lifecycle events. Connor/Allison will sync on field names.

Allison Grant: drop links here when you have them. I’ll align dashboards + tags.

Connor O'Brien: ack. also, for GA gating: we should require "annotations visible" in staging before we ship the release train.

Sahana Iyer: I can add that as an assertion in the e2e job (at least verify the event file exists in support bundle artifact). 

Rafael Mendes: sounds good. thanks all.
