title: #eng-sre: 1739381107
preview:
Aisha Bello: trying to nail down which saturation signals the bot should *highlight* vs just attach as links. GPU util is noisy. queue depth seems actionable. KV cache pressure is… maybe. thoughts?

Logan Wright: +1 util alone is misleading. 95% util might be “healthy” if batching is doing its thing.
Logan Wright: what’s actionable is *util + symptoms* (p95 latency burn / 5xx / streaming disconnects) and a second saturation indicator.

Jacob Stein: agree. on the incidents I’ve worked, queue depth / scheduler backpressure was the “oh crap” signal.
Jacob Stein: GPU util is like… always 70-98% during busy hours.

Rafael Mendes: can we define “actionable” as: when it changes what we do in first 5 min? (reroute, shed load, scale, revert deploy)

Miles Thornton: for bot v2 we need simple heuristics. if we over-highlight, oncall ignores the card.

Allison Grant: we should also align w alert tuning. we already page on some of these; bot highlights should map to “this is the likely cause”.

Aisha Bello: ok, proposing categories:
- Highlight: queue depth/backpressure, admission control rejects, KV cache evictions (maybe)
- Mention but don’t highlight: GPU util, HBM/mem util
- Deep link only: per-GPU breakdowns, kernel-level stuff

Nadia Rahman: +1. also: be careful with metric definitions across hosted vs dedicated pools.
Nadia Rahman: for hosted, queue depth is at scheduler. for dedicated, some customers run a different batching config and queue depth behaves differently.

Logan Wright: metric candidates we can standardize:
- scheduler_queue_depth (p95 over 5m)
- scheduler_backpressure_ratio (time spent throttling / total)
- admission_control_reject_rate (per sec)
- gpu_sm_utilization (avg over 1m)
- kv_cache_evictions_rate OR kv_cache_pressure (whatever runtime exports)

Caleb Johnson: KV cache “pressure” is tricky. Some runtimes report it as a gauge but semantics differ.
Caleb Johnson: evictions rate is more actionable than “% used”. % used is always ~near cap by design.

Mina Haddad: from routing perspective, if bot highlights “regional saturation” we want it to suggest: route to alt region / enable fallback model variant / reduce max_tokens.
Mina Haddad: queue depth + backpressure is the cleanest input to that decision.

Ravi Menon: +1, and the degraded mode playbook basically keys off “are we queued + throttling” vs “are we failing for other reasons”.
Ravi Menon: GPU util doesn’t tell you if you’re queuing, it just tells you you’re busy.

Aisha Bello: thresholds? need something that triggers the yellow/red callouts in the card.

Allison Grant: current alert draft (not final):
- queue_depth_p95 > 2x baseline for 10m OR absolute > N (depends on pool)
- backpressure_ratio > 0.2 for 5m
- admission_control_reject_rate > 1% of requests for 5m
- kv_cache_evictions_rate elevated + latency burn

Jacob Stein: absolute queue depth thresholds are hard bc RPS varies. can we do time-in-queue instead?
Jacob Stein: e.g. “queue_wait_p95 > 200ms for 5m” would be more portable.

Logan Wright: we have queue wait time but it’s not consistently instrumented in dedicated yet.
Logan Wright: we can do both:
- if queue_wait_p95 exists: use that
- else fallback to queue_depth normalized by capacity (queue_depth / admitted_rps or / gpu_count)

Miles Thornton: bot logic needs to be deterministic + cheap. we can’t be computing baselines on the fly.
Miles Thornton: maybe thresholds are “static per pool tier” and we tune later.

Nadia Rahman: we can ship “v0 thresholds” with conservative highlights:
- RED: backpressure_ratio >= 0.35 OR admission rejects >= 3%
- YELLOW: backpressure_ratio 0.2-0.35 OR admission rejects 1-3%
- queue_depth used as supporting context, not primary highlight

Aisha Bello: what about GPU util? we still include it, but don’t highlight unless it’s low (to rule out saturation?)

Caleb Johnson: interesting angle: low GPU util + high queue depth = likely scheduler issue or upstream throttling / misconfig.
Caleb Johnson: but that’s an edge case. don’t bake too much magic.

Rafael Mendes: for first 5 min, simplest “if backpressure + rejects are high, it’s saturation” is good.
Rafael Mendes: queue depth alone causes too many false positives during deploys (warmup) and incident bot will scream constantly.

Allison Grant: +1. queue depth spikes are common during rolling restarts.
Allison Grant: but backpressure_ratio sustained is rare without real saturation.

Mina Haddad: also: if we highlight saturation, add a line that surfaces current routing policy + fallback enabled/disabled. that’s what oncall needs to decide action.

Jacob Stein: KV cache: what threshold would we use? eviction rate > X/s?

Logan Wright: runtime team suggested: “evictions > 0 for 5m” is already bad for some workloads, but it depends.
Logan Wright: maybe we highlight KV cache only when:
- evictions_rate > 0 AND p95 latency burn > 2x
- OR “kv_cache_hit_rate < 0.8” if available

Aisha Bello: ok, consensus check:
1) Primary highlight: scheduler backpressure_ratio + admission rejects
2) Secondary: queue_depth/queue_wait (supporting)
3) KV cache: highlight only if evictions + latency burn
4) GPU util: include but never highlight as “cause” alone

Miles Thornton: works for bot UX. we can implement “Highlights” block w 1-2 bullets max.
Miles Thornton: everything else in “Signals” block + dashboard links.

Rafael Mendes: pls add note about tuning thresholds per pool type (hosted shared vs dedicated) so we don’t break enterprise trust.

Allison Grant: I’ll paste in proposed wording for the card:
- “Saturation likely (scheduler throttling + rejects). Consider failover / scale / load shed.”
- “Saturation possible (queueing elevated). Check deploy + config changes.”

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll update the standard doc + open a ticket for initial thresholds. Logan/Allison can you sanity check numbers once I draft?

Logan Wright: yup. also please keep query bounded: only pull p95 over 5m + last 30m sparkline link, no high-cardinality breakdown.

Caleb Johnson: +1 on bounded queries. per-tenant breakdown from bot will kill us during incidents.

Aisha Bello: cool. action items:
- Aisha: write threshold proposal + bot highlight rules
- Logan/Nadia: confirm metric names available in hosted + dedicated
- Allison: align with alert tuning so bot highlights match paging logic
- Miles: incorporate into bot card layout (highlight vs signals)

Miles Thornton: ack. will wire into v2 card gating (only show highlight if confidence high).

title: #eng-sre: 1739218890
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Kicking off SRE thoughts on data residency enforcement monitoring. We need dashboards + alerting before we flip this past staging.

Sean Gallagher: What I think we need at minimum:
- blocked requests (count + rate) broken down by region + route + org
- policy eval latency (p50/p95/p99)
- cross-region routing attempts (incl when fallback would have happened)
- streaming vs non-streaming error consistency
- audit log emit failures (if we depend on those for evidence)

Allison Grant: +1. Also want to distinguish “blocked_by_policy” vs “blocked_due_to_misconfig/unknown_region_mapping” vs “failed_closed_internal_error”. Those should be separate counters.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We should be careful w paging on block count alone. Once customers start pinning, some blocks are expected (misconfigured SDK endpoint, wrong project, etc). Paging should be for false positives / regressions.

Logan Wright: For telemetry naming, draft proposal:
- `residency.enforcement.blocked_requests_total{reason,region,route,org_id}`
- `residency.enforcement.allowed_requests_total{selected_region,route,org_id}`
- `residency.policy.eval_ms{result}` histogram
- `residency.routing.cross_region_attempt_total{from_region,to_region,reason}`
- `residency.gateway.error_contract_mismatch_total{endpoint}` (for the “generic 500” class)

Sean Gallagher: Love it. Can we avoid org_id as a high-card tag in the primary metrics? We can log org_id in decision logs + use sampled metrics w org_id for drilldown.

Allison Grant: Yeah. For Datadog, org_id as a tag will explode cardinality. Suggest:
- primary metrics: no org_id
- secondary: “top N orgs by blocked count” via log-based metrics or a separate log query panel

Ravi Menon: Routing-side we can emit `cross_region_attempt_total` when fallback is *considered* but blocked. That’s a good proxy for “we would have failed over to keep latency, but residency forced us not to.”

Mina Haddad: We should also track `fallback_suppressed_total` specifically. Cross-region attempt could include other paths.

Jordan Lee: Policy engine: eval path is deterministic and should be microseconds-ish locally, but we have cache lookups + config fetch in some cases. Expect p95 maybe ~5-10ms in prod if cold. We should alert if p95 > 50ms for 10m.

Amara Diallo: Gateway: for enforcement blocks we’re returning 409 w structured payload. We should add a metric from gateway that counts 409s with `error_code=residency_policy_violation` vs other 409s.

Rafael Mendes: On paging thresholds: we need something that reflects “this is breaking legit traffic”. Proposal:
- Page if `blocked_rate` (blocked / total) > 2% for 10m *AND* total rps > 50 (to avoid tiny spikes).
- Page if `blocked_requests_total` spikes > 5x baseline week-over-week in any region.
- Page if `failed_closed_internal_error` > 0.1% for 5m (this is us breaking, not customer misconfig).

Sean Gallagher: 2% might be too low or too high depending on rollout stage. During early allowlist, a single customer misconfig could create 20% block rate and we don’t want a page every time.

Allison Grant: Maybe split alerts:
- Warning (non-page): blocked_rate > 2% for 15m, notify #eng-sre + #support
- Page: blocked_rate > 10% for 10m OR blocked absolute > 10k/10m in a region
- Page immediately on `failed_closed_internal_error` (policy engine 5xx) because that’s “false positives by bug”.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also page if gateway returns generic 500 instead of the contract. That’ll create support escalations fast.

Amara Diallo: We can implement `error_contract_mismatch_total` by comparing response shape for streaming path. If endpoint is `/v1/chat/completions` streaming and we hit error, ensure it’s the standardized event. Otherwise increment.

Logan Wright: For blocked requests, we should tag with `reason` enums. Draft list:
- `region_not_allowed`
- `no_primary_region_configured`
- `policy_not_found` (should be rare)
- `fallback_disallowed`
- `unknown_internal_region`
- `enforcement_internal_error`

Sean Gallagher: `policy_not_found` should basically never happen once config is loaded. If it happens it’s a bug. We can page if > 0.01%.

Mina Haddad: What’s expected “healthy” block rate? For pinned customers, I’d expect close to 0 after they configure endpoints/SDK correctly. Maybe a trickle from rotated keys / old services.

Rafael Mendes: My guess:
- Dogfood + allowlist: can be noisy while people test.
- GA steady-state: block_rate < 0.1% overall. Anything > 1% sustained probably means docs/SDK confusion or a routing regression.

Allison Grant: We should build the dashboard with both:
1) global block rate
2) block rate for “pinned org traffic” only (needs a tag like `policy_present=true`)
3) top reasons over time
4) per-region view (EU/US/APAC)

Jordan Lee: We can include `policy_present` and `policy_level` (org/project/key) as low-card tags. That should be safe.

Sean Gallagher: Concrete alert plan (v0):
- Page: `residency.enforcement.failed_closed_total / requests_total > 0.1%` for 5m (any region)
- Page: `residency.gateway.error_contract_mismatch_total > 50` in 10m (any endpoint)
- Notify: `blocked_rate > 2%` for 15m (global)
- Notify: `blocked_rate > 5%` for 15m in a single region
- Notify: `policy.eval_p95_ms > 50ms` for 10m

Rafael Mendes: Add one more page: sudden drop in allowed requests in pinned regions could indicate we’re blocking everything. e.g. `allowed_requests_total{selected_region=eu} drops >30% vs 1h baseline AND blocked_rate rises`.

Allison Grant: That’s good but trickier. We can approximate with anomaly detection on `allowed_requests_total` by region. Let’s keep it as a “watch” alert at first.

Datadog Bot: Allison Grant created monitor “Residency Enforcement - Failed Closed Rate” (warning: >0.05% / 5m, alert: >0.1% / 5m). Assigned to @sean.gallagher.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Thanks. Also we need runbook links in the monitors (triage steps + known rollout flags). I’ll draft and share.

Logan Wright: For queries (DD):
- Blocked rate: `sum:residency.enforcement.blocked_requests_total{*}.as_count() / (sum:residency.enforcement.blocked_requests_total{*}.as_count() + sum:residency.enforcement.allowed_requests_total{*}.as_count())`
- Failed closed: `sum:residency.enforcement.blocked_requests_total{reason:enforcement_internal_error}.as_count() / (blocked+allowed)`
- Cross-region attempts: `sum:residency.routing.cross_region_attempt_total{*}.as_count()` by `to_region`

Amara Diallo: FYI gateway metric names will land as `api.gateway.*` prefix unless we explicitly register custom. I can alias but want consistency. Ok to have both?

Allison Grant: Prefer a single canonical metric for dashboards. If gateway emits `api.gateway.residency_policy_violation_total` we can use that and still keep the platform-level metric. But don’t duplicate charts.

Sean Gallagher: Action items:
- Allison: draft dashboard layout + monitor list in the observability pack PR
- Logan: finalize metric names/tags + cardinality guidelines (esp org_id)
- Amara: add gateway-specific counters for 409 residency blocks + contract mismatches
- Rafael/Sean: define paging thresholds for rollout phases (allowlist vs GA) and put in runbook

Rafael Mendes: I’ll add a note: during allowlist we should route block-rate alerts to #eng-sre only (no paging) unless failed-closed. We can flip to paging at GA.

Sean Gallagher: Works. Let’s aim to have the first version in staging by EOW so we can test with the E2E suite.
text:
Sean Gallagher: Kicking off SRE thoughts on data residency enforcement monitoring. We need dashboards + alerting before we flip this past staging.

Sean Gallagher: What I think we need at minimum:
- blocked requests (count + rate) broken down by region + route + org
- policy eval latency (p50/p95/p99)
- cross-region routing attempts (incl when fallback would have happened)
- streaming vs non-streaming error consistency
- audit log emit failures (if we depend on those for evidence)

Allison Grant: +1. Also want to distinguish “blocked_by_policy” vs “blocked_due_to_misconfig/unknown_region_mapping” vs “failed_closed_internal_error”. Those should be separate counters.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We should be careful w paging on block count alone. Once customers start pinning, some blocks are expected (misconfigured SDK endpoint, wrong project, etc). Paging should be for false positives / regressions.

Logan Wright: For telemetry naming, draft proposal:
- `residency.enforcement.blocked_requests_total{reason,region,route,org_id}`
- `residency.enforcement.allowed_requests_total{selected_region,route,org_id}`
- `residency.policy.eval_ms{result}` histogram
- `residency.routing.cross_region_attempt_total{from_region,to_region,reason}`
- `residency.gateway.error_contract_mismatch_total{endpoint}` (for the “generic 500” class)

Sean Gallagher: Love it. Can we avoid org_id as a high-card tag in the primary metrics? We can log org_id in decision logs + use sampled metrics w org_id for drilldown.

Allison Grant: Yeah. For Datadog, org_id as a tag will explode cardinality. Suggest:
- primary metrics: no org_id
- secondary: “top N orgs by blocked count” via log-based metrics or a separate log query panel

Ravi Menon: Routing-side we can emit `cross_region_attempt_total` when fallback is *considered* but blocked. That’s a good proxy for “we would have failed over to keep latency, but residency forced us not to.”

Mina Haddad: We should also track `fallback_suppressed_total` specifically. Cross-region attempt could include other paths.

- `no_primary_region_configured`
- `policy_not_found` (should be rare)
- `fallback_disallowed`
- `unknown_internal_region`
- `enforcement_internal_error`

Sean Gallagher: `policy_not_found` should basically never happen once config is loaded. If it happens it’s a bug. We can page if > 0.01%.

Mina Haddad: What’s expected “healthy” block rate? For pinned customers, I’d expect close to 0 after they configure endpoints/SDK correctly. Maybe a trickle from rotated keys / old services.

- GA steady-state: block_rate < 0.1% overall. Anything > 1% sustained probably means docs/SDK confusion or a routing regression.

1) global block rate
2) block rate for “pinned org traffic” only (needs a tag like `policy_present=true`)
3) top reasons over time
4) per-region view (EU/US/APAC)

Jordan Lee: We can include `policy_present` and `policy_level` (org/project/key) as low-card tags. That should be safe.

Sean Gallagher: Concrete alert plan (v0):

- Page: `residency.gateway.error_contract_mismatch_total > 50` in 10m (any endpoint)

title: #eng-sre: 1739132202
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Dropping this here to unblock the v2 measurement section. We keep saying “MTTR reduction” but we need to agree on baseline + how we’re going to measure. Can folks sanity check:

Baseline (last ~12 weeks, Sev0-2 only):
- Deploy regressions (service-level): median MTTR ~58m, p90 ~2h05m
- Misconfig / routing-policy: median ~42m, p90 ~1h30m
- Capacity/saturation (regional): median ~1h12m, p90 ~3h10m

These are from PD resolved time - created time, then filtered to “prod” incidents tagged sev0/1/2.

Questions:
1) Do we want MTTR = time to mitigate (customer impact stopped) or time to resolve (all follow-ups done)?
2) How do we avoid gaming (e.g., resolve early, keep mitigation ongoing)?
3) What’s the “context completeness” score that we can track per incident thread?

Sean Gallagher: +1 we need to be super explicit. For reliability KPI we should use “time to mitigate” (impact ended), not “post-incident cleanup”. Otherwise MTTR is hostage to postmortem/admin.

Sean Gallagher: Re: #2, we can enforce definition: mitigation timestamp = when error rate/latency back within SLO guardrail for 10 mins OR when on-call marks incident mitigated in PD. If those differ, we should log both and reconcile.

Aisha Bello: For measurement I’d like us to break this into:
- TT (time-to-triage): incident declared -> “likely cause” identified (deploy/config/sat)
- TM (time-to-mitigate): incident declared -> impact ended
MTTR is a lagging metric and mixes a bunch.

Aisha Bello: If bot v2 is doing its job, we should see TT drop more consistently than TM (TM can be waiting on capacity / rollback windows / etc).

Miles Thornton: +100. Bot is mostly about first 5 mins. We can instrument “first context posted” time + whether it included the 3 cards.

Miles Thornton: Also, define “incident declared” as first PD trigger vs first message in #incidents? PD is cleaner but sometimes sev2s start in Slack.

Rafael Mendes: Yeah, incidents often start in #incidents w/ “seeing 503s?” then PD later. Baseline above uses PD created time so it’s consistent. But then bot context is posted in Slack threads.

Jacob Stein: We can bridge by logging bot events with incident_id (PD) when present, and thread_ts always. When PD isn’t created yet, bot can log as “unbound” and then later attach when we detect the PD link in thread.

Talia Benson: From program mgmt POV: for the Q1 KPI we promised “MTTR reduction”, but we can absolutely report a leading indicator like TT + “context completeness”. We just need to make it defensible.

Paula Mendes: On the PRD side we were using “context completeness score” as % of incidents where deploy + config diff + saturation signals were attached within 5 mins of incident declare.

Paula Mendes: Maybe score = (deploy_card + config_card + saturation_card) / 3, with a time constraint.

Allison Grant: Careful: saturation signals aren’t always relevant (e.g., pure deploy regression). Completeness shouldn’t penalize incidents where a card is intentionally skipped.

Aisha Bello: Agree. Maybe define a “recommended cards” set based on initial symptom class (latency SLO burn vs 5xx vs streaming disconnects). We can do a simple mapping.

Aisha Bello: Example mapping:
- 5xx spike: deploy + config diff + saturation
- latency spike: saturation + config diff (+ deploy if recent)
- streaming disconnects: deploy + saturation (queue/backpressure)

Rafael Mendes: But we don’t know symptom class at start unless we infer from alert type.

Miles Thornton: We do have alert context (SLO burn link or monitor name) when PD triggers. For Slack-only starts… less.

Jacob Stein: We can do two flavors:
1) “raw completeness”: of the 3 cards, how many present
2) “expected completeness”: of expected cards based on trigger/alert type, how many present
Expected completeness only computed when we have a trigger type.

Sean Gallagher: That sounds reasonable. Keep it simple; avoid ML classification.

Rafael Mendes: What should the time window be? “Within 5 mins” is nice but might be too strict if telemetry query cache is cold.

Miles Thornton: For v2 we’re aiming for <30s bot response, but during outages we’ll degrade. For measurement, I’d propose:
- 0–5 mins: “first-5 completeness”
- 0–15 mins: “first-15 completeness”
so we don’t miss improvements when we fall back to cached links first.

Allison Grant: +1, and we should track “degraded mode used” as a dimension, otherwise a big outage will tank our score.

Aisha Bello: Instrumentation suggestion (metrics/events):
- incident_bot.context_generated{version="v2", card="deploy|config|saturation", status="ok|timeout|permission_denied|degraded|skipped"}
- incident_bot.context_latency_ms{card=...}
- incident_bot.context_completeness_raw (0..1)
- incident_bot.context_completeness_expected (0..1)

Aisha Bello: And tie them to incident_id + thread_ts + sev + region + service (if safe).

Jacob Stein: Service/region OK, tenant_id is tricky. We should not log tenant identifiers in bot metrics unless it’s hashed + approved.

Sean Gallagher: Please no tenant ids in metrics. If we need tenant dimension later, do it via access-controlled logs, not Prom.

Paula Mendes: For the KPI deck we probably only need aggregates by incident type + sev. No tenant.

Rafael Mendes: Re: baseline—any concerns with how I computed it? PD “resolved” timestamp isn’t always “impact ended”.

Sean Gallagher: It’s noisy, but it’s what we have historically. For v2, we can improve data quality by adding a PD custom field or Slack button: “mark mitigated”. But that’s process change-ish.

Talia Benson: Process change is out of scope-ish, but a lightweight “Mitigated ✅” button in the bot thread that writes a timestamp to our incident timeline export seems ok. Not forcing behavior, just capturing when people already say “mitigated”.

Miles Thornton: We can do a Slack action “Mark mitigated” that:
- posts a small ack in-thread
- emits event to incident-timeline service
- optionally nudges to update PD
But we need to be careful about permissions + not spamming.

Jacob Stein: +1. Also idempotent. We’ll have to handle “oops clicked wrong” -> “undo mitigated” or “edit timestamp”? Might be too much. Could treat it as annotation only.

Allison Grant: Even without the button, we could heuristically parse “mitigated” messages, but that’s brittle. Better to offer button and measure adoption.

Aisha Bello: For MTTR measurement experiment design: beta cohort vs control cohort.
- Beta: SRE rotation + a couple eng-oncall rotations
- Control: rotations not opted in
We can compare pre/post within cohort + between cohorts.

Paula Mendes: We should normalize by incident class because mix can change week to week. Otherwise one saturation-heavy week makes it look worse.

Rafael Mendes: Do we have enough volume for a clean cohort split? Sev0/1s are low.

Sean Gallagher: Don’t overfit. We can report:
- median TT and TM for top-3 incident classes
- context completeness (first-5 + first-15)
- adoption (% incidents where bot v2 used)
And show a few examples qualitatively.

Jacob Stein: For “bot v2 used” we should log when v2 cards were rendered (feature flag cohort) vs v1. I’ll add version tag to the payload.

Miles Thornton: + also log “manual commands used” (e.g., /incident deploys, /incident config-diff) so we can see if people still need ad-hoc queries.

Aisha Bello: Yep. A decrease in ad-hoc commands is a good proxy for “less thrash”.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll update the measurement plan doc with:
- Definitions: TT, TM, MTTR
- Baseline numbers above (call out PD timestamp caveats)
- Context completeness raw/expected + first-5/first-15
- Instrumentation events/metrics list
- Beta/control cohorts + caveats

Rafael Mendes: Owners?
- @Aisha Bello can you own final metric definitions + dashboards?
- @Jacob Stein instrumentation fields + event schema?
- @Miles Thornton slack action idea (optional) + version/adoption metrics?

Aisha Bello: Yep I’ll own metric defs + dashboard. Will also add a “degraded mode rate” panel.

Jacob Stein: I’ll draft the event schema and post here. Targeting something like:
```
event=incident_bot_context
version=v2
thread_ts=...
incident_id=...
card=deploy
status=ok
latency_ms=214
expected=true
```
(no tenant)

Miles Thornton: I’ll take adoption metrics + manual command usage. I think “Mark mitigated” button is a nice-to-have; will scope it separately so we don’t block v2.

Talia Benson: I’ll capture this in the program tracker + make sure the exec update frames TT/completeness as leading indicators for MTTR.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: keep it boring and measurable. Also: don’t forget to exclude maintenance windows / planned failovers from baseline, they skew.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. I’ll filter those out (tagged “planned” in PD) and note it in the doc.

incident-bot: (ephemeral) Reminder: When you link a PagerDuty incident in-thread, I can attach incident_id to diagnostics events.

Allison Grant: One more thought: define “context posted” time as when the bot message hits Slack, not when the backend finished computing. Slack delivery lag can happen during outages.

Jacob Stein: We can log both: compute_finished_ts and slack_posted_ts. For the completeness window we should use slack_posted_ts.

Rafael Mendes: Perfect. Closing loop here after I update the doc. Thanks all.

Rafael Mendes: Do we have enough volume for a clean cohort split? Sev0/1s are low.

Sean Gallagher: Don’t overfit. We can report:
- median TT and TM for top-3 incident classes
- context completeness (first-5 + first-15)
- adoption (% incidents where bot v2 used)
And show a few examples qualitatively.

Jacob Stein: For “bot v2 used” we should log when v2 cards were rendered (feature flag cohort) vs v1. I’ll add version tag to the payload.

Miles Thornton: + also log “manual commands used” (e.g., /incident deploys, /incident config-diff) so we can see if people still need ad-hoc queries.

Aisha Bello: Yep. A decrease in ad-hoc commands is a good proxy for “less thrash”.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll update the measurement plan doc with:
- Definitions: TT, TM, MTTR
- Baseline numbers above (call out PD timestamp caveats)
- Context completeness raw/expected + first-5/first-15
- Instrumentation events/metrics list
- Beta/control cohorts + caveats

Rafael Mendes: Owners?
- @Aisha Bello can you own final metric definitions + dashboards?
- @Jacob Stein instrumentation fields + event schema?
- @Miles Thornton slack action idea (optional) + version/adoption metrics?

Aisha Bello: Yep I’ll own metric defs + dashboard. Will also add a “degraded mode rate” panel.

Jacob Stein: I’ll draft the event schema and post here. Targeting something like:
```
event=incident_bot_context
version=v2
thread_ts=...
incident_id=...
card=deploy
status=ok
latency_ms=214
expected=true
```
(no tenant)

Miles Thornton: I’ll take adoption metrics + manual command usage. I think “Mark mitigated” button is a nice-to-have; will scope it separately so we don’t block v2.

Talia Benson: I’ll capture this in the program tracker + make sure the exec update frames TT/completeness as leading indicators for MTTR.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: keep it boring and measurable. Also: don’t forget to exclude maintenance windows / planned failovers from baseline, they skew.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. I’ll filter those out (tagged “planned” in PD) and note it in the doc.

incident-bot: (ephemeral) Reminder: When you link a PagerDuty incident in-thread, I can attach incident_id to diagnostics events.

Allison Grant: One more thought: define “context posted” time as when the bot message hits Slack, not when the backend finished computing. Slack delivery lag can happen during outages.

Jacob Stein: We can log both: compute_finished_ts and slack_posted_ts. For the completeness window we should use slack_posted_ts.

Rafael Mendes: Perfect. Closing loop here after I update the doc. Thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1739121100
preview:
Rafael Mendes: DR game day outcomes + immediate fixes (Private backup/restore) — posting while it’s fresh. Thanks all for jumping in.

Rafael Mendes: Scenario we ran: control-plane corruption (config DB + partial k8s state) -> restore into clean cluster (same region) using latest nightly backup artifact + Helm/Terraform templates. Goal RTO: 60m, RPO: 15m.

Rafael Mendes: High level result:
- RPO: ✅ met for control-plane state (backup at T-9m).
- RTO: ❌ missed (restore-to-healthy took ~1h47m; root cause was preflight gaps + ordering + one KMS retry loop).

Sean Gallagher: +1. We basically lost ~30m to “unknown unknowns” in restore ordering and then another ~15m on chasing a false negative in the validation check.

Miles Thornton: For the record: this is a good failure mode to catch now. The core restore path worked, but our operator experience isn’t deterministic yet.

Rafael Mendes: Timeline (approx):
- 10:03 start / declare game day
- 10:08 backup artifact pulled from staging bucket
- 10:18 restore apply started
- 10:26 restore job failed (missing CRDs)
- 10:41 CRDs applied manually, rerun restore
- 11:02 restore progressed but hung on KMS decrypt (retry loop)
- 11:18 forced backoff tweak + rerun (worked)
- 11:37 control plane healthy
- 11:50 tenant routing policies verified / smoke test pass

Benji Okafor: Missing CRDs was 100% on us — we assumed chart install had already laid them down. In a “clean cluster” DR that assumption doesn’t hold.

Ethan Park: Agree. We need restore to be self-sufficient (or explicitly fail w/ a clear “install prereqs first” checklist).

Hanae Suzuki: Re: KMS hang — it wasn’t “down”, but we got throttled and our retry strategy basically spammed. We need bounded exponential backoff + a clear error after N minutes.

Marcus Lin: And log the provider / key ID / region *without* leaking anything sensitive. Right now the error is opaque, so people start doing risky stuff.

Sergio Costa: Observability note: dashboard showed backup success but restore drill metric didn’t emit until the very end, so we had a blind spot during the hang. Need intermediate progress events.

Allison Grant: +1. We should emit spans per restore phase (preflight, decrypt, unpack, apply, migrate, validate) w/ durations and outcome labels.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging angle: if the fix is “apply CRDs first”, that needs to live in the offline bundle too (air-gapped). We can’t point customers to fetch YAMLs externally.

Bruno Silva: Also saw NFS slowness during unpack step in the lab runs last week. Not today’s blocker, but we should add a warning/guardrail when storage backend is NFS + high latency.

Neha Kapoor: SRE take: we’re close, but we need deterministic gates. The restore command should either:
1) complete successfully and produce a validation report, or
2) fail fast with actionable prereqs.
Not “hang and hope.”

IncidentBot: (note) Game day summary posted by @rafael.mendes. Please file follow-up items in Linear and link here.

Rafael Mendes: Concrete breakages we saw:
1) Restore preflight didn’t check CRDs presence/version -> restore job attempted to apply custom resources first and died.
2) KMS provider retry loop had no cap; noisy logs; operator didn’t know if safe to wait.
3) Validation step had one false negative (health endpoint returned 503 for ~90s during migrations; we treated it as hard fail).
4) Runbook lacked “clean cluster bootstrap order” (CRDs, namespaces, RBAC, then restore).

Sean Gallagher: Also: RBAC in the restore Job is still broader than necessary. We’re using cluster-admin in staging for convenience. That’s not shippable.

Ethan Park: On (1), quick fix is to make `restore apply` do:
- verify required CRDs exist
- verify required namespaces exist
- verify controller deployments are at expected version
- ONLY THEN apply CRs / state

Michael Nguyen: On (3) — validation should treat transient 503 as “retryable” during migration window. We can key off a “migrations_in_progress=true” marker or the restore phase state.

Sergio Costa: If we emit `redwood_private_restore_phase{phase="migrate"}` and `...{phase="validate"}` it’ll be easy to tune alerts + dashboards.

Allison Grant: Please add error taxonomy for restore failures (preflight_failed, decrypt_failed, apply_failed, migrate_failed, validate_failed) so SRE can page the right team.

Rafael Mendes: Immediate action items (please react/confirm owners):
- [ENG] Add CRD + prereq checks to restore preflight + clear error message. Owner: @benji.okafor / reviewer @ethan.park. Target: EOW.
- [SEC] Fix KMS retry policy (bounded exponential backoff, timeout, operator hint). Owner: @hanae.suzuki w/ @marcus.lin review. Target: EOW.
- [OBS] Emit per-phase restore metrics + spans; update dashboard panels to show “current phase” + duration. Owner: @sergio.costa / @allison.grant. Target: next sprint.
- [SRE] Update runbook to include clean-cluster bootstrap order + rollback/abort criteria. Owner: @sean.gallagher. Target: 48h.
- [REL] Ensure offline bundle includes CRDs/prereqs + version compatibility note. Owner: @connor.o'brien. Target: next release cut.
- [ENG] Make validation tolerant to transient 5xx during migrations; add max wait + final report. Owner: @michael.nguyen. Target: next sprint.
- [SRE/ENG] Reduce restore job RBAC scope (least privilege) + document required permissions. Owner: @sean.gallagher + @bruno.silva. Target: next sprint.

Benji Okafor: :white_check_mark: I’ll take the CRD/preflight work. I’ll link it to ENG-9822 (restore apply) and/or open a new subtask.

Ethan Park: I can review + help with the bootstrap ordering logic. Let’s make sure the error message points to the exact missing CRD names.

Hanae Suzuki: :white_check_mark: Taking KMS retry changes. I’ll sync w/ Marcus on what we can safely log (key alias vs full ARN).

Marcus Lin: Sounds good. Also please ensure we don’t write decrypted material to disk anywhere outside the tmpdir w/ restrictive perms.

Sergio Costa: :white_check_mark: I’ll add phase metrics + update the mock dashboard. Will also add a “restore stalled > X min in same phase” alert suggestion.

Allison Grant: I’ll review metric names/labels. Keep cardinality low; no tenant IDs in labels.

Sean Gallagher: :white_check_mark: Runbook update on me. I’ll add an explicit “Stop / escalate” section + Slack escalation path.

Connor O'Brien: :white_check_mark: I’ll make sure CRDs/prereqs are in the air-gapped bundle and checksums get updated. Also adding a release checklist item so we don’t regress.

Michael Nguyen: :white_check_mark: Will adjust validation + add retry window. I’ll also add a final report section that includes migration state + versions.

Bruno Silva: I can pair w/ Sean on RBAC tightening. We should ship a minimal ClusterRole for restore and not rely on cluster-admin.

Neha Kapoor: Please also capture “what would have reduced RTO” as a bullet list for exec readout. Biggest wins seem to be preflight + better progress reporting.

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Exec-friendly summary:
- RTO miss was mostly “operator friction” (missing prereqs + unclear progress), not data loss.
- Fixes are straightforward and will make the process deterministic + supportable.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll drop links to Linear tickets once created. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-sre: 1739093307
preview:
Miles Thornton: posting draft SLO proposal for provision-to-ready (fleet bring-up). want SRE eyes before we lock thresholds + wire alerts. doc is here: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/eng-sre/Provision-to-ready+SLO+draft (WIP)

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal
- SLI: time_to_ready = ts(Ready) - ts(Allocated)
- SLO (steady state): P50 <= 25m, P95 <= 90m (per region + gpu_type), and success rate >= 98.5% (Ready within 2h)
- error budget: 30d window, standard burn rate alerts
- alerting: page for sustained burn (fast + slow), ticket for regional / gpu-specific regression

Aisha Bello: thanks. 2 q’s:
1) what’s the exact “Allocated” timestamp source of truth? terraform apply? cloud instance running? control plane “capacity allocated”? we’ve had weird gaps there.
2) pls don’t page on P50 drifting during rollout week. it’s going to be noisy.

Miles Thornton: 1) good call. draft uses “fleet-manager allocation event” (control plane) as Allocated. not cloud provider state.
2) agree on rollout. thinking “rollout mode” alert policy (ticket only) until we’re past us-east A100.

Rafael Mendes: +1 on rollout mode. I’d rather page only on “hard down / stuck pipeline” not “performance drift” until flake rate stabilizes.

Rafael Mendes: also we should be careful with labels. per region+gpu_type is probably fine but *not* per pool_id / node_id in alert rules.

Allison Grant: yes pls. we already had one incident from tag explosion on another pipeline. keep cardinality tight. region, gpu_sku, step maybe ok. pool_id should be dashboard-only.

Vanessa Ortiz: from Infra side: baseline P95 right now is ~3.5h on some A10 regions (manual + retries). so P95 90m as steady state is aggressive but aligns w/ sprint goal. can we phrase as “target SLO” with staged ramp?

Miles Thornton: yep. doc has “initial objective” and “steady state SLO”. but we should make it explicit.

Aisha Bello: can we define 2 SLIs?
- “pipeline success rate” (node reaches Ready within X)
- “time-to-ready” distribution
Then page on success-rate (it indicates stuck/broken), ticket on time-to-ready regressions.

Miles Thornton: that’s basically what I was aiming at with success>=98.5%, and latency as P50/P95. but I’m ok tightening: page only if success rate burn.

Sean Gallagher: jumping in. I’m fine with ambitious targets, but paging needs to be conservative early.
Proposal:
- rollout weeks: no pages for latency SLO. ticket only.
- pages only if success-rate SLO is burning fast *and* impacting capacity (quarantine spike / backlog of Allocated).
- after rollout stabilizes (2+ weeks), enable latency pages w/ burn-rate.

Aisha Bello: +1. also make sure we have a separate “stuck in Validating/Provisioning > N minutes” symptom alert. those are actionable for oncall.

Sergio Costa: re: alert math. For burn rates, I can implement standard multiwindow multi-burn (5m/1h fast, 30m/6h slow). But we need the SLO definition crisp.
If SLO is “98.5% ready within 2h”, then the SLI is basically a ratio of successes over total allocations. That’s pretty clean.

Miles Thornton: agreed. will set as:
- Success SLO: 98.5% of nodes transition Allocated->Ready within 2h (rolling 30d)
- Latency objective: P50 <= 25m, P95 <= 90m (non-paging initially)

Rafael Mendes: for paging threshold, can we set:
- page if fast-burn > 14.4x for 5m/1h (classic) AND at least 20 nodes in window (avoid small-sample paging)
- otherwise ticket

Allison Grant: +1 on minimum event count. also add “dedicated vs shared” dimension? dedicated issues are customer-visible.

Vanessa Ortiz: dedicated vs shared: yes but careful - pool_id too high-card. we can label pool_type={dedicated,shared} and maybe customer_tier? but that might also explode.

Aisha Bello: pool_type is fine. customer tier probably not. we can route notifications based on pool_type at the alert level.

Sergio Costa: noted. I’ll keep dimensions to: env, region, gpu_type, pool_type.

Ethan Park: quick q - for ts(Ready), are we using readiness service write as authoritative? (not kubelet ready)

Miles Thornton: yes. Ready = readiness service state == Ready (control plane write), which already incorporates validations.

Ethan Park: ok. then we should log an audit trail for transitions; otherwise debugging the SLI is painful.

Miles Thornton: yep, control plane emitting events per transition is part of ENG-4822. SLO-toolkit can compute from those.

Sean Gallagher: decision:
- adopt success-rate SLO now (with rollout-mode alerting)
- latency objective dashboards now; page later
- paging: only success-rate fast burn + min sample + capacity impact signal

Aisha Bello: capacity impact signal = what metric? quarantine_count? backlog_allocated_not_ready?

Sergio Costa: we can do:
- `fleet_readiness_state{state="Allocated"}` minus `state="Ready"` as backlog gauge
- `fleet_readiness_state{state="Quarantined"}` as spike
But for alerts, I’d prefer a simple condition: success-rate burn + backlog > threshold (e.g., > 50 nodes) to page.

Rafael Mendes: works. during us-east A100 rollout the numbers might be small though. maybe threshold as % of expected bring-up? hmm.

Vanessa Ortiz: bring-up sizes vary. could we page on backlog growth rate instead? like backlog increasing for 30m.

Allison Grant: careful on rates and noisy signals. backlog > N is easier.

Miles Thornton: compromise:
- page condition A: fast burn on success SLO (multiwindow)
- page condition B: backlog > 25 for 30m OR quarantined > 10 for 30m (per region/pool_type)
- plus min sample >= 20 allocations in the eval window

Aisha Bello: ok. and add a “rollout_mode=true” feature flag label or separate alert route that downgrades to ticket.

Sergio Costa: I can implement alert rules with a config toggle (values file) for rollout mode. We’ll start ticket-only for latency and ticket-only for success rate unless backlog/quarantine thresholds hit.

Sean Gallagher: please write this up in the doc + link to the Linear ticket so it’s traceable.

Miles Thornton: will update doc and ENG-4821 w/ the agreed thresholds + rollout mode plan.

Aisha Bello: last thing: add an explicit note that we’re not paging on P50/P95 drift because “time-to-ready” is multi-modal and will improve stepwise as automation lands.

Miles Thornton: done. thanks all.

Sergio Costa: I’ll draft the alert rules and post for review later today.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: works. during us-east A100 rollout the numbers might be small though. maybe threshold as % of expected bring-up? hmm.

Vanessa Ortiz: bring-up sizes vary. could we page on backlog growth rate instead? like backlog increasing for 30m.

Allison Grant: careful on rates and noisy signals. backlog > N is easier.

Miles Thornton: compromise:
- page condition A: fast burn on success SLO (multiwindow)
- page condition B: backlog > 25 for 30m OR quarantined > 10 for 30m (per region/pool_type)
- plus min sample >= 20 allocations in the eval window

Aisha Bello: ok. and add a “rollout_mode=true” feature flag label or separate alert route that downgrades to ticket.

Sergio Costa: I can implement alert rules with a config toggle (values file) for rollout mode. We’ll start ticket-only for latency and ticket-only for success rate unless backlog/quarantine thresholds hit.

Sean Gallagher: please write this up in the doc + link to the Linear ticket so it’s traceable.

Miles Thornton: will update doc and ENG-4821 w/ the agreed thresholds + rollout mode plan.

Aisha Bello: last thing: add an explicit note that we’re not paging on P50/P95 drift because “time-to-ready” is multi-modal and will improve stepwise as automation lands.

Miles Thornton: done. thanks all.

Sergio Costa: I’ll draft the alert rules and post for review later today.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup:

title: #eng-sre: 1738890055
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Quick runbook review request before we merge the staged rollouts ops doc. Specifically the override steps (force-promote / hold / abort / force-rollback), audit reqs, and what evidence we expect folks to capture for postmortems. Threading here so we can converge.

Rafael Mendes: Current runbook section draft (internal link): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Runbook+-+Dedicated+Staged+Rollouts#Overrides

Sean Gallagher: Reading now. Main thing: we need to be explicit about when override is allowed (severity + who approves) and what “evidence” means. Otherwise oncall will click force-promote to clear a page.

Peter Holtz: +1. Also we should align language with “fail-closed by default on missing metrics” so operators know why the UI is blocking and what manual verification is acceptable.

Ethan Park: From control plane side: override actions are fully audited (new event types in audit log). But runbook should tell folks to include the audit_event_id in the incident timeline.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For security/compliance: runbook must say overrides require admin role (RBAC: `dedicated_rollouts.override`). And every override must include a `reason` string. If reason is blank we should treat it as non-compliant.

Allison Grant: Evidence capture: please don’t ask for “screenshots from Grafana” as the only thing. We should capture query links + time window and the stage_id labels. Otherwise we can’t reproduce.

Logan Wright: +1. Telemetry side: best is to capture the gate evidence payload returned by Console backend (it includes query template + observed values). That’s the durable artifact.

Connor O'Brien: Release eng angle: runbook should instruct to pause promotions during deploy windows when telemetry lag is expected. Otherwise you’ll get a bunch of “unknown” gates and people will override.

Talia Benson: Can someone propose the exact checklist text? I can paste it into the runbook doc after we align here.

Irene Choi: Also for Dedicated: add an explicit warning about small pools. Force-promote from 1% -> 10% can be a *real* capacity change if that maps to replica partitions. Evidence should include “pool size / headroom before and after.”

Rafael Mendes: Ok, let’s go line-by-line on override types:
1) HOLD (pause in current stage)
2) ABORT (rollback to previous stable)
3) FORCE-PROMOTE (advance stage despite gate)
4) FORCE-ROLLBACK (rollback even if gates passing)

Rafael Mendes: Today the runbook says “Override requires SRE oncall approval.” Is that enough?

Sean Gallagher: Not enough. We need: (a) when it’s acceptable, (b) who is allowed to do it, (c) which channel to document approval.

Sean Gallagher: Proposal:
- HOLD: allowed by oncall to stop blast radius while investigating. No approval needed beyond oncall, but must log reason.
- ABORT: allowed by oncall if gate fails OR incident ongoing. No extra approval, but must create incident/ticket.
- FORCE-PROMOTE: requires (1) SRE oncall + (2) Dedicated PM or Eng owner ack in #eng-platform or incident channel.
- FORCE-ROLLBACK: allowed by oncall for mitigation, but if customer-facing, page Dedicated/Platform owner.

Peter Holtz: +1. Make it “two-person rule” for force-promote.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: If we say “PM ack” we should also specify acceptable approvers: “Platform Eng Manager/Director oncall equivalent” etc. PMs don’t always have admin perms anyway.

Ethan Park: In practice, `force_promote` is guarded by admin. That’s usually SRE oncall + one of: Platform oncall, Dedicated oncall, or Eng sponsor. PM can ACK but not execute.

Rafael Mendes: Makes sense. Re audit requirements: what do we need to say in runbook?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Minimum:
- All overrides must be executed via Console or API with authenticated user (no shared service token).
- Must include reason.
- Must link to incident/ticket id in reason (or separate field if UI supports).
- Must verify audit event emitted and visible in audit trail.

Allison Grant: Also capture “gate state at time of override.” Eg: “error_rate gate failing due to missing data” is different than “error_rate gate failing due to 3% 5xx.”

Logan Wright: Gate evidence payload includes a `status_reason` and an `evaluation_window`. Tell folks to paste it into incident notes.

Rafael Mendes: Can we include a snippet for API override (for cases when Console is broken)?

Ethan Park: Yes. Current endpoints:
- POST `/v1/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollouts/{rollout_id}:hold`
- POST `/v1/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollouts/{rollout_id}:promote`
- POST `/v1/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollouts/{rollout_id}:abort`
- POST `/v1/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollouts/{rollout_id}:force_promote`
- POST `/v1/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollouts/{rollout_id}:force_rollback`

Ethan Park: Recommend including `Idempotency-Key` header. Also `If-Match` with rollout `etag` if you have it (avoid double-promote).

Peter Holtz: Please include the warning: “DO NOT retry force_promote without checking current stage; it’s stage-advancing.”

Rafael Mendes: Here’s a runbook-friendly example:

Rafael Mendes: 
```bash
# Force-promote to next stage (admin only)
export POOL_ID=dp_123
export ROLLOUT_ID=drp_456
export IDEMP=inc-88031-forcepromote-1

curl -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Idempotency-Key: $IDEMP" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  "https://api.redwood.ai/v1/dedicated/pools/$POOL_ID/rollouts/$ROLLOUT_ID:force_promote" \
  -d '{"reason":"INC-88031: gate unknown due to telemetry lag; manually verified p95 < 900ms + 5xx < 0.5% in stage=canary over last 20m; approved by @platform-oncall"}'
```

Connor O'Brien: Add: “If telemetry lag suspected, check ingest lag dashboard before overriding.” (We had this exact issue during beta.)

Allison Grant: +1 and include the canonical link. For runbook, reference: “Telemetry Ingest Lag (prod)” dashboard.

Logan Wright: Also add: don’t use raw stage_id in ad-hoc metrics queries unless you’re using the allowlisted label set. We had cardinality regressions. But for evidence capture, the console evidence payload is safe.

Sean Gallagher: On postmortem evidence: can we standardize what to attach?

Sean Gallagher: I’d like a checklist block:
- Rollout identifiers: pool_id, rollout_id, current_stage, target_stage, regions.
- Gate snapshot: evidence payload OR links to the exact dashboard panels (with time range).
- Capacity snapshot: reserved replicas, headroom %, GPU health summary, queue depth.
- Action: override type, actor, time, reason, approval ack.
- Result: impact (errors/latency/429), whether rollback needed.

Irene Choi: Include “partition sizing before/after.” Specifically for replica-based partitions: canary partition replicas count.

Peter Holtz: And “customer traffic shape.” If it’s bursty, a 5m window might be meaningless; for override we often use 15-30m manual check.

Rafael Mendes: Ok. One debate: hold vs abort guidance. For gate fail, do we say “abort immediately” or “hold and investigate”?

Sean Gallagher: Default should be HOLD first (stop progress) unless it’s clearly damaging. ABORT if customer impact rising or if the gate failing is a hard safety signal (OOM spike, 429 surge, GPU unhealthy).

Peter Holtz: Agree. We should call out the “hard gates” that imply likely ongoing harm.

Allison Grant: Let’s list examples:
- hard: OOM rate, GPU health (ECC / node taints), 429/request drops, error rate above threshold
- soft/unknown: missing data, low sample size, borderline p95

Logan Wright: Missing data should be treated as unknown; system is fail-closed. Override path must require manual verification + acknowledgement of telemetry incident.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: And if it’s telemetry incident, still require reason includes “telemetry issue id” (ticket link). That’ll matter for audits.

Connor O'Brien: Also add: “Do not force-promote during active incident unless the goal is to *reduce* risk (e.g., promote off a bad old build).” Otherwise it becomes change-on-incident.

Ethan Park: FYI: force-rollback today reverts traffic split to previous stage and marks rollout “held” (not “aborted”) so we preserve the plan state for review. Runbook should match that semantics.

Rafael Mendes: Good catch. I’ll update language: force-rollback transitions -> HELD with rollback marker, abort -> ABORTED.

Talia Benson: I can draft the final runbook text block. Please confirm these decisions:
1) Two-person rule for force-promote.
2) Every override includes reason + incident/ticket.
3) Evidence: paste gate evidence payload or dashboard links w/ time range + stage.
4) Prefer HOLD on gate fail unless hard-signal/customer impact.

Sean Gallagher: Yes.

Peter Holtz: Yes.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yes, and explicitly say RBAC permission name.

Allison Grant: Yes, include “query links/time window” guidance.

Logan Wright: Yes, and mention ingest lag check before override.

Irene Choi: Yes, plus capacity/partition snapshot.

Connor O'Brien: Yes.

Rafael Mendes: Great. One more: do we want “force-promote visibility to customer admins” called out? It’s in audit logs.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: We should note: customer admins with audit access can see it. So reasons should be customer-safe (no internal-only info), but still descriptive.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Don’t put “telemetry is broken again lol” in reason.

Rafael Mendes: lol. Adding explicit guidance: “Reasons are customer-visible; keep professional.”

Talia Benson: I’ll update the runbook doc today and tag @Rafael Mendes + @Kaitlyn Nguyen for final review.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: please also add a short “Postmortem evidence” template block so folks can copy/paste into incident doc.

Talia Benson: will do.

Allison Grant: When you add the template, include a field for “gate evaluation window” and “sample size” if shown.

Logan Wright: + field for “telemetry freshness / ingest lag at time of decision.”

Ethan Park: And include “audit_event_id” + “idempotency_key” (if API used) so we can trace retries.

Rafael Mendes: Perfect. Closing the loop here once the runbook is updated.

title: #eng-sre: 1738873007
preview:
Sean Gallagher: we need to land the dashboard annotations piece for safe apply/rollback. right now it’s hard to tell “did p95 move b/c rollout vs organic.”

Sean Gallagher: question: what’s the best way to annotate apply/promote/rollback events and also tag metrics w/ `config_version` so the gate eval + oncall dashboards can slice.

Allison Grant: +1. for grafana, annotations can come from (a) grafana annotations API, (b) loki log query, (c) prometheus “event” metrics (hacky). i’d prefer loki because we already have audit logs flowing there.

Aisha Bello: agree. also re: metric tags: we have to be careful with cardinality. `tenant/model/region` already heavy. adding `config_version` everywhere might explode series.

Benji Okafor: on control plane side we’ll emit audit events for apply/promote/rollback w/ version id + rollout id. can add whatever fields you need for annotations.

Sean Gallagher: ok. for the dashboard: i want vertical lines with text like `apply ocv_123 scope=tenant:acme model=llama-3.1-70b region=us-east-1 canary=10%` and then `rollback to ocv_120`.

Allison Grant: if audit events land in loki as JSON, we can annotate with a loki query like:
```
{app="audit-log"} |= "optimization_config" | json | action =~ "apply|rollback|promote"
```
Grafana can render labels as the annotation text.

Noah Patel: runtime metric tagging: we *can* attach `config_version` at emit time because workers know their currently applied version. but if we add it to every request metric it’s a lot.

Aisha Bello: gating needs p95/p99 + 5xx *by config_version*, at least during a rollout. do we need it 24/7?

Sean Gallagher: ideally the “rollout health” dashboard should show canary vs baseline for the same model/region, so yes during rollout. outside rollout, not important.

Allison Grant: compromise: add `config_version` label only on the “gate metrics” we use in the rollout controller:
- `redwood_inference_requests_total`
- `redwood_inference_request_errors_total` (or status class)
- `redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket`
but *only* for traffic that is under a rollout policy (i.e. requests routed via canary controller). everything else can omit/blank label.

Noah Patel: we can do that by having the router/runtime set `config_version` label only when header present. e.g. router injects `x-redwood-opt-config-version` for canary traffic, and runtime plumbs it into metrics context.

Benji Okafor: we already planned to add `config_version` to response headers optionally for debugging (openai compat PR). router can inject too.

Felix Schneider: from Console side: we need annotations to show in our embedded dashboard panels. if annotations come from loki query, that should just work.

Allison Grant: yep. but we need consistent fields in the audit log payload so the annotation text isn’t garbage.

Allison Grant: proposal for audit fields used by dashboards:
- `event_name`: `optimization_config.apply|promote|rollback`
- `config_version`: `ocv_...`
- `previous_config_version`: `ocv_...` (esp for rollback)
- `rollout_id`: `orc_...`
- `scope_tenant`, `scope_model`, `scope_region`
- `actor` (user/service)
- `result`: `started|paused|completed|failed` (or separate events)

Aisha Bello: +1 on `result`. otherwise we’ll annotate “apply” but it might have failed validation and never hit runtime.

Benji Okafor: we can emit:
1) `...apply_requested`
2) `...apply_started`
3) `...apply_completed`
4) `...apply_failed`
Same for rollback. should be easy.

Sean Gallagher: pls do, but we should decide which ones become dashboard annotations. too many lines = unreadable.

Allison Grant: annotate only “started” and “completed/failed” maybe. requested is noisy.

Noah Patel: one nuance: rollback is async. you can “request rollback” and runtime converges over ~N minutes. we should annotate the request time + “converged” time.

Aisha Bello: yes, because alert noise happens during convergence window.

Sean Gallagher: metric tagging details: do we tag by `config_version` only, or also `rollout_id`?

Allison Grant: avoid `rollout_id` label. it’s basically unbounded. `config_version` is bounded-ish (few concurrent versions per tenant/model/region). and we can always correlate rollout_id via logs/annotations.

Aisha Bello: what about the case where the same config version is applied to two scopes at same time? `config_version` alone isn’t enough.

Allison Grant: gate queries will include the existing scope labels:
`tenant`, `model`, `region`, plus `config_version`.
We already need those anyway for the controller.

Noah Patel: cardinality concern remains for `tenant`. do we really tag tenant on latency buckets? that’s massive.

Sean Gallagher: for Hosted it’s already there on some metrics (at least for internal). for customer-facing grafana we filter to a tenant. but prometheus still stores it.

Allison Grant: for the rollout health dashboard, we can do:
- internal prom (full labels) used by controller + SRE
- customer dashboards: either pre-aggregated or using Mimir w/ tenant isolation
But for now, to ship, we keep tenant label because controller needs per-tenant gating.

Aisha Bello: ok but can we at least make `config_version` label appear only when rollout active, so series count is limited?

Noah Patel: yes. implement as:
- default metrics: no `config_version` label (or `config_version=""`)
- during rollout-routed requests: set to actual `ocv_...`
That creates extra series but only for the rollout slice.

Allison Grant: in PromQL we can then compare baseline vs canary:
```
histogram_quantile(0.95,
  sum by (le, model, region, config_version) (
    rate(redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket{tenant="$tenant", model="$model", region="$region"}[5m])
  )
)
```
Baseline is `config_version=""` (or `"stable"`), canary is `ocv_...`.

Sean Gallagher: i’d rather use `config_version="stable"` than empty string. empty is annoying in legends.

Noah Patel: we can set stable = `"active"`? but that’s confusing.

Allison Grant: use `config_version="baseline"` for non-rollout traffic. explicit.

Benji Okafor: makes sense. controller can treat baseline as “whatever was active before rollout started.” but note baseline may still be a real version. how do we surface that?

Sean Gallagher: good point. we need the actual baseline version id somewhere.

Allison Grant: two options:
1) baseline traffic label is the actual version id (then you always have it)
2) baseline label is `baseline` and you join with an “active version” gauge metric.
I prefer (1): runtime always labels with its current applied config version (real id). Then canary and baseline are just different version ids.

Aisha Bello: doesn’t that reintroduce cardinality 24/7? because config_version is always set.

Noah Patel: yep. if every request metric has config_version always set, we store series per version even when not rolling out. still probably not terrible, but it accumulates over time.

Allison Grant: prom retention is 30d. versions older than 30d fall out. but series churn still matters.

Sean Gallagher: we need to be conservative. plan:
- only tag with real `config_version` during rollout-routed traffic
- for everything else, tag `config_version="stable"`
- ALSO emit a separate low-cardinality gauge:
  `redwood_optimization_config_active{tenant, model, region} 1` with label `config_version="ocv_..."`
This gives us baseline version id without tagging all request metrics.

Allison Grant: +1, that’s clean. grafana can show a stat panel “Active config version” via that gauge, and annotations show apply/rollback.

Benji Okafor: control plane can publish the active version metric too? or runtime?

Noah Patel: runtime is source of truth for what’s applied. but control plane knows intended target. i’d rather have runtime emit `..._active` so it reflects convergence.

Aisha Bello: and alert if intended != applied for > X min.

Sean Gallagher: ok action items:
- @Benji Okafor add audit event fields so we can annotate from loki; include started+completed+failed+converged
- @Noah Patel implement `config_version` metric label only for rollout traffic + emit `redwood_optimization_config_active` gauge w/ actual ocv
- @Allison Grant update dashboard + panels + annotation query in observability-pack; add note about promql

incident-bot: reminder: runbook link for safe apply/rollback dashboards (draft) will be added to #eng-oncall once merged.

Felix Schneider: once we have the audit fields locked, Console can also show them in history timeline (same payload). pls don’t rename fields later :)

Benji Okafor: ack. will align with security audit taxonomy too (Marcus asked for `actor`, `request_id`, `reason`).

Sean Gallagher: cool. i’ll mark this thread as the decision: annotations from loki audit logs; metric tagging limited; add active gauge. :thumbsup:
text:
Sean Gallagher: we need to land the dashboard annotations piece for safe apply/rollback. right now it’s hard to tell “did p95 move b/c rollout vs organic.”

Sean Gallagher: question: what’s the best way to annotate apply/promote/rollback events and also tag metrics w/ `config_version` so the gate eval + oncall dashboards can slice.

Allison Grant: +1. for grafana, annotations can come from (a) grafana annotations API, (b) loki log query, (c) prometheus “event” metrics (hacky). i’d prefer loki because we already have audit logs flowing there.

Aisha Bello: agree. also re: metric tags: we have to be careful with cardinality. `tenant/model/region` already heavy. adding `config_version` everywhere might explode series.

Benji Okafor: on control plane side we’ll emit audit events for apply/promote/rollback w/ version id + rollout id. can add whatever fields you need for annotations.

Sean Gallagher: ok. for the dashboard: i want vertical lines with text like `apply ocv_123 scope=tenant:acme model=llama-3.1-70b region=us-east-1 canary=10%` and then `rollback to ocv_120`.

Allison Grant: if audit events land in loki as JSON, we can annotate with a loki query like:
```
{app="audit-log"} |= "optimization_config" | json | action =~ "apply|rollback|promote"
```
Grafana can render labels as the annotation text.

Noah Patel: runtime metric tagging: we *can* attach `config_version` at emit time because workers know their currently applied version. but if we add it to every request metric it’s a lot.

Aisha Bello: gating needs p95/p99 + 5xx *by config_version*, at least during a rollout. do we need it 24/7?

Sean Gallagher: ideally the “rollout health” dashboard should show canary vs baseline for the same model/region, so yes during rollout. outside rollout, not important.

Allison Grant: compromise: add `config_version` label only on the “gate metrics” we use in the rollout controller:
- `redwood_inference_requests_total`
- `redwood_inference_request_errors_total` (or status class)
- `redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket`
but *only* for traffic that is under a rollout policy (i.e. requests routed via canary controller). everything else can omit/blank label.

Noah Patel: we can do that by having the router/runtime set `config_version` label only when header present. e.g. router injects `x-redwood-opt-config-version` for canary traffic, and runtime plumbs it into metrics context.

Benji Okafor: we already planned to add `config_version` to response headers optionally for debugging (openai compat PR). router can inject too.

Felix Schneider: from Console side: we need annotations to show in our embedded dashboard panels. if annotations come from loki query, that should just work.

Allison Grant: yep. but we need consistent fields in the audit log payload so the annotation text isn’t garbage.

Allison Grant: proposal for audit fields used by dashboards:
- `event_name`: `optimization_config.apply|promote|rollback`
- `config_version`: `ocv_...`
- `previous_config_version`: `ocv_...` (esp for rollback)
- `rollout_id`: `orc_...`
- `scope_tenant`, `scope_model`, `scope_region`
- `actor` (user/service)
- `result`: `started|paused|completed|failed` (or separate events)

Aisha Bello: +1 on `result`. otherwise we’ll annotate “apply” but it might have failed validation and never hit runtime.

Benji Okafor: we can emit:
1) `...apply_requested`
2) `...apply_started`
3) `...apply_completed`
4) `...apply_failed`
Same for rollback. should be easy.

Sean Gallagher: pls do, but we should decide which ones become dashboard annotations. too many lines = unreadable.

Allison Grant: annotate only “started” and “completed/failed” maybe. requested is noisy.

Noah Patel: one nuance: rollback is async. you can “request rollback” and runtime converges over ~N minutes. we should annotate the request time + “converged” time.

Aisha Bello: yes, because alert noise happens during convergence window.

Sean Gallagher: metric tagging details: do we tag by `config_version` only, or also `rollout_id`?

Allison Grant: avoid `rollout_id` label. it’s basically unbounded. `config_version` is bounded-ish (few concurrent versions per tenant/model/region). and we can always correlate rollout_id via logs/annotations.

Aisha Bello: what about the case where the same config version is applied to two scopes at same time? `config_version` alone isn’t enough.

Allison Grant: gate queries will include the existing scope labels:
`tenant`, `model`, `region`, plus `config_version`.
We already need those anyway for the controller.

Noah Patel: cardinality concern remains for `tenant`. do we really tag tenant on latency buckets? that’s massive.

Sean Gallagher: for Hosted it’s already there on some metrics (at least for internal). for customer-facing grafana we filter to a tenant. but prometheus still stores it.

Allison Grant: for the rollout health dashboard, we can do:
- internal prom (full labels) used by controller + SRE
- customer dashboards: either pre-aggregated or using Mimir w/ tenant isolation
But for now, to ship, we keep tenant label because controller needs per-tenant gating.

Aisha Bello: ok but can we at least make `config_version` label appear only when rollout active, so series count is limited?

Noah Patel: yes. implement as:
- default metrics: no `config_version` label (or `config_version=""`)
- during rollout-routed requests: set to actual `ocv_...`
That creates extra series but only for the rollout slice.

Allison Grant: in PromQL we can then compare baseline vs canary:
```
histogram_quantile(0.95,
  sum by (le, model, region, config_version) (
    rate(redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket{tenant="$tenant", model="$model", region="$region"}[5m])
  )
)
```
Baseline is `config_version=""` (or `"stable"`), canary is `ocv_...`.

Sean Gallagher: i’d rather use `config_version="stable"` than empty string. empty is annoying in legends.

Noah Patel: we can set stable = `"active"`? but that’s confusing.

Allison Grant: use `config_version="baseline"` for non-rollout traffic. explicit.

Benji Okafor: makes sense. controller can treat baseline as “whatever was active before rollout started.” but note baseline may still be a real version. how do we surface that?

Sean Gallagher: good point. we need the actual baseline version id somewhere.

Allison Grant: two options:
1) baseline traffic label is the actual version id (then you always have it)
2) baseline label is `baseline` and you join with an “active version” gauge metric.
I prefer (1): runtime always labels with its current applied config version (real id). Then canary and baseline are just different version ids.

Aisha Bello: doesn’t that reintroduce cardinality 24/7? because config_version is always set.

Noah Patel: yep. if every request metric has config_version always set, we store series per version even when not rolling out. still probably not terrible, but it accumulates over time.

Allison Grant: prom retention is 30d. versions older than 30d fall out. but series churn still matters.

Sean Gallagher: we need to be conservative. plan:
- only tag with real `config_version` during rollout-routed traffic
- for everything else, tag `config_version="stable"`
- ALSO emit a separate low-cardinality gauge:
  `redwood_optimization_config_active{tenant, model, region} 1` with label `config_version="ocv_..."`
This gives us baseline version id without tagging all request metrics.

Allison Grant: +1, that’s clean. grafana can show a stat panel “Active config version” via that gauge, and annotations show apply/rollback.

Benji Okafor: control plane can publish the active version metric too? or runtime?

Noah Patel: runtime is source of truth for what’s applied. but control plane knows intended target. i’d rather have runtime emit `..._active` so it reflects convergence.

Aisha Bello: and alert if intended != applied for > X min.

Sean Gallagher: ok action items:
- @Benji Okafor add audit event fields so we can annotate from loki; include started+completed+failed+converged
- @Noah Patel implement `config_version` metric label only for rollout traffic + emit `redwood_optimization_config_active` gauge w/ actual ocv
- @Allison Grant update dashboard + panels + annotation query in observability-pack; add note about promql

incident-bot: reminder: runbook link for safe apply/rollback dashboards (draft) will be added to #eng-oncall once merged.

Felix Schneider: once we have the audit fields locked, Console can also show them in history timeline (same payload). pls don’t rename fields later :)

Benji Okafor: ack. will align with security audit taxonomy too (Marcus asked for `actor`, `request_id`, `reason`).

Sean Gallagher: cool. i’ll mark this thread as the decision: annotations from loki audit logs; metric tagging limited; add active gauge. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-sre: 1738870108
preview:
Aisha Bello: can we add audit pipeline graphs to the *standard* on-call dashboard? right now it’s buried in the audit-logging dashboard and folks miss it during incidents

Sean Gallagher: +1. if it’s going to be on the standard, we should keep it small + actionable. what panels do you want?

Aisha Bello: thinking a small “Audit pipeline” row with 4 panels:
- ingest end-to-end lag (p50/p95)
- drop rate (validated events vs received)
- exporter retries / send failures
- backlog (buffer depth / queue age)

Rafael Mendes: please also add links in the row header:
- runbook
- postmortem template
we keep re-litigating “where’s the PM doc” when things are on fire

Kevin Osei: metrics exist but naming is a mess across hosted vs private exporter.
for hosted ingestion we have:
- `audit_ingest_lag_seconds{env,region}`
- `audit_events_dropped_total{reason}`
- `audit_ingest_queue_depth{shard}`
exporter has:
- `audit_export_send_failures_total{target}`
- `audit_export_retry_queue_depth{target}`

Allison Grant: for standard dashboard, please avoid shard as a dimension (cardinality). aggregate to total depth + max age if possible.
also: make sure we tag panels with `service=audit` so they show up in the global search

Sean Gallagher: agree w/ Allison. also “drop rate” panel should show both absolute dropped + %.
we should annotate with deploy markers, esp since exporter retries can spike after config changes

Logan Wright: small ask: can you also add a single-stat for “schema validation failures” (events rejected b/c schema mismatch)? that’s what usually triggers Support escalations

Kevin Osei: yep, we have `audit_events_rejected_total{reason=schema_validation}` (and `reason=missing_required_field`, etc). could roll those into one panel

Aisha Bello: ok updated row:
1) E2E delivery lag (ingest -> sink) p50/p95
2) Drop/reject % (dropped + rejected) w/ breakdown by reason in tooltip
3) Backlog (queue depth + oldest age)
4) Exporter send failures + retries (by target but capped to top 5)

Rafael Mendes: link for postmortem template should be the standard SRE one, not audit-specific. we just add “Audit logging” as affected system
Confluence: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENG/SRE+Postmortem+Template+v2

Sean Gallagher: runbook link should go to the audit pipeline incident runbook (the one with backfill steps + customer comms snippets)

Aisha Bello: yep: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENG/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook

Allison Grant: reminder: if we’re adding a row to the standard on-call dashboard, we should also link the postmortem template *and* the “incident channel checklist” (optional). but at minimum PM template is good

Sean Gallagher: let’s not bloat it. runbook + PM template is fine.
Aisha, can you make the PR to observability-pack? I’ll review today.

Aisha Bello: will do. target is the “Oncall - Core Platform” grafana dashboard (the one new hires get pinned)

Grafana Bot: Aisha Bello updated dashboard “Oncall - Core Platform” (uid: oncall-core-platform) in folder “Oncall”.

Kevin Osei: heads up: private exporter failures won’t show in hosted env unless we plumb the private cluster metrics into the same datasource. but for now, this at least helps for hosted/dedicated + internal dogfooding

Rafael Mendes: that’s fine. add a small note in the panel description: “Private: check cluster-local dashboard” so oncall doesn’t assume green == everything green

Sean Gallagher: +1. also, can we add the postmortem template link into the incident bot message template? (separate task)

Aisha Bello: I’ll open a follow-up for incident bot template update. for this thread: dashboard row + links shipping today.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once merged, please add to the runbook “Related dashboards” section so we have two-way linking

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- Aisha: PR with row + panels + links (today)
- Sean: review/merge + announce in #eng-oncall
- Rafael: follow-up task for incident bot PM link (separate)

Aisha Bello: done. PR incoming in ~30m.
