title: #eng-sre: 1738806227
preview:
Rafael Mendes: We need to lock alerting for rollout gate failures before beta expands. Specifically: when do we PAGE vs create a ticket? And how do we route (SRE oncall vs owning team) when a gate fails on a Dedicated pool stage.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Last week we had a bunch of “gate failed” signals and everyone stared at it b/c unclear if it was a product thing or infra thing.

Peter Holtz: Can we define it in terms of “rollout is actively changing prod traffic” vs not? i.e. if a rollout is RUNNING and gates fail, we should treat that as potential customer impact.

Allison Grant: From obs side: we can do alert labels for `gate_type` / `signal_source` (routing / runtime / fleet / telemetry). But someone needs to own the mapping.

Sergio Costa: I can wire dashboards/alerts templates either way, but we need the policy. Also want to avoid a page storm when a rollout spans multi-region.

Rafael Mendes: Draft proposal:
- Gate FAIL during RUNNING stage => page Dedicated oncall *if* it’s a “customer-impact” gate (error rate, 429/drop, p99 latency, saturation/headroom).
- Gate FAIL during RUNNING stage for “safety/health” signals (GPU health, OOM spikes) => page Fleet oncall.
- Gate UNKNOWN (missing data / telemetry lag) => ticket by default, page only if persists > X mins AND rollout is trying to auto-promote.
Thoughts?

Jordan Lee: From policy engine side: gate evaluation will emit `result = pass|fail|unknown` + `reason` (e.g. `insufficient_samples`, `metrics_stale`, `query_error`). We can expose that in alert annotations. Also: for determinism we should avoid auto-promotion if any gate is unknown.

Logan Wright: +1 to ticket for unknown. Telemetry has occasional backfills/lag; paging every time there’s staleness will be noisy. Better: page only if `metrics_stale` crosses a higher threshold AND affects multiple pools.

Vanessa Ortiz: For GPU health: if we’re gating on ECC/taints/throttling, those are usually actionable by fleet oncall. I’d rather get paged directly than have SRE triage.

Irene Choi: Agree. But please add “is this isolated to the canary partition” context. A single bad node in the canary shouldn’t page fleet if it’s already drained.

Rafael Mendes: So we need “blast radius” in the alert: stage % / replica count, region, #instances affected.

Sergio Costa: We can include that if telemetry emits stage + partition tags (I think PR-28521 did). For alert grouping, we should group by `rollout_id` and `gate_name` and maybe `region`.

Allison Grant: Alertmanager grouping proposal:
- group_by: `rollout_id`, `pool_id`, `stage_id`, `gate_name`
- add `region` in the text but don’t group unless we want separate incidents per region
Also add inhibition rule: if “Rollout Aborted” fires, inhibit gate failure pages.

Sean Gallagher: Re page vs ticket thresholds, can we define a small set of severities?
- SEV2 page: likely customer-visible (elevated 5xx, sustained 429, p99 regression beyond SLO)
- SEV3 page: safety risk but not yet impact (GPU health, OOM rate)
- Ticket: unknown/noisy/low sample size

Peter Holtz: I’d simplify to:
1) “GateFailedCustomerImpact” => page Dedicated/SRE oncall
2) “GateFailedFleetHealth” => page Fleet
3) “GateUnknown” => ticket Telemetry/Obs
And have *runbook* tell oncall how to route if it’s actually an app bug.

Chloe Martin: From Console side, we can surface the gate failure reason and link to the exact query + time window. If we include a runbook link in alert annotations, we can keep oncall from spelunking.

Ethan Park: Control plane can include `owning_team` on the gate definition. Eg latency/error gates => SRE, GPU health => Fleet, audit export => Security/Compliance, etc. Would that help routing?

Allison Grant: Yes, if it’s a stable label. But for paging we still want PD service based on team. If we can output `pagerduty_service = dedicated-sre | fleet-oncall | telemetry-oncall` we’re golden.

Jordan Lee: We can’t make policy engine call PD, but it can emit the label in gate evaluation result -> metrics stream. Alerting rules can route.

Rafael Mendes: Ok. Let’s define routing map (v1):
- `gate_type=latency|error_rate|request_drop|http_429|saturation|queue_depth` => page `Dedicated SRE Oncall` when rollout_state=RUNNING.
- `gate_type=gpu_health|oom_rate|node_unhealthy` => page `Fleet Oncall` when rollout_state=RUNNING.
- `gate_type=quality_regression` (if enabled for specific pools) => ticket `Applied ML / Evals` unless rollout is >50% stage (then page Dedicated SRE?).
- `result=unknown` w/ `reason=metrics_stale|query_error` => ticket `Telemetry/Observability`. Page only if unknown persists >30m while RUNNING and stage is not held.
Any objections?

Sean Gallagher: For quality regression, I’m nervous paging SRE for that. Can we keep it ticket-only for beta? (since non-goal is arbitrary custom metrics and quality signals may be sparse)

Logan Wright: +1 ticket-only initially. Also quality metrics tend to be async; paging will be chaos.

Rafael Mendes: Works. Beta: quality gates are “block promotion” but alert as ticket.

Peter Holtz: We should also add “anti-spam”:
- require 2 consecutive evaluations (e.g. 2x5m) before paging
- auto-resolve when gate passes for 10m
- suppress pages if rollout is HELD (operator intentionally paused)

Allison Grant: YES. Otherwise you’ll get paged during holds.

Sergio Costa: Implementation details for alerts (Prometheus):
- Metric: `dedicated_rollout_gate_status{result="fail"}` with labels `pool_id`, `rollout_id`, `stage_id`, `gate_name`, `gate_type`, `owning_team`, `rollout_state`.
- Page rule:
  - `result=fail` AND `rollout_state="running"` FOR 10m AND `gate_type` in impact set
- Ticket rule:
  - `result=unknown` FOR 30m OR `result=fail` with non-paging gate types
We can send tickets via Jira integration, pages via PD.

Vanessa Ortiz: For fleet paging rule, make sure it’s not firing on “expected drains.” If the canary partition is 1 GPU and it goes unhealthy and we drain it, gate might still show fail until next eval.

Jordan Lee: Gate evaluator can emit `suppressed=true` when we detect “no capacity assigned” / stage is effectively 0 replicas. Not sure we have that in v1.

Ethan Park: Control plane knows assigned replicas per stage; we can emit `stage_effective_replicas`. Alerts can check `>0`.

Rafael Mendes: Great. Let’s add this condition to paging rules: `stage_effective_replicas >= min_replicas_for_stage` (or just >0).

Sean Gallagher: Also, what about multi-region? If one region is impaired, do we want separate incidents?

Peter Holtz: For paging: one PD incident per rollout_id + gate_name. In the description include “regions failing: us-east, eu-west”. That keeps it actionable.

Allison Grant: We can do alert annotations with a computed list is hard in Prometheus, but we can include a link to Grafana panel that lists per region. That’s enough.

Sergio Costa: I’ll update the template to:
- Summary: `Rollout gate failed: <gate_name> (<gate_type>)`
- Details: pool_id, rollout_id, stage_id, stage_traffic%, effective_replicas, region (if label), result_reason, observed_value, threshold
- Links: Console rollout URL + Grafana “Gate evidence” dashboard + runbook

Chloe Martin: Console URL format we’re using internally: `/console/dedicated/pools/<pool_id>/rollouts/<rollout_id>?stage=<stage_id>` (subject to change but close). I’ll drop a stable redirect link once we merge.

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
1) Sergio/Allison: implement 3 alert rules (ImpactGateFail->PD dedicated-sre, FleetGateFail->PD fleet, Unknown->Jira Telemetry) w/ FOR durations + HELD suppression.
2) Ethan/Jordan: ensure metrics include `rollout_state`, `owning_team`, `stage_effective_replicas`, `reason`.
3) Rafael/Sean: update oncall playbook w/ routing + what “unknown” means + when to force-hold/abort.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: add explicit note: paging for gate fail is not “rollout must be aborted”, it’s “human should look”. We should keep abort criteria in runbook.

Rafael Mendes: yep. Will add.

Allison Grant: One more: we should add an inhibition for “Telemetry pipeline down” so gate unknown tickets don’t explode. Telemetry already has an alert we can use as a parent.

Logan Wright: We have `telemetry_ingest_lag_seconds > 600` page. If that’s firing, inhibit the per-rollout `metrics_stale` tickets.

Sergio Costa: Cool. I’ll wire inhibition based on `reason="metrics_stale"` + global ingest lag alert.

Vanessa Ortiz: Please include `reservation_size` / GPU type somewhere? Helpful context when triaging saturation gates.

Rafael Mendes: Maybe in the dashboard, but alert text can get too big. If we can include `pool_size_gpus` label without blowing cardinality, do it.

Allison Grant: pool_size is low cardinality per pool, so ok.

Ethan Park: We can expose pool_size via control plane metric on the rollout series.

Rafael Mendes: Great. Closing this thread once templates are in review.

Sergio Costa: I’ll post PR link in-channel when ready.
text:

Sergio Costa: I can wire dashboards/alerts templates either way, but we need the policy. Also want to avoid a page storm when a rollout spans multi-region.

Rafael Mendes: Draft proposal:
- Gate FAIL during RUNNING stage => page Dedicated oncall *if* it’s a “customer-impact” gate (error rate, 429/drop, p99 latency, saturation/headroom).
- Gate FAIL during RUNNING stage for “safety/health” signals (GPU health, OOM spikes) => page Fleet oncall.
- Gate UNKNOWN (missing data / telemetry lag) => ticket by default, page only if persists > X mins AND rollout is trying to auto-promote.
Thoughts?

Jordan Lee: From policy engine side: gate evaluation will emit `result = pass|fail|unknown` + `reason` (e.g. `insufficient_samples`, `metrics_stale`, `query_error`). We can expose that in alert annotations. Also: for determinism we should avoid auto-promotion if any gate is unknown.

Logan Wright: +1 to ticket for unknown. Telemetry has occasional backfills/lag; paging every time there’s staleness will be noisy. Better: page only if `metrics_stale` crosses a higher threshold AND affects multiple pools.

Vanessa Ortiz: For GPU health: if we’re gating on ECC/taints/throttling, those are usually actionable by fleet oncall. I’d rather get paged directly than have SRE triage.

Irene Choi: Agree. But please add “is this isolated to the canary partition” context. A single bad node in the canary shouldn’t page fleet if it’s already drained.

Rafael Mendes: So we need “blast radius” in the alert: stage % / replica count, region, #instances affected.

Sergio Costa: We can include that if telemetry emits stage + partition tags (I think PR-28521 did). For alert grouping, we should group by `rollout_id` and `gate_name` and maybe `region`.

Allison Grant: Alertmanager grouping proposal:
- group_by: `rollout_id`, `pool_id`, `stage_id`, `gate_name`
- add `region` in the text but don’t group unless we want separate incidents per region
Also add inhibition rule: if “Rollout Aborted” fires, inhibit gate failure pages.

Sean Gallagher: Re page vs ticket thresholds, can we define a small set of severities?
- SEV2 page: likely customer-visible (elevated 5xx, sustained 429, p99 regression beyond SLO)
- SEV3 page: safety risk but not yet impact (GPU health, OOM rate)
- Ticket: unknown/noisy/low sample size

Peter Holtz: I’d simplify to:
1) “GateFailedCustomerImpact” => page Dedicated/SRE oncall
2) “GateFailedFleetHealth” => page Fleet
3) “GateUnknown” => ticket Telemetry/Obs
And have *runbook* tell oncall how to route if it’s actually an app bug.

Chloe Martin: From Console side, we can surface the gate failure reason and link to the exact query + time window. If we include a runbook link in alert annotations, we can keep oncall from spelunking.

Ethan Park: Control plane can include `owning_team` on the gate definition. Eg latency/error gates => SRE, GPU health => Fleet, audit export => Security/Compliance, etc. Would that help routing?

Allison Grant: Yes, if it’s a stable label. But for paging we still want PD service based on team. If we can output `pagerduty_service = dedicated-sre | fleet-oncall | telemetry-oncall` we’re golden.

Jordan Lee: We can’t make policy engine call PD, but it can emit the label in gate evaluation result -> metrics stream. Alerting rules can route.

Rafael Mendes: Ok. Let’s define routing map (v1):
- `gate_type=latency|error_rate|request_drop|http_429|saturation|queue_depth` => page `Dedicated SRE Oncall` when rollout_state=RUNNING.
- `gate_type=gpu_health|oom_rate|node_unhealthy` => page `Fleet Oncall` when rollout_state=RUNNING.
- `gate_type=quality_regression` (if enabled for specific pools) => ticket `Applied ML / Evals` unless rollout is >50% stage (then page Dedicated SRE?).
- `result=unknown` w/ `reason=metrics_stale|query_error` => ticket `Telemetry/Observability`. Page only if unknown persists >30m while RUNNING and stage is not held.
Any objections?

Sean Gallagher: For quality regression, I’m nervous paging SRE for that. Can we keep it ticket-only for beta? (since non-goal is arbitrary custom metrics and quality signals may be sparse)

Logan Wright: +1 ticket-only initially. Also quality metrics tend to be async; paging will be chaos.

Rafael Mendes: Works. Beta: quality gates are “block promotion” but alert as ticket.

Peter Holtz: We should also add “anti-spam”:
- require 2 consecutive evaluations (e.g. 2x5m) before paging
- auto-resolve when gate passes for 10m
- suppress pages if rollout is HELD (operator intentionally paused)

Allison Grant: YES. Otherwise you’ll get paged during holds.

Sergio Costa: Implementation details for alerts (Prometheus):
- Metric: `dedicated_rollout_gate_status{result="fail"}` with labels `pool_id`, `rollout_id`, `stage_id`, `gate_name`, `gate_type`, `owning_team`, `rollout_state`.
- Page rule:
  - `result=fail` AND `rollout_state="running"` FOR 10m AND `gate_type` in impact set
- Ticket rule:
  - `result=unknown` FOR 30m OR `result=fail` with non-paging gate types
We can send tickets via Jira integration, pages via PD.

Vanessa Ortiz: For fleet paging rule, make sure it’s not firing on “expected drains.” If the canary partition is 1 GPU and it goes unhealthy and we drain it, gate might still show fail until next eval.

Jordan Lee: Gate evaluator can emit `suppressed=true` when we detect “no capacity assigned” / stage is effectively 0 replicas. Not sure we have that in v1.

Ethan Park: Control plane knows assigned replicas per stage; we can emit `stage_effective_replicas`. Alerts can check `>0`.

Rafael Mendes: Great. Let’s add this condition to paging rules: `stage_effective_replicas >= min_replicas_for_stage` (or just >0).

Sean Gallagher: Also, what about multi-region? If one region is impaired, do we want separate incidents?

Peter Holtz: For paging: one PD incident per rollout_id + gate_name. In the description include “regions failing: us-east, eu-west”. That keeps it actionable.

Allison Grant: We can do alert annotations with a computed list is hard in Prometheus, but we can include a link to Grafana panel that lists per region. That’s enough.

Sergio Costa: I’ll update the template to:
- Summary: `Rollout gate failed: <gate_name> (<gate_type>)`
- Details: pool_id, rollout_id, stage_id, stage_traffic%, effective_replicas, region (if label), result_reason, observed_value, threshold
- Links: Console rollout URL + Grafana “Gate evidence” dashboard + runbook

Chloe Martin: Console URL format we’re using internally: `/console/dedicated/pools/<pool_id>/rollouts/<rollout_id>?stage=<stage_id>` (subject to change but close). I’ll drop a stable redirect link once we merge.

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
1) Sergio/Allison: implement 3 alert rules (ImpactGateFail->PD dedicated-sre, FleetGateFail->PD fleet, Unknown->Jira Telemetry) w/ FOR durations + HELD suppression.
2) Ethan/Jordan: ensure metrics include `rollout_state`, `owning_team`, `stage_effective_replicas`, `reason`.
3) Rafael/Sean: update oncall playbook w/ routing + what “unknown” means + when to force-hold/abort.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: add explicit note: paging for gate fail is not “rollout must be aborted”, it’s “human should look”. We should keep abort criteria in runbook.

Rafael Mendes: yep. Will add.

Allison Grant: One more: we should add an inhibition for “Telemetry pipeline down” so gate unknown tickets don’t explode. Telemetry already has an alert we can use as a parent.

Logan Wright: We have `telemetry_ingest_lag_seconds > 600` page. If that’s firing, inhibit the per-rollout `metrics_stale` tickets.

Sergio Costa: Cool. I’ll wire inhibition based on `reason="metrics_stale"` + global ingest lag alert.

Vanessa Ortiz: Please include `reservation_size` / GPU type somewhere? Helpful context when triaging saturation gates.

Rafael Mendes: Maybe in the dashboard, but alert text can get too big. If we can include `pool_size_gpus` label without blowing cardinality, do it.

Allison Grant: pool_size is low cardinality per pool, so ok.

Ethan Park: We can expose pool_size via control plane metric on the rollout series.

Rafael Mendes: Great. Closing this thread once templates are in review.

Sergio Costa: I’ll post PR link in-channel when ready.

title: #eng-sre: 1738726666
preview:
Sean Gallagher: heads up: seeing a bunch of Snowflake query timeouts + queueing starting ~10 mins ago. correlates with the unit-econ backfill job kicking off. dashboard endpoints are also spiking p95.

Aisha Bello: yup, alerts firing:
- WarehouseCreditBurnRateHigh (econ_analytics)
- QueryQueueDepthHigh
- API unit-econ /breakdown p95 > 8s

Mei Lin: that might be my fault 😬 I started the 2025Q1 backfill run from the new backfill tooling. it’s doing hour-partitioned inserts into the materialized agg tables. I set concurrency to 32 to “get it done overnight”.

Allison Grant: 32 is… ambitious lol. what warehouse size are you running against?

Mei Lin: econ_analytics warehouse, size = L. no explicit statement timeout; using default. queries are mostly `INSERT INTO agg_hourly ... SELECT ... GROUP BY ...` over raw metering.

Caleb Johnson: I’m seeing completeness checks lagging too. hourly pipeline is fine but backfill is competing for same warehouse resources, so the checks are timing out.

Sean Gallagher: ok we need to throttle this. we can’t take the whole warehouse down during business hours.

Sean Gallagher: proposals:
1) cut concurrency (like 4-6 max)
2) add a sleep between partitions / small batches (e.g., per-day chunks)
3) run during a maintenance window (off-hours PT) + announce in #eng-platform
4) consider using a dedicated backfill warehouse or at least a separate Snowflake “warehouse” so normal dashboards aren’t impacted

Aisha Bello: +1 separate warehouse if possible. even small query queue depth impacts the console and folks start reporting “dashboard broken”.

Mei Lin: I can stop the run now. what’s the recommended concurrency? the tooling takes `--max-workers`.

Allison Grant: for backfills we usually start at 4 and watch credits/queue depth. if it’s stable, creep to 6-8. 32 on L will just stampede.

Logan Wright: agree. also: please don’t run backfills on the same warehouse that serves prod dashboards without coordination. finance is actively validating QTD numbers this week.

Mei Lin: totally fair. sorry. stopping now.

Mei Lin: (stopping means canceling the in-flight queries + exiting the runner, correct?)

Sean Gallagher: yep. cancel running queries, let warehouse drain. if the job is idempotent, we can resume with narrower ranges.

Nadia Rahman: quick note: if you’re backfilling raw metering -> agg tables, double-check you’re not re-scanning the entire metering table each hour partition. we saw that pattern earlier and it got expensive fast.

Mei Lin: good call. right now it’s doing `WHERE request_ts >= :start AND request_ts < :end` but it still hits a large micro-partition range because of clustering. we haven’t reclustered on request_ts for that raw table.

Allison Grant: that explains the pain. even with low concurrency, scans may be heavy.

Caleb Johnson: could you backfill from an intermediate hourly staging table instead of raw? less data to scan and already normalized dims.

Logan Wright: we have `metering_hourly_normalized` as part of the hourly pipeline. use that as source for backfill if possible. it’s partitioned and way cheaper.

Mei Lin: ok. I’ll switch the backfill source to `metering_hourly_normalized` for the historical range. (for the last 48h we can keep current pipeline.)

Aisha Bello: metrics update: queue depth dropping now, timeouts slowing. looks like cancel is helping.

Sean Gallagher: thanks. can we agree on an operational plan for backfill runs?

Sean Gallagher: suggested guardrails:
- max-workers default = 4
- hard cap = 8 without SRE approval
- require `--window` param (e.g., 1 day at a time) so it’s naturally chunked
- run in 10pm-6am PT maintenance window unless explicitly approved
- post start/end + expected credit burn in #eng-sre + #eng-platform

Allison Grant: +1 and we should add a Resource Monitor / credit quota on the backfill warehouse if we create one, so it fails safe instead of taking everything down.

Mei Lin: works for me. I’ll re-run tonight with max-workers=4 and day-sized chunks. also I’ll request a separate warehouse for backfills.

Logan Wright: please also add a note to the runbook: “don’t backfill on econ_analytics during business hours” + the recommended flags.

Caleb Johnson: and a quick verification step after each day chunk (row counts + token totals) so we don’t have to rerun huge ranges if something is off.

Nadia Rahman: + add `STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS` override on the runner so we don’t have hanging queries. something like 900s per chunk.

Mei Lin: ack. I’ll put together a PR to the backfill runner defaults (max-workers=4, day chunk default, statement timeout) and update the oncall doc.

Aisha Bello: I’ll tune alerts to page only if queue depth > X for > 10m (today was legit though). right now it’s a bit chatty.

Sean Gallagher: cool. once the warehouse fully recovers we can close the loop. Mei, can you drop the exact command you ran so we can document “what not to do”? 😅

Mei Lin: lol yes:
`backfill_unit_econ --start 2025-01-01 --end 2025-02-04 --max-workers 32 --source raw_metering_requests`

Sean Gallagher: perfect. we’ll immortalize that in the runbook as an anti-pattern.

Allison Grant: adding “warehouse DoS” section to the playbook :)

Mei Lin: deserved.

Aisha Bello: status now green. p95 dashboard queries back to ~900ms. credits stabilizing.

Sean Gallagher: 👍 closing. next step: maintenance window backfill + separate warehouse request.

title: #eng-sre: 1738724102
preview:
Sean Gallagher: we need to land the “error budget aware promotion” rule for Dedicated staged rollouts. tl;dr: no promote when we’re burning budget hard, and definitely no promote during an active incident. can we align today on what signal(s) we use + thresholds?

Paula Mendes: +1. product wants something we can explain simply in Console: “Promotion blocked: incident in progress” or “blocked: error budget burn high”. But it needs to be deterministic (policy engine) and not too noisy.

Rafael Mendes: SRE view: promotions during elevated error rates have been a repeat footgun. We should block promotion when burn rate is >X for any of the rollout SLOs (availability + latency). Also: if there’s an active SEV for the pool’s region, block.

Peter Holtz: careful: “active incident” definition. Do we mean any open PD incident tagged redwood-inference? or specifically incidents that impact the pool’s routing target (region/fleet)? if we do “any incident”, it’ll basically stop all promotions during random unrelated stuff.

Allison Grant: +1. we should scope it to incidents that touch Dedicated serving path. We can use incident labels: service=dedicated-data-plane OR service=smart-routing OR service=telemetry? (telemetry outages matter too if gate eval is fail-closed)

Jordan Lee: from policy engine side: we can implement eligibility as another gate (pre-flight) that returns “BLOCKED” with reason codes. Needs to be based on a stable query. Incident integration is trickier than metrics.

Logan Wright: metrics piece is easy-ish: compute burn rate from SLO error budget policy. but Dedicated has per-pool SLOs and also shared per-region components. For v1, are we doing pool-level SLOs only?

Rafael Mendes: i’d start with pool-level signals for sure, but we *also* need region-level “fleet is on fire” backstop. if us-east fleet is degraded, don’t promote a pool in us-east even if that pool isn’t receiving much traffic / metrics are sparse.

Mina Haddad: routing perspective: if we block promotions during regional impairment, also reduces chaos. but we need to define how we detect region impairment. if it’s “any incident in region”, see above.

Sean Gallagher: proposal: 2 checks:
1) Incident check: block if there’s an open incident with impact_scope includes “dedicated-serving” AND region intersects pool regions.
2) Burn check: block if burn rate for pool’s “dedicated_pool_availability” or “dedicated_pool_latency” exceeds threshold.

Paula Mendes: what threshold + time window? PM needs defaults.

Peter Holtz: burn rate should be classic multi-window: if 30m burn > 2x AND 6h burn > 1x => block. That catches spikes + sustained. For latency maybe more sensitive?

Allison Grant: reminder: our SLOs are 30d windows. burn rate is (current error rate) / (allowed error rate). For “block promotion” we want conservative but not to the point of never promoting on spiky customers.

Rafael Mendes: also want “no promote during high burn even if stage gates pass” – bc stage gates are scoped to canary partition; burn can be global pool health. If global is burning, promoting can amplify.

Jordan Lee: what’s the source of truth for “burn rate”? do we have an existing SLO API? if not, we end up duplicating PromQL in policy engine.

Logan Wright: we can expose computed burn from the SLO service (we already calculate it for error budget dashboards). It’s in Telemetry pipeline, but we need to ensure it’s low-latency + cached. Alternative is direct PromQL but that’s brittle.

Sean Gallagher: I’d rather not hardcode PromQL in policy engine. Can we have a simple internal endpoint like `GET /slo/burn?scope=dedicated_pool&pool_id=...` returning burn over windows?

Logan Wright: doable. We already have something similar for internal dashboards; might need auth + caching + add pool_id dimension.

Paula Mendes: on “no promote during incident”: can we avoid building a full incident API integration for v1 and just have SRE set a “promotion freeze” toggle? (manual hold)

Rafael Mendes: manual hold exists, but the point is to make it automatic. We keep forgetting to set holds during SEVs.

Peter Holtz: but if incident integration slips, a fallback is: block if burn > threshold OR if there’s a “global change freeze” flag set by oncall.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can consume a “change_freeze” config from control plane today. incident integration later.

Allison Grant: plus: “telemetry outage” scenario. If we fail-closed on missing gate metrics, promotions are already blocked. But burn-rate endpoint might be missing too. What do we do: unknown burn => block? probably yes.

Sean Gallagher: yes: eligibility should be fail-closed. If we can’t determine burn or incident status, block promotion with reason “eligibility_unknown”. allow override via admin force-promote.

Paula Mendes: messaging: Console needs to differentiate “blocked because unknown” vs “blocked due to high burn” so customers don’t think the rollout system is broken.

Mina Haddad: +1 and make sure support knows the playbook.

Rafael Mendes: thresholds suggestion:
- Availability burn: block if (30m > 2.0 AND 6h > 1.0) OR (30m > 5.0) hard stop.
- Latency burn: block if (30m > 2.0 AND 6h > 1.0) but define latency SLO first (p95?)
Also block if 429 rate is elevated? (it’s not always in SLO)

Allison Grant: 429s should roll into availability SLI if customer-visible failures, but currently some teams treat 429 separately. If we don’t include it, we’ll promote while throttling.

Logan Wright: we can include 429 in the availability error budget calculation for Dedicated pools (>=400 and !=408?) but need to confirm definitions. Changing it might affect existing budgets.

Sean Gallagher: let’s not change SLI definition in this thread. For promotion eligibility, we can add an extra “saturation guardrail” gate that checks 429/queue depth, separate from burn.

Jordan Lee: +1. keep “error budget aware” gate purely driven by existing SLOs, otherwise it’s a moving target.

Paula Mendes: ok so:
- eligibility gate A: `error_budget_burn` based on availability+latency SLO burn
- eligibility gate B: `incident_freeze` (either incident integration or oncall freeze flag)
- separate rollout stage gates handle 429/queue depth/etc

Peter Holtz: what about low traffic pools where burn is noisy / divides by tiny numbers? we need minimum sample size.

Allison Grant: burn calc already has “insufficient events” handling. If insufficient data, return “unknown”. That would block promotions unless override. Is that ok? for low-traffic Dedicated pools, promotion blocks forever.

Rafael Mendes: for low traffic, we should allow promotion with manual ack. like “burn unknown due to low sample size; requires SRE/customer admin confirmation” -> promote with a reason.

Jordan Lee: implementation wise: policy engine can return `BLOCKED_REQUIRES_ACK` vs `BLOCKED`. Promotion API could accept `acknowledge_risk=true` + `reason` for that case, and we audit it.

Sean Gallagher: I like that. splits into:
- HARD block: active incident, high burn
- SOFT block: unknown burn due to insufficient data (requires acknowledgement)

Paula Mendes: can we keep v1 simpler: unknown => blocked but allow force-promote for admins only? customer admins won’t have it.

Rafael Mendes: Dedicated customers *will* ask for it. But admin-only might be ok for beta, as long as TAMs/SRE can do it with audit.

IncidentBot: [status] SEV-3 incident opened: INC-18422 “us-east1 dedicated fleet elevated p99” (service: dedicated-data-plane, region: us-east1)

Mina Haddad: lol perfect timing. this is exactly what we need the block for.

Sean Gallagher: ok. for incident signal: if IncidentBot posts are in #incidents, we need API not slack scraping :)

Logan Wright: incident system has a read API (internal). It’s not super polished but it exists. We can query “open incidents” filtered by labels. I’ll dm the endpoint.

Jordan Lee: if we can get incident list via internal service with caching, policy engine can call it. Need strict timeouts; if it times out, return unknown => hard block or soft?

Allison Grant: timeout => hard block. otherwise people will promote during incident service outages.

Paula Mendes: agreed. and surface “blocked: incident status unavailable” in Console.

Rafael Mendes: also need “no promote while page is firing” even if incident not declared yet. but that’s v2.

Peter Holtz: +1 but please no more dependencies for v1.

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- Jordan: define reason codes + state model for eligibility gate results.
- Logan: confirm we can expose burn from SLO service with pool_id + windows 30m/6h. include “unknown” semantics.
- Allison/Rafael: propose default thresholds (2x/1x + 5x hard stop) and sanity check vs historical.
- Paula: confirm product copy + who can override (admin only for beta).

Paula Mendes: 👍 will put into PM-827 notes. also: need to ensure audit logs capture override reason.

Jordan Lee: ack. will add `promotion_blocked_reason` and `override_reason` to audit event payload.

Allison Grant: please also log the observed burn values + windows at decision time (for postmortems). doesn’t need full series, just the numbers.

Rafael Mendes: +1. otherwise we’ll argue later whether it was “really high burn” or not.

Sean Gallagher: done. will follow up with a short spec in this channel once Jordan/Logan confirm feasibility.

Peter Holtz: careful: “active incident” definition. Do we mean any open PD incident tagged redwood-inference? or specifically incidents that impact the pool’s routing target (region/fleet)? if we do “any incident”, it’ll basically stop all promotions during random unrelated stuff.

Allison Grant: +1. we should scope it to incidents that touch Dedicated serving path. We can use incident labels: service=dedicated-data-plane OR service=smart-routing OR service=telemetry? (telemetry outages matter too if gate eval is fail-closed)

Jordan Lee: from policy engine side: we can implement eligibility as another gate (pre-flight) that returns “BLOCKED” with reason codes. Needs to be based on a stable query. Incident integration is trickier than metrics.

Logan Wright: metrics piece is easy-ish: compute burn rate from SLO error budget policy. but Dedicated has per-pool SLOs and also shared per-region components. For v1, are we doing pool-level SLOs only?

Rafael Mendes: i’d start with pool-level signals for sure, but we *also* need region-level “fleet is on fire” backstop. if us-east fleet is degraded, don’t promote a pool in us-east even if that pool isn’t receiving much traffic / metrics are sparse.

Mina Haddad: routing perspective: if we block promotions during regional impairment, also reduces chaos. but we need to define how we detect region impairment. if it’s “any incident in region”, see above.

Sean Gallagher: proposal: 2 checks:
1) Incident check: block if there’s an open incident with impact_scope includes “dedicated-serving” AND region intersects pool regions.
2) Burn check: block if burn rate for pool’s “dedicated_pool_availability” or “dedicated_pool_latency” exceeds threshold.

Paula Mendes: what threshold + time window? PM needs defaults.

Peter Holtz: burn rate should be classic multi-window: if 30m burn > 2x AND 6h burn > 1x => block. That catches spikes + sustained. For latency maybe more sensitive?

Allison Grant: reminder: our SLOs are 30d windows. burn rate is (current error rate) / (allowed error rate). For “block promotion” we want conservative but not to the point of never promoting on spiky customers.

Rafael Mendes: also want “no promote during high burn even if stage gates pass” – bc stage gates are scoped to canary partition; burn can be global pool health. If global is burning, promoting can amplify.

Jordan Lee: what’s the source of truth for “burn rate”? do we have an existing SLO API? if not, we end up duplicating PromQL in policy engine.

Logan Wright: we can expose computed burn from the SLO service (we already calculate it for error budget dashboards). It’s in Telemetry pipeline, but we need to ensure it’s low-latency + cached. Alternative is direct PromQL but that’s brittle.

Sean Gallagher: I’d rather not hardcode PromQL in policy engine. Can we have a simple internal endpoint like `GET /slo/burn?scope=dedicated_pool&pool_id=...` returning burn over windows?

Logan Wright: doable. We already have something similar for internal dashboards; might need auth + caching + add pool_id dimension.

Paula Mendes: on “no promote during incident”: can we avoid building a full incident API integration for v1 and just have SRE set a “promotion freeze” toggle? (manual hold)

Rafael Mendes: manual hold exists, but the point is to make it automatic. We keep forgetting to set holds during SEVs.

Peter Holtz: but if incident integration slips, a fallback is: block if burn > threshold OR if there’s a “global change freeze” flag set by oncall.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can consume a “change_freeze” config from control plane today. incident integration later.

Allison Grant: plus: “telemetry outage” scenario. If we fail-closed on missing gate metrics, promotions are already blocked. But burn-rate endpoint might be missing too. What do we do: unknown burn => block? probably yes.

Sean Gallagher: yes: eligibility should be fail-closed. If we can’t determine burn or incident status, block promotion with reason “eligibility_unknown”. allow override via admin force-promote.

Paula Mendes: messaging: Console needs to differentiate “blocked because unknown” vs “blocked due to high burn” so customers don’t think the rollout system is broken.

Mina Haddad: +1 and make sure support knows the playbook.

Rafael Mendes: thresholds suggestion:
- Availability burn: block if (30m > 2.0 AND 6h > 1.0) OR (30m > 5.0) hard stop.
- Latency burn: block if (30m > 2.0 AND 6h > 1.0) but define latency SLO first (p95?)
Also block if 429 rate is elevated? (it’s not always in SLO)

Allison Grant: 429s should roll into availability SLI if customer-visible failures, but currently some teams treat 429 separately. If we don’t include it, we’ll promote while throttling.

Logan Wright: we can include 429 in the availability error budget calculation for Dedicated pools (>=400 and !=408?) but need to confirm definitions. Changing it might affect existing budgets.

Sean Gallagher: let’s not change SLI definition in this thread. For promotion eligibility, we can add an extra “saturation guardrail” gate that checks 429/queue depth, separate from burn.

Jordan Lee: +1. keep “error budget aware” gate purely driven by existing SLOs, otherwise it’s a moving target.

Paula Mendes: ok so:
- eligibility gate A: `error_budget_burn` based on availability+latency SLO burn
- eligibility gate B: `incident_freeze` (either incident integration or oncall freeze flag)
- separate rollout stage gates handle 429/queue depth/etc

Peter Holtz: what about low traffic pools where burn is noisy / divides by tiny numbers? we need minimum sample size.

Allison Grant: burn calc already has “insufficient events” handling. If insufficient data, return “unknown”. That would block promotions unless override. Is that ok? for low-traffic Dedicated pools, promotion blocks forever.

Rafael Mendes: for low traffic, we should allow promotion with manual ack. like “burn unknown due to low sample size; requires SRE/customer admin confirmation” -> promote with a reason.

Jordan Lee: implementation wise: policy engine can return `BLOCKED_REQUIRES_ACK` vs `BLOCKED`. Promotion API could accept `acknowledge_risk=true` + `reason` for that case, and we audit it.

Sean Gallagher: I like that. splits into:
- HARD block: active incident, high burn
- SOFT block: unknown burn due to insufficient data (requires acknowledgement)

Paula Mendes: can we keep v1 simpler: unknown => blocked but allow force-promote for admins only? customer admins won’t have it.

Rafael Mendes: Dedicated customers *will* ask for it. But admin-only might be ok for beta, as long as TAMs/SRE can do it with audit.

IncidentBot: [status] SEV-3 incident opened: INC-18422 “us-east1 dedicated fleet elevated p99” (service: dedicated-data-plane, region: us-east1)

Mina Haddad: lol perfect timing. this is exactly what we need the block for.

Sean Gallagher: ok. for incident signal: if IncidentBot posts are in #incidents, we need API not slack scraping :)

Logan Wright: incident system has a read API (internal). It’s not super polished but it exists. We can query “open incidents” filtered by labels. I’ll dm the endpoint.

Jordan Lee: if we can get incident list via internal service with caching, policy engine can call it. Need strict timeouts; if it times out, return unknown => hard block or soft?

Allison Grant: timeout => hard block. otherwise people will promote during incident service outages.

Paula Mendes: agreed. and surface “blocked: incident status unavailable” in Console.

Rafael Mendes: also need “no promote while page is firing” even if incident not declared yet. but that’s v2.

Peter Holtz: +1 but please no more dependencies for v1.

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- Jordan: define reason codes + state model for eligibility gate results.
- Logan: confirm we can expose burn from SLO service with pool_id + windows 30m/6h. include “unknown” semantics.
- Allison/Rafael: propose default thresholds (2x/1x + 5x hard stop) and sanity check vs historical.
- Paula: confirm product copy + who can override (admin only for beta).

Paula Mendes: 👍 will put into PM-827 notes. also: need to ensure audit logs capture override reason.

Jordan Lee: ack. will add `promotion_blocked_reason` and `override_reason` to audit event payload.

Allison Grant: please also log the observed burn values + windows at decision time (for postmortems). doesn’t need full series, just the numbers.

Rafael Mendes: +1. otherwise we’ll argue later whether it was “really high burn” or not.

Sean Gallagher: done. will follow up with a short spec in this channel once Jordan/Logan confirm feasibility.

title: #eng-sre: 1738702109
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Kicking off thread on SLO gates + paging policy for Dedicated burst. Goal: bursting must *never* silently blow up non-burst tenants.

Proposal: add an automated “non-burst protection gate” that can reduce/disable burst when neighbor SLOs regress. We should be able to ship this before beta expands.

Key idea:
- We measure latency SLOs only on *non-burst* traffic (baseline Dedicated capacity lanes), per region/hwclass.
- If those degrade beyond threshold vs rolling baseline, we progressively clamp burst caps (or trip global burst circuit breaker) until recovery.
- Gate integrates with rollout flags (per region/hw class) + admission controller.

Open questions:
1) What exact SLI? p95 vs p99? request latency vs queueing?
2) What windows / deltas (absolute vs relative) are safe + not noisy?
3) Paging: when do we page SRE vs alert-only?

Rafael Mendes: +1. If this isn’t fully automated we’ll be chasing our tail on incidents.

First stab: make it a 2-stage gate:
- Stage A (soft clamp): if non-burst p95 > SLO for 5m OR p95 delta > +20% vs trailing 30m baseline, clamp burst_ratio_max to 1.1 for that pool.
- Stage B (hard stop): if non-burst p99 > SLO for 10m OR p99 delta > +30%, disable burst (admission returns BURST_DISABLED_FLEET_SAFETY) for that pool.

Paging:
- Page on Stage B. Stage A is ticket/Slack alert.

Allison Grant: We need to be super careful with baseline definition to avoid the gate flapping during organic traffic changes.

Maybe define “expected non-burst latency” as a quantile from last 24h at same time-of-week? Might be overkill for v1.

Simpler v1: compare against a short rolling baseline but only when utilization isn’t changing too fast.
- Gate should ignore periods where non-burst QPS changes >X% in 5m (so we don’t clamp burst because a customer launched something on baseline).

Also: we should include a “tail amplification” signal. Like p99/p50 ratio for non-burst lanes. It tends to catch queuing pathologies earlier.

Peter Holtz: From routing side, it’s critical that the gate decision is deterministic at the pool level and cheap to evaluate.

If SRE gate says “disable burst in us-east/H100”, routing should not be doing per-request heuristics.

Also, I’d rather have the admission controller own this gate so denial reason codes are consistent.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1 on admission controller owning enforcement.

Implementation-wise we can push a “pool safety state” computed by telemetry/metrics pipeline:
- OK
- CLAMPED (cap override)
- DISABLED

Then admission controller checks that state before token bucket fairness logic.

But we need to define a cooldown/hysteresis so it doesn’t oscillate.

Mina Haddad: Routing can help with clean segmentation: we already tag requests with `capacity_lane=baseline|burst` at gateway.

So we can compute SLI for baseline-only by filtering on that tag.

Also: if burst is disabled, do we want routing to *stop trying* to burst quickly? (avoid retry storms)

Logan Wright: Metric taxonomy angle: we should standardize the SLI queries and labels now.

If we do per-tenant labels, cardinality will kill us. We should do pool-level only for paging.

Suggested labels: region, hw_class, capacity_lane, model_family (optional; maybe too high-card).

Allison Grant: Agree: pool-level for paging/gate. Per-tenant for dashboards only, sampled.

For SLO, we just need “non-burst tenants protected” at pool granularity.

Sean Gallagher: Ok, trying to make this concrete.

SLO definition (for gate):
- Scope: Dedicated baseline lane only (non-burst)
- Pool: region + hw_class
- SLI: request end-to-end latency at gateway (includes queue + runtime)
- Target: same as current Dedicated latency SLO (baseline)

Gate triggers (v1):
- Soft clamp if either:
  (a) nonburst_p95 > SLO_p95_threshold for 5m
  OR
  (b) (nonburst_p95 / nonburst_p95_baseline_30m) > 1.2 for 10m
- Hard disable if either:
  (a) nonburst_p99 > SLO_p99_threshold for 10m
  OR
  (b) (nonburst_p99 / nonburst_p99_baseline_30m) > 1.3 for 15m

Cooldown/hysteresis:
- Once clamped, hold for 30m after recovery before returning to OK.
- Once disabled, require manual ack OR 60m stable to re-enable (TBD; leaning manual for beta).

PromQL sketch (don’t bikeshed names yet):
```promql
nonburst_p95 = histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le,region,hw_class)(rate(rw_req_latency_seconds_bucket{capacity_lane="baseline"}[5m])))
baseline_p95_30m = avg_over_time(nonburst_p95[30m])
ratio = nonburst_p95 / baseline_p95_30m
```

Paging policy:
- Page SRE on hard disable OR if clamp persists >2h.
- Non-paging alert on soft clamp (to #eng-sre + create Jira).

Rafael Mendes: This looks reasonable. One thought: p99 can be super spiky. I like using the ratio check w/ longer window.

Also, make sure our alert text tells oncall what changed (“burst gate tripped for us-east/H100; state=DISABLED; reason=NONBURST_P99_REGRESSION”).

Peter Holtz: On retry storms: we should return 429 with explicit denial reason + `Retry-After` when gate is DISABLED.

If we just silently route to baseline, customers will think burst “works” but they’re just saturating baseline.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. Also admission should have a distinct denial reason code for the gate vs “pool saturated”. Helps support.

Mina Haddad: On routing side we can stop reattempting burst for N seconds once we get the gate denial reason. So we don’t hammer admission controller.

Allison Grant: For the SLI, gateway end-to-end is fine, but make sure we’re not mixing streaming vs non-streaming. Streaming “TTFT” vs full request latency changes shape.

Maybe define SLI as TTFT for streaming routes + standard latency for non-streaming? Might be too much for v1 though.

Sean Gallagher: Good catch. For v1 gate, I’d prefer one metric (TTFT) because that’s what customers feel first and it’s less tied to response length.

Let’s do:
- SLI: `time_to_first_token` for streaming
- Else: `time_to_first_byte`

And only gate on chat/generate endpoints (exclude embeddings for now) since they have different SLOs.

Logan Wright: That’s doable. We already emit `rw_req_ttft_seconds_bucket` and `rw_req_ttfb_seconds_bucket`.

We should confirm labeling is consistent across gateway + router. I’ll sanity check in staging.

Rafael Mendes: We also need to decide: is “hard disable” auto rollback? I’d like it to flip the region/hwclass allowlist flag off as well.

Sean Gallagher: Yeah. Suggest:
- Gate writes `burst_pool_state=DISABLED` to config store (or policy engine overrides)
- Release controller watches that and disables burst rollout flag for that pool (so new tenants don’t get it)

For beta: require manual re-enable via oncall runbook (reduce risk).

Peter Holtz: Works for me as long as routing reads the same source of truth.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll sync with Ethan/Jordan on where the “pool safety state” should live (policy engine vs config). Determinism requirement is important.

Allison Grant: Can we add a “neighbor impact canary” dashboard panel that shows baseline lane p95/p99 overlaid with burst utilization? It’ll help validate the gate isn’t tripping randomly.

Sean Gallagher: Yes. Let’s capture action items:
- Sean: draft SLO gate section + paging policy update in Dedicated burst SLO doc; share for review.
- Allison/Logan: confirm metric definitions + labels, propose final PromQL.
- Keiko/Peter/Mina: confirm enforcement path (admission controller + routing behavior + retry/backoff).

I’ll post doc link shortly.

Sean Gallagher: Doc stub up here (WIP): https://confluence.redwood.inference/display/ENGSRE/Dedicated+Burst+-+SLO+Gates+%2B+Alerting

Please comment esp on thresholds + cooldown + paging.

Rafael Mendes: Added comments on the manual re-enable + runbook requirement. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: Commented on TTFT vs TTFB and excluding embeddings. Also suggested adding an “error-rate guard” (timeouts) as secondary signal.

Peter Holtz: I’ll open a small routing change to honor `Retry-After` + cache gate denials for 30s. Should cut load.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll align with infra on whether “clamp” should lower max burst ratio globally or per-tenant. I think global first, then per-tenant as follow-up.

Sean Gallagher: Sounds good. Let’s aim to finalize thresholds by end of week so ENG can wire the gate behind feature flag for dogfood.

title: #eng-sre: 1738695410
preview:
Aisha Bello: hey all — need quick alignment on alert thresholds for the onboarding funnel + data freshness checks for the new KPI dashboard. goal is to catch regressions during cohort rollout without paging ourselves to death.

Aisha Bello: current draft alerts:
- TTFS p50 and p90 regression (vs 7d baseline)
- TTFS funnel drop-off spike at specific step(s)
- data freshness (events + derived tables)

Aisha Bello: would love SRE eyes on what should page vs ticket.

Sean Gallagher: :eyes: thanks. +1 on avoiding noisy pages. can you define what “regression” means here? % change? absolute minutes?

Chris Osei: metric defs we’re using in PM-751: TTFS = time from signup -> first successful 2xx completion (server-side) for hosted API. TTFS distribution is heavy-tailed.

Chris Osei: my suggestion was to alert on relative change in p50/p90 *and* sample size guardrail.

Allison Grant: +1 sample size guardrail. also: we should explicitly separate “true regression” vs “pipeline delay” because we already get tickets about usage dashboard lag (SUP-18544).

Logan Wright: on data freshness — we have two legs:
1) raw onboarding events ingestion (gateway -> pipeline)
2) derived dataset / dashboard materialization

Logan Wright: raw events are near-real-time (few mins). derived dataset is 15-min schedule right now.

Mei Lin: derived tables run every 15m but can slip if the warehouse queue backs up. when it slips, funnel charts look like drop-offs because last step completions don’t show.

Aisha Bello: exactly. so I’m thinking:
- freshness alert for raw events stream (lag > 10m)
- freshness alert for derived dataset (latest partition age > 45m)

Aisha Bello: page on raw stream? ticket on derived?

Sean Gallagher: raw stream lag > 10m should be page, yes. that breaks the “progress service” + checklist UI state, right?

Logan Wright: UI state is mostly from progress service, not warehouse. but progress service consumes the same events (internal ingestion) — if the gateway emission is busted, UI won’t advance.

Caleb Johnson: depends on the failure mode. we have:
- gateway emission missing (bad deploy)
- pipeline ingestion lag (queue)
- warehouse job lag

Caleb Johnson: I’d page for gateway emission missing OR internal ingestion lag. warehouse job lag is SEV0 for dashboards only (ticket).

Allison Grant: can we detect “gateway emission missing” directly? like event volume flatlines?

Logan Wright: yes. we can alert on event volume (per env/region) dropping below expected. but “expected” is tricky in low traffic cohorts.

Chris Osei: we can scope alerts to cohorts > N signups/day. during dogfood it’ll be tiny.

Dev Patel: adding support view here: we really care that checklist steps don’t regress/lie. if dashboard charts are delayed, support can live with it; if checklist UI shows no progress, we get slammed.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: so page on anything that impacts checklist correctness. ticket for analytics/reporting.

Aisha Bello: ok, proposed classification:
PAGER:
1) onboarding events ingestion lag > 10m (prod) OR no events for 10m in any active region (with N>threshold)
2) progress service error rate > 2% (5m) on `GET /checklist/progress` and `POST /internal/events`
3) “step regression” anomaly (step state decreases) above tiny threshold (might indicate ordering bug again)

TICKET:
A) derived dataset freshness > 45m
B) dashboard query errors / missing partitions
C) funnel step completion rate change alerts (monitor-only initially)

Sean Gallagher: step regression anomaly is interesting — how do we measure it?

Caleb Johnson: progress service can emit a counter when it receives an out-of-order event that would have caused a backward transition but gets ignored. after PR-28510 we should see ~0.

Logan Wright: we already log `monotonic_transition_violation` with tenant + step + event id. we can aggregate into a metric. (PII-free)

Allison Grant: careful on tenant cardinality. aggregate only.

Logan Wright: yep — aggregate by step + env + region only.

Mei Lin: on funnel drop-off spike — suggest we alert on *relative* step-to-step conversion drop, but only daily rollup. hourly is too noisy.

Chris Osei: agree. also require minimum denominator (e.g., >= 200 signups/day) before firing.

Aisha Bello: what thresholds do we like?
- TTFS p50 regression: +25% vs trailing 7d baseline for 2 hours?
- TTFS p90 regression: +30% vs baseline for 2 hours?

Sean Gallagher: percentages make sense but watch for baseline drift. maybe compare to same day-of-week? but MVP can be trailing 7d.

Allison Grant: add an absolute floor too. like if p50 jumps by > 5 minutes AND > 25%.

Chris Osei: +1. TTFS p50 can be like 2-3 min for healthy. 25% might be ~45s, could be noise.

Aisha Bello: ok:
TTFS p50: page only if (delta > 5m AND > 25%) for 2h, with sample size >= 100 signups in that window.
TTFS p90: page only if (delta > 10m AND > 30%) for 2h, sample size >= 100.

Logan Wright: note: signups aren’t in the events stream today; we infer cohort from auth tenant creation in control plane. but that’s in the warehouse. for near-real-time we can approximate with “new tenants with first request attempt” or just use event counts.

Mei Lin: yeah sample size gating in near-real-time might be hard. but we can still gate on raw event volume (e.g., `onboarding.first_success` count >= X).

Sean Gallagher: keep it simple. use `first_success` event count for gating.

Dev Patel: also can we add a non-paging alert for 401/403 spikes? that’s top onboarding pain.

Caleb Johnson: we already have gateway auth failure metrics. can add a monitor: if 401 rate for “new tenants” spikes. but “new tenants” segmentation is the hard part again.

Allison Grant: maybe start with overall 401 spikes as ticket, and once user-agent tagging lands (openai compat PR-265) we can filter onboarding flows.

Logan Wright: yep, the UA tags will help attribute. in the meantime, we can at least trend.

Aisha Bello: summarizing decisions:
- PAGE: raw onboarding event ingestion lag (>10m) + progress service error budget issues + monotonic violation spikes
- TICKET: derived dataset freshness (>45m), dashboard errors
- Monitor-only for now: funnel drop-off spikes + 401/429 spikes until segmentation improves

Aisha Bello: I’ll update the dashboard spec + alerts in the observability pack PR accordingly and loop back.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: sounds good. one more: add a “data quality” check for missing required fields (schema v1). that’s bitten us before.

Logan Wright: good call — we can alert on % of events failing validation > 0.5% (10m) as ticket (page if > 5%).

Allison Grant: +1. and please document in runbook what support should say when dashboard is behind vs when checklist is behind.

Dev Patel: I’ll add a note to support macros once we have the exact wording.

Aisha Bello: perfect. thanks all.

(DeployBot): Reminder: Observability Pack PR #199 updated 6 minutes ago by Aisha Bello (adds onboarding funnel dashboard + alert stubs).
text:

- TTFS funnel drop-off spike at specific step(s)
- data freshness (events + derived tables)

Aisha Bello: would love SRE eyes on what should page vs ticket.

Chris Osei: metric defs we’re using in PM-751: TTFS = time from signup -> first successful 2xx completion (server-side) for hosted API. TTFS distribution is heavy-tailed.

Chris Osei: my suggestion was to alert on relative change in p50/p90 *and* sample size guardrail.

Allison Grant: +1 sample size guardrail. also: we should explicitly separate “true regression” vs “pipeline delay” because we already get tickets about usage dashboard lag (SUP-18544).

Logan Wright: on data freshness — we have two legs:
1) raw onboarding events ingestion (gateway -> pipeline)
2) derived dataset / dashboard materialization

Logan Wright: raw events are near-real-time (few mins). derived dataset is 15-min schedule right now.

Mei Lin: derived tables run every 15m but can slip if the warehouse queue backs up. when it slips, funnel charts look like drop-offs because last step completions don’t show.

- pipeline ingestion lag (queue)
- warehouse job lag

title: #eng-sre: 1738637750
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock down *standard* gate thresholds + templates for Dedicated staged rollouts. Right now every rollout doc/thread has different numbers and we’re going to end up with noisy alerts + inconsistent promote decisions.

Sean Gallagher: Can we align on v1 defaults this week? At minimum: error rate, p95/p99 latency, saturation/headroom, OOM rate, 429/drop, GPU health. Plus: evaluation window + minimum sample size.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also want a clear stance on “per-customer variability” — we can’t tune per account in v1 or we’ll never scale oncall.

Peter Holtz: Agree. If we do fixed numbers that are too strict we’ll just stall rollouts forever (then operators force-promote and we lose safety). If too lax, gates won’t catch regressions. Need to tie to SLO + error budget.

Paula Mendes: (looping in) I’m drafting defaults for PM-827 but need SRE signoff. Question: should we template by “tier” (standard vs premium Dedicated) or by “pool size / throughput class” too?

Allison Grant: From observability side: we need gate queries that are stable + not overly sensitive to traffic spikes. Also keep cardinality low. If gate is “p99” with tiny sample sizes it’s going to flap.

Logan Wright: On telemetry: some metrics are 1–2m delayed depending on region + backpressure. If the gate windows are 5m and we require continuous data, we’ll hit “unknown” a lot.

Sean Gallagher: What I’d like:
- Standard templates per gate type
- Default thresholds by tier
- Minimum N requests *and* staleness detection
- Explicit “unknown” handling (fail-closed w/ manual override?)

Rafael Mendes: Re: unknown handling: fail-open makes me nervous. During telemetry impairment we’d basically be promoting blind.

Priya Natarajan: We already have leadership leaning toward fail-closed + override for missing metrics (w/ audit). But we need guardrails so we don’t block all rollouts during a telemetry incident.

Peter Holtz: Maybe policy:
- If gates are unknown due to telemetry staleness, state becomes “blocked: missing data” and requires human action (hold or force-promote)
- Force-promote requires admin + typed reason + ticket link

Paula Mendes: That maps to what product wants in Console: “Gate status: PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN” + show “why unknown” (stale, no samples, query error)

Allison Grant: Concrete suggestions:
- Error rate gate: use 5xx + model-runtime errors (exclude client 4xx) over rolling 10m, require >= 500 req for p95/p99 evaluation (?)
- Latency: p95 and p99 over 10m, compare *delta* vs baseline? (but baseline is hard)

Rafael Mendes: Baseline comparisons scare me for v1. Baseline shifts w/ time of day + workload mix. I’d rather do absolute ceilings tied to Dedicated SLO.

Sean Gallagher: Dedicated SLO for most pools is 99.9% and p95 < X ms (varies by model). We can’t bake model-specific thresholds into a generic gate template unless we key off the pool’s SLO config.

Mina Haddad: Routing perspective: stage partitions can reduce effective capacity, so we should have a “saturation/headroom” gate that catches queue depth spikes before latency melts.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. Fleet metrics we can provide reliably:
- GPU util (smoothed)
- queue depth / scheduler backlog
- OOM events per replica
- unhealthy GPU/node count (ECC, Xid, taints)
But note: small pools have noisy utilization; need “min replica count per stage” or we’ll get false saturation.

Irene Choi: Capacity guideline draft says canary stage should be at least 2 replicas per region for Dedicated, otherwise you’re basically single-node canary and you’ll see 429s if that node hiccups.

Peter Holtz: On 429/drop: should be a hard gate. If the stage introduces 429s above baseline, that’s a regression for customers.

Sean Gallagher: Proposed v1 gate set (defaults):
1) Error rate: (5xx + internal) / total <= 0.5% over 10m, min 1k req
2) 429 rate: <= 0.2% over 10m, min 1k req
3) p95 latency: <= SLO_p95 * 1.2 over 10m, min 500 req
4) p99 latency: <= SLO_p99 * 1.2 over 10m, min 500 req
5) Saturation/headroom: queue depth p95 <= threshold, GPU util p95 <= 90% (or headroom >= 10%)
6) OOM rate: 0 over 10m (or <= 1 per 10m?)
7) GPU health: unhealthy replicas <= 0 (or <= 1?)
Thoughts?

Rafael Mendes: I like tying latency to pool SLO instead of absolute ms. For error rate, 0.5% feels high for “gate” (that’s 99.5%). Maybe gate should be stricter than SLO? But we also want room for noise.

Allison Grant: If you use 10m windows, 0.5% on low traffic can still be 1–2 errors and looks scary. We need the min request count you listed, and maybe also min error count to avoid divide-by-small-N weirdness.

Logan Wright: Implementation detail: we can compute as rate() over 10m but we should also require “samples >= X” and “data fresh within 2m”. Otherwise it should be UNKNOWN not FAIL.

Paula Mendes: For MVP, can we ship with:
- Standard templates + default thresholds for “Standard Dedicated” and “Premium Dedicated”
- Pool config provides SLO_p95/p99
- Saturation gate uses queue depth + GPU util headroom
- OOM gate = 0 (strict)
- GPU health gate = 0 critical events (ECC/Xid)
And let customers request overrides via support (not self-serve)

Peter Holtz: OOM=0 might be too strict if we have occasional startup OOMs unrelated to steady state. Could define OOM rate threshold with exclusion window? E.g., ignore first 2 minutes after stage start.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. We see transient OOMs during warmup / KV cache sizing changes. If the gate runs immediately it will fail even if steady state is fine.

Sean Gallagher: Good call. Let’s define a “warmup period” per stage before gates evaluate. Ex: first 3m after promotion = collecting, then evaluate over next 10m.

Rafael Mendes: That also helps alert noise. Don’t page on expected warmup blips. But if warmup exceeds X minutes, mark as suspicious.

Allison Grant: If we do warmup, please make it explicit in dashboards (stage timeline annotation: “warming up”) so people don’t think gates are broken.

Priya Natarajan: Action items:
- Sean/Rafael: propose final default thresholds + window/sample/warmup rules (by EOD tomorrow)
- Paula: incorporate into PM-827 + PRD language “limited config”
- Logan/Allison: sanity check queries + staleness detection and confirm telemetry can support the required breakdowns
- Vanessa/Irene/Mina: confirm saturation + partitioning guardrails so small pools don’t fail by design

Sean Gallagher: Works. I’ll put a doc stub in SRE space + link here. Also: alerts routing — can we make gate FAIL create a ticket first (not page) unless it’s 429/error budget burn?

Rafael Mendes: +1. Page only if:
- gate fail implies active customer impact (429 spike, error rate spike) OR
- rollout is auto-aborting and we need someone to confirm capacity is stable
Otherwise ticket + Slack notification.

Logan Wright: Re: “error budget burn” gate — if we want that in v1, we need to define how to compute burn per pool reliably. Might be v2.

Paula Mendes: For v1, we can keep “error budget aware promotion” as a policy note (no promote during incident) but not a computed gate.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: as long as we standardize the queries and keep them consistent across Console and alerting.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. Expect a draft template shortly. Please pile on with concerns about thresholds being too aggressive/too loose for real Dedicated traffic.

Peter Holtz: One more: please ensure templates distinguish between *stage* metrics and *overall pool* metrics. We need to gate on canary partition signals, but also watch overall to avoid starving baseline traffic.

Mina Haddad: Yup — routing will emit stage_id tags for “candidate” vs “baseline” partitions. But we need a clear allowlist of labels or we’ll blow up cardinality again.

Allison Grant: Noted. We’ll keep dashboards at stage_id + region + pool_id only, no per-model-request tags.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Closing loop once draft is in.

title: #eng-sre: 1738607744
preview:
Aisha Bello: seeing something weird in the Dedicated throughput SLO dashboard for streaming routes. tokens/sec looks ~1.8–2.2x higher than non-streaming for same deployment/model. feels like we’re double counting output tokens per request when streaming. anyone else seeing this?

Sergio Costa: yeah, I noticed the “tokens_out_per_s” panel spikes exactly when customers flip `stream=true`. query is based on `redwood.inference.tokens_out_total` / `redwood.inference.request_duration_seconds`. if tokens_out_total is inflated, the ratio goes nuts.

Sean Gallagher: +1 this will break the SLO evaluator if the canonical metric is tokens/sec. can we confirm what we emit for streaming? do we count per chunk or per request?

Logan Wright: there are two different paths:
- non-streaming: we emit `tokens_out` once on finalize
- streaming: we emit `tokens_out` *per chunk* today (because chunks are “responses” in the gateway layer), and then we also emit a finalize event. so if the metric pipeline sums all those, it’s basically (sum chunk tokens) + (final total tokens). :facepalm:

Rafael Mendes: oh lol. that explains why burn-rate alerts are noisy when customers are mostly streaming.

Aisha Bello: can you point me to where that’s instrumented? trying to understand if it’s just the gateway or runtime too.

Logan Wright: gateway. there’s a counter increment in the SSE writer and another increment on request end. historically we wanted “tokens delivered so far” for progress charts, but we used the same metric name/labels.

Sergio Costa: is it actually “chunk tokens” or “cumulative tokens so far” per chunk?

Logan Wright: cumulative, which makes it worse. each chunk is `tokens_emitted_so_far` not `delta_tokens`. so if you sum across chunks you get triangular numbers.

Sean Gallagher: ok so three problems:
1) streaming path emits multiple points
2) it emits cumulative not delta
3) we also emit finalize total
=> any downstream sum will be wrong.

Owen Phillips: I pulled an example from a customer trace. request outputs 420 tokens total.
SSE chunks:
- chunk1 reports 60
- chunk2 reports 140
- chunk3 reports 260
- chunk4 reports 420
finalize reports 420
If you sum: 60+140+260+420+420 = 1300 tokens. :grimacing:

Aisha Bello: yep, that would give ~3x on average depending on chunk count.

Rafael Mendes: impact: any throughput view that sums `tokens_out_total` over window will be inflated; also per-request derived metrics will be bad. SLO alerting depends on windowed throughput floor, so this is customer-facing if we start sharing SLO stats.

Cole Summers: does this also touch billing metering? please tell me no.

Logan Wright: billing uses metering events from the control plane, not this gateway metric. different pipeline. so invoices should be fine.

Cole Summers: :thumbsup: ok.

Sean Gallagher: recommendation: define *one* canonical counter for SLO: `tokens_out_final_total` emitted once per request completion, tagged `stream=true/false` but same semantics. and if we want streaming progress metrics, emit a *gauge* like `tokens_out_emitted_so_far` or a delta counter under a different name.

Sergio Costa: +1. also dashboard should switch to the canonical counter so we don’t get whiplash with stream.

Aisha Bello: quick stopgap: can we update the SLO evaluator to ignore `stream=true` until fix lands?

Sean Gallagher: maybe but that’ll exclude a big chunk of Dedicated traffic. better to fix the metric semantics quickly + backfill? (backfill optional)

Logan Wright: I can patch today:
- remove increment on SSE chunk path for the canonical counter
- keep a separate gauge (or counter delta) behind new name
- ensure finalize emits exact output token count once
Also add a unit test that simulates 4 chunks and asserts only one increment.

Owen Phillips: we also need to check tokenization source. are we counting “model tokens generated” or “UTF-8 bytes / token approximation”? if streaming uses a different codepath, we could have another discrepancy.

Logan Wright: both streaming and non-streaming call the same tokenizer util on the final assembled text. the bug is purely the repetition/cumulative increments.

Rafael Mendes: can we validate with a quick prom query?
Something like:
`sum by (deployment_id)(increase(redwood_inference_tokens_out_total{stream="true"}[5m])) / sum by (deployment_id)(increase(redwood_inference_requests_total{stream="true"}[5m]))`
should roughly equal avg output tokens. today it’ll be ~2-3x for streamed.

Sergio Costa: yeah, I ran that and got avg_out_tokens ~1200 on a workload that’s usually ~450. matches Owen’s example.

Aisha Bello: ok action items:
- Logan patch instrumentation + rename/add new metric for progress
- Sergio update dashboards to use the finalized-only counter
- Sean I’ll update the SLO doc draft to explicitly say “output tokens counted once at request completion” and note streaming is not chunk-summed
- Rafael can you help me tune alerts once fixed? want to avoid alert storm when the metric drops by 2x overnight.

Rafael Mendes: yep. once fix deploys, baseline shifts. we should temporarily silence burn-rate alerts for the Dedicated throughput SLO for ~1h while we confirm. or at least set a maintenance window.

Sean Gallagher: also please add a note in the incident/alert runbook: if tokens/sec suddenly changes by ~2x for streaming-heavy customers, check metric version.

Logan Wright: I’ll open a PR and link here.

Logan Wright: PR up: `pr-19422` “fix throughput metric double-counting for streaming” (removes chunk increments, adds `tokens_out_emitted_so_far` gauge). needs review from SRE + obs. https://github.com/redwood/pr-19422

Sergio Costa: reviewing now. dashboard change will follow once metric is in.

Aisha Bello: thanks. once merged, can we roll behind feature flag? want to keep ability to compare old/new for a bit.

Logan Wright: I put it behind `metrics.streaming_tokens_progress_separate=true` and the canonical counter change is unconditional (but just makes it correct). we can also add a temporary metric `tokens_out_total_legacy_streaming` if you need comparison, but I’d rather not keep it around.

Sean Gallagher: no need for legacy metric. if anyone asks, we’ll explain old metric was incorrect for streaming. SLO reporting should use corrected semantics.

Rafael Mendes: :+1: ship it.

Sergio Costa: merged dashboard PR will switch to `tokens_out_final_total` once deployed. will also add a small annotation on the panel “streaming accounting fix deployed” so we can correlate.

Aisha Bello: cool. I’ll mark the current alert noise as metric issue, not real throughput regression. once this is live we’ll re-evaluate thresholds.

Owen Phillips: one more thing: does “finalize” happen on client disconnect? if client cancels mid-stream, do we emit finalize tokens_out? might affect SLO if lots of cancels.

Logan Wright: good catch. finalize happens on server completion; if client disconnects, we may stop generation early depending on config. in that case finalize emits whatever was generated before cancel. that’s intended.

Sean Gallagher: and SLO excludes customer-initiated cancels anyway (we can tag `termination_reason=client_disconnect` and exclude in evaluator).

Aisha Bello: ok closing the loop. will watch deploy + post graphs here.

Rafael Mendes: :eyes: ping when it hits prod, I’ll keep an eye on burn-rate.

Aisha Bello: deploy bot says it’s in us-east canary. tokens/sec for one canary deployment dropped ~2.1x (expected). no more weird spikes when `stream=true`. :thumbsup:
text:
Aisha Bello: seeing something weird in the Dedicated throughput SLO dashboard for streaming routes. tokens/sec looks ~1.8–2.2x higher than non-streaming for same deployment/model. feels like we’re double counting output tokens per request when streaming. anyone else seeing this?

Sergio Costa: yeah, I noticed the “tokens_out_per_s” panel spikes exactly when customers flip `stream=true`. query is based on `redwood.inference.tokens_out_total` / `redwood.inference.request_duration_seconds`. if tokens_out_total is inflated, the ratio goes nuts.

Sean Gallagher: +1 this will break the SLO evaluator if the canonical metric is tokens/sec. can we confirm what we emit for streaming? do we count per chunk or per request?

Logan Wright: there are two different paths:
- non-streaming: we emit `tokens_out` once on finalize
- streaming: we emit `tokens_out` *per chunk* today (because chunks are “responses” in the gateway layer), and then we also emit a finalize event. so if the metric pipeline sums all those, it’s basically (sum chunk tokens) + (final total tokens). :facepalm:

Rafael Mendes: oh lol. that explains why burn-rate alerts are noisy when customers are mostly streaming.

Aisha Bello: can you point me to where that’s instrumented? trying to understand if it’s just the gateway or runtime too.

Logan Wright: gateway. there’s a counter increment in the SSE writer and another increment on request end. historically we wanted “tokens delivered so far” for progress charts, but we used the same metric name/labels.

Sergio Costa: is it actually “chunk tokens” or “cumulative tokens so far” per chunk?

Logan Wright: cumulative, which makes it worse. each chunk is `tokens_emitted_so_far` not `delta_tokens`. so if you sum across chunks you get triangular numbers.

Sean Gallagher: ok so three problems:
1) streaming path emits multiple points
2) it emits cumulative not delta
3) we also emit finalize total
=> any downstream sum will be wrong.

Owen Phillips: I pulled an example from a customer trace. request outputs 420 tokens total.
SSE chunks:
- chunk1 reports 60
- chunk2 reports 140
- chunk3 reports 260
- chunk4 reports 420
finalize reports 420
If you sum: 60+140+260+420+420 = 1300 tokens. :grimacing:

Aisha Bello: yep, that would give ~3x on average depending on chunk count.

Rafael Mendes: impact: any throughput view that sums `tokens_out_total` over window will be inflated; also per-request derived metrics will be bad. SLO alerting depends on windowed throughput floor, so this is customer-facing if we start sharing SLO stats.

Cole Summers: does this also touch billing metering? please tell me no.

Logan Wright: billing uses metering events from the control plane, not this gateway metric. different pipeline. so invoices should be fine.

Cole Summers: :thumbsup: ok.

Sean Gallagher: recommendation: define *one* canonical counter for SLO: `tokens_out_final_total` emitted once per request completion, tagged `stream=true/false` but same semantics. and if we want streaming progress metrics, emit a *gauge* like `tokens_out_emitted_so_far` or a delta counter under a different name.

Sergio Costa: +1. also dashboard should switch to the canonical counter so we don’t get whiplash with stream.

Aisha Bello: quick stopgap: can we update the SLO evaluator to ignore `stream=true` until fix lands?

Sean Gallagher: maybe but that’ll exclude a big chunk of Dedicated traffic. better to fix the metric semantics quickly + backfill? (backfill optional)

Logan Wright: I can patch today:
- remove increment on SSE chunk path for the canonical counter
- keep a separate gauge (or counter delta) behind new name
- ensure finalize emits exact output token count once
Also add a unit test that simulates 4 chunks and asserts only one increment.

Owen Phillips: we also need to check tokenization source. are we counting “model tokens generated” or “UTF-8 bytes / token approximation”? if streaming uses a different codepath, we could have another discrepancy.

Logan Wright: both streaming and non-streaming call the same tokenizer util on the final assembled text. the bug is purely the repetition/cumulative increments.

Rafael Mendes: can we validate with a quick prom query?
Something like:
`sum by (deployment_id)(increase(redwood_inference_tokens_out_total{stream="true"}[5m])) / sum by (deployment_id)(increase(redwood_inference_requests_total{stream="true"}[5m]))`
should roughly equal avg output tokens. today it’ll be ~2-3x for streamed.

Sergio Costa: yeah, I ran that and got avg_out_tokens ~1200 on a workload that’s usually ~450. matches Owen’s example.

Aisha Bello: ok action items:
- Logan patch instrumentation + rename/add new metric for progress
- Sergio update dashboards to use the finalized-only counter
- Sean I’ll update the SLO doc draft to explicitly say “output tokens counted once at request completion” and note streaming is not chunk-summed
- Rafael can you help me tune alerts once fixed? want to avoid alert storm when the metric drops by 2x overnight.

Rafael Mendes: yep. once fix deploys, baseline shifts. we should temporarily silence burn-rate alerts for the Dedicated throughput SLO for ~1h while we confirm. or at least set a maintenance window.

Sean Gallagher: also please add a note in the incident/alert runbook: if tokens/sec suddenly changes by ~2x for streaming-heavy customers, check metric version.

Logan Wright: I’ll open a PR and link here.

Logan Wright: PR up: `pr-19422` “fix throughput metric double-counting for streaming” (removes chunk increments, adds `tokens_out_emitted_so_far` gauge). needs review from SRE + obs. https://github.com/redwood/pr-19422

Sergio Costa: reviewing now. dashboard change will follow once metric is in.

Aisha Bello: thanks. once merged, can we roll behind feature flag? want to keep ability to compare old/new for a bit.

Logan Wright: I put it behind `metrics.streaming_tokens_progress_separate=true` and the canonical counter change is unconditional (but just makes it correct). we can also add a temporary metric `tokens_out_total_legacy_streaming` if you need comparison, but I’d rather not keep it around.

Sean Gallagher: no need for legacy metric. if anyone asks, we’ll explain old metric was incorrect for streaming. SLO reporting should use corrected semantics.

Rafael Mendes: :+1: ship it.

Sergio Costa: merged dashboard PR will switch to `tokens_out_final_total` once deployed. will also add a small annotation on the panel “streaming accounting fix deployed” so we can correlate.

Aisha Bello: cool. I’ll mark the current alert noise as metric issue, not real throughput regression. once this is live we’ll re-evaluate thresholds.

Sean Gallagher: and SLO excludes customer-initiated cancels anyway (we can tag `termination_reason=client_disconnect` and exclude in evaluator).
