title: #eng-sre: 1738436709
preview:
Sean Gallagher: For the Private backup/restore dashboards (PR-94), what do we actually want to graph + alert on? I keep coming back to `last_success_ts`, `duration`, `bytes`, but we need to avoid paging during planned maintenance windows / upgrades where backups are intentionally paused.

Allison Grant: Yep, those are the right primitives.
For graphs, I’d do 4 panels:
1) “Time since last successful backup” (computed)
2) Duration p50/p95 (or last duration if it’s periodic)
3) Backup size bytes (last + trend)
4) Failure reasons (stacked by error_category)

Allison Grant: For alerting, avoid alerting directly on `success==0` (too noisy). Prefer “no success within expected interval”.
If we have `*_last_success_timestamp_seconds`, alert on `time() - last_success > (interval + grace)`.

Sergio Costa: Agree. In the metrics PR we’ve got:
- `redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{cluster,namespace,backup_type}`
- `redwood_private_backup_duration_seconds_bucket{...}` (hist)
- `redwood_private_backup_bytes_total{...}` (counter; increments per artifact)
- `redwood_private_backup_runs_total{status="success"|"failure", error_category=...}`

Sergio Costa: For Grafana, I was planning a stat panel:
`time() - max(redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{backup_type="control_plane"})`
then show as “age”.

Neha Kapoor: +1 on age-of-last-success.
Re: maintenance windows: we need a consistent signal for “don’t page”. Two options:
1) Alertmanager silences / scheduled maintenance
2) A metric we can inhibit on (maintenance mode)
I prefer (2) for determinism in customer envs, but we should support both.

Bruno Silva: Helm chart already has a `backup.cronjob.suspend` value (we set it during some upgrades). We could expose that as a metric via the installer/operator, but today it’s just k8s.
Maybe easiest is: if CronJob is suspended OR scaled to 0, inhibit “backup overdue”.

Sean Gallagher: How would we do “CronJob suspended” in Prom without kube-state-metrics? (some customers don’t have full kube metrics)

Rafael Mendes: For Redwood-managed observability pack we do ship kube-state-metrics in most reference installs, but not guaranteed for air-gapped minimal installs.
We should have a Redwood-owned signal too.
Maybe the installer writes a ConfigMap + exporter emits `redwood_maintenance_mode{component="backup"}=1`.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: during upgrades we *do* intentionally pause scheduled backups sometimes. I’d rather not rely on humans to silence.
If we can make the installer set `maintenance_mode` at the start of upgrade and unset at the end, that’s ideal.

Allison Grant: +1.
Design: an inhibition rule in Alertmanager:
- source: `redwood_private_maintenance_mode{scope="backup"} == 1`
- target: `PrivateBackupOverdue` (and maybe also `PrivateBackupJobFailuresHigh`)

Sergio Costa: We don’t have `redwood_private_maintenance_mode` today, but we can at least build the dashboard with annotations for “maintenance enabled” once it exists.
For now, could do “soft” suppression: only page if overdue for 2x interval.

Sean Gallagher: I’m okay with 2x interval + grace as a stopgap.
Something like:

```
# expected: nightly => 24h
(time() - max(redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{backup_type="control_plane"})) > (48 * 3600)
```

But we should also catch “backup is running but stuck” (duration insane).

Allison Grant: For “stuck”: alert on duration > threshold, but careful: backup duration varies w/ object store.
Maybe:
- warn if p95 duration > 2h (over last 7d)
- page if a single run exceeds hard cap (e.g., 6h)
If you only have histograms, you can alert on max of last-run duration if we emit a gauge too.

Sergio Costa: We currently emit a gauge for last run duration:
`redwood_private_backup_last_duration_seconds{backup_type}`
I can add it to the dashboard if helpful.

Neha Kapoor: Yes please. “Last run duration” is super useful for on-call.
Also: bytes. I’d like a sanity check alert for “bytes == 0” or “no bytes increase” when we *think* a backup ran.
But we need to avoid false positives when there were no changes.

Rafael Mendes: I’d avoid “no bytes increase” (state can be stable). But “artifact missing / manifest missing” we can do.
If the backup run emits `runs_total{status="success"}` but `bytes_total` didn’t move at all, that’s suspicious. However counters can be reset on restarts.
Maybe better: emit `redwood_private_backup_last_artifact_bytes` gauge.

Bruno Silva: In the installer `backup create` output we already compute size pre-upload.
Should be easy to export `last_artifact_bytes` and `last_artifact_sha256_ok` (0/1).
That helps both dashboard and the restore validation tooling.

Sean Gallagher: Ok, dashboard proposal (v1):
- Age since last success (stat + time series)
- Last run duration (stat + time series)
- Artifact size (last_artifact_bytes) (stat + time series)
- Runs by status (success/failure) w/ error_category
- Table: last 10 runs w/ duration + bytes + error_category (if we can)

Sean Gallagher: Alert proposal (v1):
1) `PrivateBackupOverdue` (page) if age > 2x interval + 1h grace
2) `PrivateBackupStuck` (page) if last_duration > 6h
3) `PrivateBackupFailuresHigh` (warn) if failures in last 24h > N
Then later add inhibition with `maintenance_mode`.

Allison Grant: Looks good.
One detail: “interval” isn’t always 24h; some customers do every 6h. Can we label expected interval?
If not, we can document that alert threshold is configurable in values.

Sergio Costa: In the observability pack we can template the threshold (Helm values):
`backup.expectedIntervalSeconds` + `backup.overdueMultiplier` + `backup.graceSeconds`.
Default: expected=86400, multiplier=2, grace=3600.

Connor O'Brien: Works. Release can set `expectedIntervalSeconds` during bringup.
Also please annotate on dashboard that restore drills may disable backups in staging (so people don’t freak out).

Neha Kapoor: Re: false positives during maintenance windows — we should also add a runbook note:
- If doing planned maintenance: set `maintenance_mode` (once shipped) OR create an AM silence.
- Validate backups post-maintenance by running an on-demand `backup create`.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Sergio can you add a “Maintenance / silenced” note to the dashboard header and link to the runbook section?

Sergio Costa: Yep. I’ll add a text panel at top:
- expected interval
- alert thresholds
- “If you’re in a maintenance window, silence `PrivateBackupOverdue`”
And later switch that guidance to “enable maintenance mode” once we have the metric.

Rafael Mendes: Also: for maintenance windows we should avoid pages during *installer-driven upgrades* specifically.
If Connor’s team can emit a metric during upgrade, that’s the cleanest. Let’s track as a follow-up in ENG.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll file a ticket for `redwood_private_maintenance_mode` metric + AM inhibition rule.
In the meantime we’ll ship the 2x interval gating.

Allison Grant: Sounds good. When you build the “age” panel, please show it in hours + color thresholds (green < interval, yellow < 2x, red >= 2x). Helps a lot during incidents.

Sergio Costa: done. Will post screenshots in #eng-sre once I’ve got the next dashboard revision.

Sean Gallagher: thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1738362044
preview:
Rafael Mendes: heads up, the `RouteExperiments / Attribution drop rate high` alert has paged 6x in the last ~24h and every time it’s been noise. can we tune this before it burns out on-call?

Grafana Alert Bot: FIRING: RouteExperimentsAttributionDropRateHigh
- env=prod
- cluster=router-us-east-1
- drop_rate=3.4%
- window=5m
- labels: service=router, metric=attribution_drop_rate
- dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/route-experiments/guardrails?var=attribution
- runbook: (missing)

PagerDuty: Triggered incident: [SEV3] RouteExperimentsAttributionDropRateHigh (prod/router-us-east-1)

Sean Gallagher: +1, saw this twice during my rotation too. what’s the actual failure mode we’re trying to catch here? missing `experiment_id/variant_id` tags entirely, or just sampling/aggregation jitter?

Logan Wright: it’s supposed to catch “we’re running experiments but attribution tags aren’t making it to OTel spans / metrics rollups”. but right now it’s basically measuring *any* missing tags at router level, even when no experiment is active on that route.

Caleb Johnson: also we changed tagging strategy last week (reduced cardinality) so a bunch of spans don’t carry expanded tags unless sampled. that makes the numerator/denominator funky for small windows.

Allison Grant: looking at the rule: it’s `sum(rate(requests_missing_experiment_dims[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[5m])) > 0.02`. no min-traffic gate, no “experiment-enabled” filter, and 5m is pretty spiky.

Aisha Bello: :eyes: yeah 5m will flap hard. can we add `for: 20m` and a min requests threshold? (e.g., only evaluate when requests_total > 200/min)

Mei Lin: +1 on min traffic. also, can we compute drop rate only for requests that *should* have experiment dims? e.g., `experiment_assigned=true` or `experiment_id != ""` on the request context.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine side, we can expose a boolean like `routing.experiment_applied` in logs/spans. not sure if it’s already a metric label.

Logan Wright: we have `experiment_active` in the request context now (set when the route has an experiment config and the evaluator ran). in OTel it’s `rw.experiment.active` attribute, but i don’t think it’s in the metrics series.

Caleb Johnson: metrics pipeline currently only keeps a restricted label set (to avoid cardinality blowups). `rw.experiment.active` got dropped by the relabel rules. we could re-add it as a low-cardinality dimension (bool).

Allison Grant: ok so immediate tuning options without pipeline changes:
1) longer window (`[30m]`)
2) add `for: 15m`
3) bump severity: warn only (no pager)
4) add min-traffic gate via `sum(rate(requests_total[5m])) > X`

Sean Gallagher: i’d do (1)+(2)+(4) and keep paging only if it’s *really* bad. right now 2% missing for 5m isn’t actionable.

Rafael Mendes: agree. what should thresholds be? we’ve been seeing 1–4% “missing” even when everything is fine.

Logan Wright: based on staging analysis + prod spot checks:
- baseline “expected missing due to sampling/retries”: ~0.5–1.5%
- when the streaming retry bug was present we saw 8–15% sustained
so maybe:
- WARN if >3% for 30m (and total_rps > some min)
- CRIT/page if >8% for 15m

Aisha Bello: works. and i’d prefer routing WARN to #eng-sre only, CRIT pages.

Allison Grant: +1. also add a separate alert for “variant 0 traffic” or “attribution == 0 when experiment enabled” since that’s the real scary one.

Caleb Johnson: we do have `experiment_assignment_count` (from evaluator). could make a ratio: if assignments > 0 but `requests_with_experiment_dims` ~ 0 => attribution pipeline broken.

Sean Gallagher: that’s the right signal. can we ship a quick patch now and follow up with a better signal next week?

Rafael Mendes: yes please. i just want the pager noise down today.

Allison Grant: proposing concrete rule changes for now:
- expression: `drop_rate = missing / total`
- evaluate over `[30m]`
- add `and sum(rate(requests_total[5m])) > 50` (approx >50 rps aggregated)
- WARN: drop_rate > 0.03 `for: 30m`
- CRIT: drop_rate > 0.08 `for: 15m`
- set WARN contact point = Slack only
- CRIT contact point = PD + Slack

Logan Wright: 50 rps aggregated might still be low for some clusters, but ok as a first pass. alternatively use absolute counts: `missing > 1000` in 30m. either way reduces low-traffic flaps.

Aisha Bello: can we gate by absolute counts *and* ratio? e.g., `drop_rate > 0.03 and missing_rate > 1 rps`.

Allison Grant: yup, we can do `sum(rate(missing[5m])) > 1` as a second gate.

Sean Gallagher: do that. if we’re missing <1 rps it’s not worth paging anyone.

Rafael Mendes: +1. also can we add runbook link in the alert payload? the bot message says runbook missing.

Allison Grant: yep, will update the alert annotations with the runbook URL (the debugging runbook in confluence) and include the “top clusters/routes” panel link.

Caleb Johnson: heads up: the metric name in prom is `rw_router_requests_missing_experiment_dims_total` (counter), not `requests_missing_experiment_dims`. make sure the query matches the actual series.

Allison Grant: thx. i’ll update in the observability-pack PR and sync with Rafael for review.

Jordan Lee: side note: the flaps also correlate with deploys because we drop some spans during restarts. longer window + for should help.

Sean Gallagher: ok action items:
- Allison: patch alert rule + add runbook link, change WARN->Slack only
- Rafael: once merged, announce in #eng-sre and note new paging threshold
- Logan/Caleb: follow-up ticket to add `experiment_active` / assignment-based alert (better signal)

Allison Grant: on it. will open PR shortly.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. pls also set initial severity to SEV4/warn unless it crosses the CRIT threshold. on-call is getting hammered.

Allison Grant: ack. updating routing so only CRIT triggers PD.

Grafana Alert Bot: RESOLVED: RouteExperimentsAttributionDropRateHigh
- env=prod
- cluster=router-us-east-1
- previous_drop_rate=3.4%

Sean Gallagher: :pray: let’s get the tuned rules in before the next deploy train.

Allison Grant: PR up: https://github.com/redwood/observability-pack/pull/1942 (tunes windows, adds min missing-rate gate, adds runbook annotation). please review.

Rafael Mendes: reviewed + approved. LGTM.

Allison Grant: merged. will roll out with next grafana rules sync (~15 min).

Aisha Bello: can we also backtest the new thresholds vs last 7d? just to make sure we’re not blinding ourselves.

Mei Lin: i can pull a quick query on historical counters and estimate how often it would have fired. will drop results in thread.

Mei Lin: quick backtest (last 7d, prod):
- old rule (>2% over 5m): would have fired ~38 times across clusters
- new WARN (>3% over 30m + missing_rate>1rps): would have fired 4 times (all during the streaming retry incident window)
- new CRIT (>8% over 15m + missing_rate>1rps): would have fired 1 time (same incident)
seems good.

Sean Gallagher: perfect. ship it.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: if anyone sees remaining noise, tag me + @Allison Grant. next step is the assignment-based alert so we’re not measuring “missing when no experiment”.

title: #eng-sre: 1738283440
preview:
Sean Gallagher: getting a bunch of pages from the unit-econ pipeline alerts overnight. feels noisy vs actionable. can we tune thresholds before this hits broader dogfood?

PagerDuty: [P1] Alert triggered: unit_econ_hourly_completeness_low (completeness=0.972 threshold=0.99) | service=unit-econ-pipeline | runbook=https://go.redwood/runbooks/unit-econ#completeness

Allison Grant: yeah saw that too. current rule is paging on *any* hourly completeness < 0.99 for any dimension combo. that’s basically guaranteed to flap when a region is late.

Aisha Bello: +1. we’re effectively alerting on the most granular series. we should alert on aggregate coverage first, then optionally surface drilldowns in the dashboard.

Logan Wright: agree. a single region lagging ingestion for 10 min shouldn’t page SRE. also worth noting we had a backfill running ~02:00 that delayed the hourly job.

Mei Lin: the warehouse job didn’t fail, it just finished at :37 instead of :12. freshness alert fired because it expects tables updated by :20.

Sean Gallagher: ok, can we align on severity levels?
- page: metric materially unusable for exec/finance reporting
- slack: partial degradation / investigate during biz hours

Caleb Johnson: proposed:
1) freshness: page only if last_successful_hour < now-2h (i.e., >2 hours behind) for 2 consecutive evals
2) completeness: page only if total output_tokens completeness < 0.98 across *all* regions+tiers OR if any required dimension missing entirely for >3h
3) anomaly: drop alerts should be slack-only unless >30% drop AND persists 2 hours

Nadia Rahman: for completeness, can we avoid tying paging to quantization_profile coverage? it’s new and we’re still enforcing allowed values; will be spiky during rollouts.

Allison Grant: yeah quant_profile should be “diagnostic” not “gating” for now. gate on region + tier + model (coarse) only.

Aisha Bello: also current completeness calc includes streaming + non-streaming. streaming parity fixes just rolled out. I’d rather page on *aggregate output_tokens* + *rows written* than sub-dimensions.

Sean Gallagher: what’s the current prometheus / grafana rule? can someone paste?

Aisha Bello: from observability-pack (simplified):
```
# pages
alert: UnitEconCompletenessLow
expr: unit_econ_completeness_ratio < 0.99
for: 10m
labels:
  severity: page
annotations:
  summary: "Unit econ completeness below 99%"

alert: UnitEconFreshnessLag
expr: (time() - unit_econ_last_success_ts) > 3600
for: 5m
labels:
  severity: page
```
It’s too aggressive on both.

Logan Wright: + we compute completeness_ratio per (model, region, tier, streaming_flag, quant_profile). that’s why it fires constantly.

Mei Lin: yep. when any single series has 0 tokens for an hour (e.g., dedicated tier in ap-south), completeness ratio drops.

Allison Grant: suggestion: calculate completeness at the *hour bucket* level (all dims collapsed) as:
`sum(observed_output_tokens) / sum(expected_output_tokens)` where expected comes from gateway request counts * avg tokens? but we don’t have expected super solid.

Caleb Johnson: we already have a simpler invariant: number of raw metering events ingested vs number aggregated. plus: “required dimensions coverage” check in the aggregation job.

Nadia Rahman: +1 to using ingestion lag + aggregation success + required dims present. “expected tokens” gets philosophical fast.

Sean Gallagher: ok, let’s do practical tuning for v1.

Aisha Bello: proposal v1 alerting matrix:
- Freshness (tables updated):
  - SEV=page if lag > 2h for 15m
  - SEV=slack if lag > 1h for 15m
- Completeness (hour buckets present):
  - SEV=page if missing_hours >= 2 in last 6h (for the *global* aggregate table)
  - SEV=slack if missing_hours >= 1 in last 6h
- Dimension coverage (region/tier/model):
  - SEV=slack only, and only if missing for 3 consecutive hours
- Token drop anomaly:
  - SEV=slack only unless drop > 40% AND persists 2h AND not during backfill window

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: also add a “backfill in progress” mute. we can set a metric/label from the backfill job and use it as inhibition.

Logan Wright: we can emit `unit_econ_backfill_running{env="prod"}=1` when the backfill tool is active. also we know the job name.

Mei Lin: can also set a warehouse maintenance window label; backfills can be throttled but will still impact freshness.

Sean Gallagher: can we avoid hardcoding windows? I’d rather inhibit on the backfill_running metric.

Caleb Johnson: yep. inhibition rule:
```
inhibit_rules:
- source_match:
    alertname: UnitEconBackfillRunning
  target_match_re:
    alertname: UnitEcon(FreshnessLag|CompletenessLow|TokenDropAnomaly)
  equal: [env]
```

Aisha Bello: we don’t currently have UnitEconBackfillRunning alert, but we can add a non-paging one purely for inhibition.

Nadia Rahman: pls make sure it doesn’t mask real outages when backfill metric gets stuck at 1.

Allison Grant: add TTL: backfill_running only emitted while tool is running; plus alert if it’s been 1 for >8h (slack-only).

Sean Gallagher: sounds good.

Aisha Bello: on completeness definition: do we agree to move it from per-dimension ratio to “missing hour buckets” check?

Caleb Johnson: yes. for v1, “missing hour buckets in mv_unit_econ_hourly_global” is the closest to “metric is unusable.”

Logan Wright: and it matches what Finance cares about for QTD rollups.

Sean Gallagher: ok, action items:
- @Aisha update rules in observability-pack PR (severity split, new inhibition)
- @Caleb update runbook section re: alert meanings + how to confirm missing hours
- @Logan / @Mei add backfill_running metric from tool

Aisha Bello: ack. will target today. also will adjust `for:` durations so we don’t flap on transient lags.

Allison Grant: please also update the annotation text. right now the page just says “completeness below 99%” with no context. would be good to include last_success_hour + which table.

Aisha Bello: yep, will add. example:
"hourly global table lagging by {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}; last_success_hour={{ $labels.last_hour }}"

PagerDuty: Incident resolved: unit_econ_hourly_completeness_low (auto-resolved)

Sean Gallagher: perfect example of noise. let’s get the changes in before next oncall rotation.

Mei Lin: fyi: we also saw timezone boundary weirdness in Finance view yesterday. shouldn’t page on that, but we may want a slack warning if day-boundary mismatch spikes.

Caleb Johnson: agreed but that’s more of a data-quality check than SRE paging. can add to the “confidence” indicator later.

Sean Gallagher: cool. let’s keep paging scope tight for now.

Aisha Bello: I’ll post the PR link here once up.

Aisha Bello: PR is up in observability-pack: "tune unit econ pipeline alerts (noise reduction)". changes:
- freshness page at >2h, slack at >1h
- completeness now checks missing hour buckets in global MV
- dimension coverage slack-only, 3h consecutive
- inhibition on backfill_running
will ask Sean/Allison for review.

Allison Grant: reviewing now.

Sean Gallagher: reviewed, left 2 comments (wording + ensure `for: 15m` on page).

Logan Wright: backfill_running metric will land in the backfill tool tomorrow AM. will update thread when merged.

Aisha Bello: merged alert changes. will deploy with tonight’s observability-pack rollout.

Sean Gallagher: thanks all. if we still see pages overnight we’ll revisit thresholds, but this should cut >80% of it.

title: #eng-sre: 1737839022
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Heads up: proposing a game day next week to simulate *experiment config propagation lag* and verify alerts + runbooks + rollback steps before we broaden beta. Goal is to reproduce the staging “stale config” behavior on demand and make sure on-call muscle memory is there.

Sean Gallagher: Suggested window: Thu 10:00–11:30 PT. @Rafael Mendes can you run the facilitation?

Rafael Mendes: yup. I can drive. Let’s align on the exact scenario(s) so it’s not just “break stuff and vibe” :)

Allison Grant: +1. We should also validate the Grafana panels + alert routing. Last time we had propagation-lag we only noticed because Console results looked stale.

Aisha Bello: If we do this, I want clear “stop conditions” + a safety plan. We can do it in staging only, but we’ve had staging metrics changes spill over into shared alert channels.

Connor O'Brien: Fwiw release train freeze starts Fri noon. If this uncovers changes, we need a quick decision on whether it blocks.

Ethan Park: From control plane side: we can simulate config propagation lag by forcing the policy engine cache TTL to something huge or by blocking the config watcher stream. There’s a debug knob in staging.

Jordan Lee: For data plane: easiest is to drop/slow the config refresh from experiment-service -> router evaluator. In the evaluator we also have a “treat config missing as disabled” default; we should verify we get the behavior we want when config is stale vs unavailable.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, proposal for the game day plan:
1) Baseline: start a small route experiment on a test route in staging (50/50) and confirm normal attribution + split.
2) Inject lag: block config propagation (simulate 20+ min staleness) while making a config change (e.g. ramp from 50/50 to 90/10).
3) Verify detection: propagation lag metric + alert fires; Console shows banner/guardrail; runbook steps are accurate.
4) Verify mitigation: rollback the change + optionally stop experiment; confirm traffic returns to expected.

Chloe Martin: For Console, we have the “config staleness” banner behind the route experiments flag. It shows when results API sees config_version mismatch > threshold. We should verify the copy + that it links to the runbook.

Allison Grant: Re dashboards/alerts, the ones we care about:
- `experiment_config_propagation_lag_seconds` (p95)
- `experiment_config_staleness_seconds` (max)
- attribution drop rate panel (just to ensure we don’t confuse those)

Logan Wright: Please also keep an eye on telemetry. If propagation is lagged, attribution might be correct but *expected/observed* variant ratio changes will lag. We need to make sure people don’t mis-diagnose it as split skew.

Aisha Bello: Can we define “success” for the game day? Like: alert fires within X minutes of injection; the page includes runbook link; mitigation returns lag metric to normal within Y.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Draft success criteria:
- Alert “Experiment config propagation lag” fires within 5 min of crossing threshold.
- Alert payload includes runbook URL + dashboard link.
- On-call can follow runbook to identify stale config version + verify it’s not just results aggregation lag.
- Mitigation (unblock watcher / restart cache) reduces lag below threshold within 10 min.
- We can confirm the routing decision explains stale version via debug endpoint.

Sean Gallagher: Perfect. Also want to check escalation path: SRE -> Control Plane -> Policy Engine, with clear ownership.

Ethan Park: Ownership wise: propagation pipeline is shared (experiment-service publishes, policy engine consumes). If the watcher stream is blocked, that’s on us (control plane). If evaluator caching is sticky beyond staleness bounds, that’s on policy engine.

Jordan Lee: We also need to confirm the fallback behavior. If config is stale, we should keep using last-known-good; if config is unavailable, we should fail “safe” (no experiment) but still route via normal rollout/fallback policy.

Chloe Martin: Question: do we want to test the “stop experiment” semantics during staleness? i.e., user hits Stop in Console but the router still applies old config for N minutes.

Rafael Mendes: Yes, that’s a great second scenario, but keep it bounded. Maybe do it as step 2b if time permits.

Allison Grant: Also can we ensure the alert doesn’t page *all* of #eng-sre? We should route it to staging-only / low severity for the game day.

Aisha Bello: +1 please. The last attribution-drop alert was noisy and people ignored it after.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. For the game day we’ll temporarily route alerts to `#eng-sre` only (no paging), label as “GAMEDAY”, then revert.

Incident Bot: Reminder: Any alert routing changes must be reverted post-event. Owner: Rafael Mendes. Checklist: update rule -> post change -> validate -> revert.

Rafael Mendes: Noted.

Rafael Mendes: Prep checklist (by EOD Wed):
- [@Ethan Park] confirm staging knob to block watcher stream + how to unblock
- [@Jordan Lee] confirm evaluator cache behavior + safe restart steps
- [@Allison Grant] provide direct Grafana links + confirm alert rule threshold
- [@Chloe Martin] confirm Console banner is enabled in staging + where it sources staleness
- [@Logan Wright] confirm where to query config_version / attribution fields

Ethan Park: For blocking: we can toggle `EXPERIMENT_CONFIG_WATCHER_PAUSE=true` on the policy-engine staging deployment. It pauses consuming updates but keeps serving.

Jordan Lee: If we do that, we should also note what “staleness” means: we track `config_last_refresh_ts` per instance. Alert uses p95 across instances. We should pick a threshold that guarantees it triggers even if one instance is refreshing.

Allison Grant: The current draft alert is p95 > 300s for 10m. For game day we can temporarily set to 120s for 5m, just in staging.

Aisha Bello: Please document the temporary change in-thread and in the runbook so nobody thinks we had an actual incident.

Rafael Mendes: Will do.

Chloe Martin: Console banner: route experiments -> details page -> “Config may be stale” banner. It triggers when results API sees `router_config_version != control_plane_version` AND last_seen_update_age > 5m.

Logan Wright: For attribution / version checks:
- OTel span attrs: `experiment.id`, `experiment.variant`, `experiment.config_version`
- Router logs: `exp_cfg_ver` and `cfg_age_s`
We can query via Honeycomb: `service=router-staging` + group by `exp_cfg_ver`.

Sean Gallagher: Great. During game day, we should record timings in-thread: injection time, alert fire time, mitigation time, recovery time.

Rafael Mendes: Agenda for Thu 10:00 PT:
- 10:00 kickoff + roles (facilitator, observer, scribe)
- 10:10 baseline validate
- 10:20 inject lag + change traffic split
- 10:25 observe metrics + wait for alert
- 10:40 follow runbook + debug endpoint
- 11:00 mitigation + confirm recovery
- 11:15 retro + action items

Connor O'Brien: Works. Please add me to invite; I’ll track any “release blocking” follow-ups.

Allison Grant: I’ll drop dashboard links here before then.

Ethan Park: Confirmed on the knob + revert steps. We should also set a hard stop: if anything looks like it might impact non-staging, we abort and roll back immediately.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also: do not touch shared prod alert routes. Only staging.

Rafael Mendes: ack.

Rafael Mendes: One more: should we test auto-stop? If propagation lag causes guardrail evaluation to misfire, we don’t want auto-stop thrashing.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s not test hard-stop during this game day. Soft-stop/notification only. We can do a separate game day for guardrail false positives.

Jordan Lee: +1. Mixing scenarios will make it hard to interpret.

Allison Grant: agreed.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. I’ll send calendar + keep this thread as the scratchpad. Please add any gotchas / links.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: I’ll post final checklist Wed afternoon.

title: #eng-sre: 1737765510
preview:
Sean Gallagher: starting a thread on SLOs + alert routing for the unit econ pipeline (freshness + completeness). goal: make Finance comfortable using blended $/1M output tokens in exec reporting without waking people up for noise.

Sean Gallagher: current mental model is 3 layers:
1) ingestion (metering events landing)
2) hourly aggregation job (warehouse tables/views)
3) API/dashboard availability (query endpoints)

Sean Gallagher: proposal v0 (numbers up for debate):
- Freshness SLO: 99% of hours have complete aggregates available within 90 min of the hour boundary.
- Completeness SLO: 99.5% of hourly buckets have non-null {model, region, tier} AND output_tokens > 0 where requests > 0.
- Correctness: more tricky; for v1 use “reconciliation delta vs billing” as a weekly check + alert only on large deltas.

Sean Gallagher: alerting idea:
- warn (Slack) if freshness lag > 60 min for 2 consecutive hours
- page (PD) if freshness lag > 120 min OR completeness drops below threshold for 3 consecutive hours

Sean Gallagher: routing question: should this page SRE on-call (infra) or telemetry/analytics on-call (Logan’s rotation) w/ SRE as secondary?

Allison Grant: +1 to splitting freshness vs completeness alerts. freshness tends to be infra/warehouse/backfill contention. completeness often a code deploy/regression.

Allison Grant: on PD routing, I’d prefer primary to telemetry/analytics for completeness + dimension drops. SRE primary for “warehouse down / queries timing out / job stuck” type.

Logan Wright: agree in principle. we do have an analytics oncall but it’s not 24/7 (yet). if we page at 2am it’ll still hit someone, just want to avoid pages for “hourly job took 20 min longer.”

Logan Wright: also note: we have known daylight savings / day-boundary weirdness; don’t want that to trigger pages.

Aisha Bello: can we define freshness in terms of “data available up to hour H-1”? then track lag as now - max(hour_available). easier to compute than “within 90 min of each hour boundary.”

Aisha Bello: i can wire alerts in grafana/prometheus either way but we need crisp signals.

Caleb Johnson: on completeness: should separate “missing dimension coverage” vs “null mapping due to upstream” vs “zero tokens because true zero traffic.”

Caleb Johnson: metric suggestion:
- `econ_hourly_rows_expected` vs `econ_hourly_rows_present` (by required dimensions)
- `econ_null_dim_rate{dim=region|tier|model}`
- `econ_output_tokens_drop_ratio` (current hour vs trailing 24h median)

Caleb Johnson: drop ratio catches the streaming undercount class of bugs better than pure null checks.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also want to explicitly scope v1 to output token metering parity. we’ve already seen streaming path undercount.

Nadia Rahman: completeness SLO should include streaming vs non-streaming parity? maybe as a separate “guardrail metric” with looser SLO for v1.

Mei Lin: on freshness: warehouse side, if we’re depending on materialized view refresh + partitions, 90 min is ok but paging at 120 min might be too aggressive during planned maintenance/backfills.

Mei Lin: maybe:
- slack warn at 90 min lag
- page at 180 min lag
and add “maintenance window label” to silence.

Sean Gallagher: good points. trying to balance Finance expectations (they asked for QTD daily by 9am PT) with operational reality.

Talia Benson: from program POV, let’s align to the deliverable: exec reporting = daily. internal dashboard = hourly. maybe we can have two SLOs: “hourly (engineering)” and “daily (finance view)”.

Allison Grant: yes. two SLOs reduces paging pressure.
- hourly SLO: best effort + Slack alerts
- daily SLO: page if not ready by a cutoff time (e.g., 09:00 PT)

Aisha Bello: we can do that. define:
- `econ_freshness_lag_minutes` (continuous)
- `econ_daily_ready{date=...}` computed at ~09:00 PT based on having 24 hourly buckets for prior day (or 23/25 on DST days)

Logan Wright: routing:
- Slack alerts to #eng-sre + #eng-platform (so everyone sees)
- PD page for “daily not ready by 09:00 PT” -> SRE primary (since it’s an availability SLO)
- PD page for “completeness regression after deploy” -> telemetry oncall primary during business hours, otherwise Slack + next-day followup

Caleb Johnson: i like business-hours paging for completeness. otherwise it becomes a lot of false urgency. we can still have a “SEV2 if huge drop” for 24/7.

Nadia Rahman: define “huge drop” carefully. tokens can legitimately drop due to traffic. but a 70% drop with request count stable is suspicious.

Aisha Bello: we can compare both tokens and request_count. e.g.
- if `requests` within +/- 15% of trailing 24h median AND `output_tokens` down > 50% => anomaly.

Mei Lin: and exclude known low-traffic tiers/regions to avoid flapping.

Sean Gallagher: proposed final-ish:
Signals
1) Freshness lag: `econ_freshness_lag_minutes` = minutes since last successfully published hourly aggregate.
2) Completeness: `econ_required_dim_coverage_pct` and `econ_null_dim_rate`.
3) Anomaly: `econ_output_tokens_per_request` vs trailing baseline (catches undercount).

Alert policy
A) Hourly freshness
- Slack warn: lag > 90 min for 2 consecutive evals
- Slack critical: lag > 180 min
(no PD unless it hits the daily SLO)

B) Daily finance readiness
- PD page (SRE primary): by 09:00 PT, prior day aggregates not complete OR reconciliation view missing

C) Completeness/dimension regression
- Slack warn: required_dim_coverage < 99.5% for 3 consecutive hours
- PD page (Telemetry primary, SRE secondary) only if (coverage < 97% OR null_dim_rate spikes 5x) AND during 08:00–18:00 local (PT)
- 24/7 PD page only for “SEV threshold” anomaly: requests stable AND output_tokens_per_request drops > 60% for 2 hours

Sean Gallagher: does that match everyone’s expectations?

Allison Grant: +1. that seems sane. :thumbsup:

Logan Wright: works for me. we should document owners explicitly: telemetry owns completeness/anomaly runbooks; SRE owns freshness/daily readiness.

Caleb Johnson: i’ll draft the runbook sections for completeness + validation queries (null dims, expected rows, output_tokens/request).

Nadia Rahman: please include a step “check streaming meter parity canary” + link to the canary dashboard once it exists.

Aisha Bello: i can implement the alert rules + routing once we lock the metric names. drop them in the unit-econ grafana folder and tag #eng-sre + #eng-platform.

Mei Lin: i’ll add a note on maintenance/backfill silences. backfills can overload the warehouse and make freshness laggy; we should silence hourly alerts during approved windows.

Talia Benson: action items i captured:
- Sean: write SLO doc draft w/ the split hourly vs daily + owners
- Aisha: implement metrics + alert rules (grafana)
- Caleb: runbook for completeness/anomaly
- Mei: define/schedule maintenance window + backfill throttling guidance
- Logan/Nadia: confirm telemetry metric names + baseline logic

Sean Gallagher: perfect. i’ll post a confluence draft link later today for async review.

- Caleb: runbook for completeness/anomaly
- Mei: define/schedule maintenance window + backfill throttling guidance
- Logan/Nadia: confirm telemetry metric names + baseline logic

Sean Gallagher: perfect. i’ll post a confluence draft link later today for async review.

title: #eng-sre: 1737671139
preview:
Sean Gallagher: need to lock SLO/alert thresholds for Private backup success + restore drill cadence before we wire paging. aiming for something we can defend to customers + not melt on-call. thoughts?

Neha Kapoor: +1. we should separate SLO (reported) vs paging (operational). esp for scheduled jobs.

Allison Grant: can we anchor on the metrics we’re emitting in `pr-24344`? i’ve seen: `redwood_backup_success_total`, `redwood_backup_failure_total`, `redwood_backup_duration_seconds`, `redwood_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds`, and restore drill equivalents.

Sergio Costa: yup. dashboard mock uses `last_success_ts`, `duration`, and a burn-down of failures by reason code.

Rafael Mendes: restore drills: what’s the expectation? weekly in staging, monthly in prod? also do we define RTO as “restore job completed” or “smoke tests green + API serving”?

Miles Thornton: for DR, RTO should be “service functional” (health checks + routing + auth). restore completed but control plane not ready is meaningless.

Sean Gallagher: proposal v0:
- Backup job success SLO (prod): 99.0% over 30d for scheduled backups
- Restore drill SLO (staging): 95% of drills complete (restore+validation) within 90 min over 30d
- Restore drill cadence: weekly (staging), monthly (customer-run in prod recommended; we only page if our automation fails in our staging)
- Alerts:
  - backup hasn’t succeeded in > 26h => page (prod)
  - backup consecutive failures >= 3 => page (prod)
  - restore drill fail => ticket + slack ping, page only if 2 consecutive fails or if regression in duration > 2x baseline

Neha Kapoor: 26h makes sense for daily backups. do we also support 6h cadence customers? we should keep alert window relative to configured schedule.

Allison Grant: +1. alert should use `expected_interval_seconds` label or chart-config value. if we can’t, we can document “default daily schedule” and let customers tune.

Sergio Costa: we can template it in the helm values and surface as metric label (or configmap) consumed by the alert rule.

Sean Gallagher: we *don’t* have `expected_interval` as a metric yet. easiest: alert on `time() - last_success_ts > X` and set X based on schedule in the alert routing (values file).

Miles Thornton: just be careful: if customer intentionally pauses backups during maintenance window, they’ll page themselves unless they silence.

Rafael Mendes: we can include “maintenance silences” in the runbook. also: I prefer paging on “no successful backup within 2x interval” vs hard 26h.

Allison Grant: agree. common pattern:
- warn at 1.5x interval
- page at 2x interval

Sean Gallagher: ok. for default daily schedule => warn at 36h, page at 48h.

Neha Kapoor: 48h is too lax for some enterprise RPOs. but that’s *alerting*, not RPO. they can still do daily backups; we just avoid spurious pages if one job gets skipped.

Miles Thornton: if we’re telling customers “RPO 24h” and we only page at 48h, that’s a mismatch. but we can define paging policy as “operator action required when we’re at risk of breaching RPO.”

Sean Gallagher: fair point. could set page at 30h for daily schedule. gives buffer for retry + still within RPO-ish.

Sergio Costa: we also have retries in CronJob. typical failures resolve on retry #2 within ~30m. paging at 30h after last success is reasonable.

Allison Grant: also we should avoid paging on single failure if next run succeeds. use `last_success_ts` based alert, not per-run failure.

Rafael Mendes: +1. page on “staleness” and “consistently failing”.

Sean Gallagher: drafting alert rules (prometheus-ish):

```yaml
- alert: PrivateBackupStale
  expr: (time() - redwood_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{env="prod"}) > (30 * 60 * 60)
  for: 15m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: "Private backup has not succeeded in >30h"

- alert: PrivateBackupFailuresHigh
  expr: increase(redwood_backup_failure_total{env="prod"}[6h]) >= 3
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: "Private backup failing repeatedly (>=3 failures / 6h)"
```

Allison Grant: nit: if schedule is daily, `increase(failure_total[6h])` might not fire if only 1 run/day. better as “consecutive failures” or “last run failed AND staleness approaching.”

Sergio Costa: we can do "staleness page" + "job failing every retry" detection (cronjob emits multiple failures per run). depends on how the CLI reports retries.

Sean Gallagher: good call. our cron retries are within same run and do emit failures. so 3 failures in 6h could still trigger within a single day.

Neha Kapoor: what about a warning-level alert for visibility?

Allison Grant: suggest:
- warning at 27h (or 1.125x)
- page at 30h

Sean Gallagher: ok.

Rafael Mendes: restore drill: do we page? this is staging automation. paging for staging noise is bad.

Sean Gallagher: proposal: restore drill alerts route to `#eng-sre` + create Linear ticket. page only if (a) 2 consecutive drill failures OR (b) drill duration > 2h (breach) AND it’s within 7 days of scheduled game day / release cut.

Neha Kapoor: i like “no page by default, ticket + slack” but add override: if we’re in “release hardening window” (label/flag), page on failure.

Sergio Costa: we can route based on tag `window=release` in alertmanager (manual toggle). otherwise `severity=notify`.

Allison Grant: define SLI for drill latency as `p95(restore_drill_total_time)`? or just last run duration? p95 over 30d is stable but harder to explain.

Miles Thornton: for drills, last-run is most actionable. the SLO can still be percentile-based. for paging, last run.

Sean Gallagher: ok:
SLO (internal): 95% of staging drills complete within 90m over rolling 90d.
Alert (notify): last drill failed OR last drill duration > 120m.
Page: 2 consecutive failures OR last success > 14d (cadence drift).

Rafael Mendes: “last success > 14d” should be a page. if we’re not exercising restore, we’re blind.

Neha Kapoor: agreed.

Allison Grant: can we align to docs language: "we recommend customers run quarterly full restores" etc. our internal cadence can be weekly staging.

Sean Gallagher: yep. customer guidance can be: daily backups, weekly integrity check, quarterly restore rehearsal (or per policy).

Datadog: [Automated] Monitor suggestion: "redwood_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds staleness" new monitor draft created (not enabled).

Sean Gallagher: lol thanks.

PagerDuty: [Automated] @Sean Gallagher created escalation policy draft "private-backup-restore" (no services attached).

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, i’ll include:
- last_success_age (hours)
- success rate (30d)
- backup duration p50/p95
- artifact bytes
- restore drill: last status, last duration, last success age

Allison Grant: also add a small panel for "silenced" / "maintenance" so folks know why it’s quiet.

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- I’ll update SLO toolkit PR with the above thresholds + routing notes.
- Sergio: implement dashboard + alert rules with default daily schedule, make interval configurable.
- Allison: sanity check wording against observability standards.
- Neha/Rafael: sign off on paging policy (no staging page unless consecutive failures / cadence breach).

Neha Kapoor: :thumbsup: please explicitly state: prod pages only when risk to RPO (backup stale) or repeated failures + no recent success.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: and make sure runbook says how to silence during planned maintenance.

Sean Gallagher: will do. posting summary in the runbook thread too.

Allison Grant: last note: if backup is "successful" but verification fails (checksum/decrypt), that should count as failure. make sure metrics treat that as non-success.

Sergio Costa: yup, the CLI reports `result=verified` vs `result=created_only`. we’ll treat created_only as warn and verified as success.

Sean Gallagher: perfect. done.

title: #eng-sre: 1737489850
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Dropping here for review: draft runbook for assignment debugging (route experiments). Goal is to cover “why is my split wrong / why is a user flipping variants / why are metrics missing” w/ copy-pastable checks. Please add any missing known-bad patterns.

Sean Gallagher: skimming now. Overall solid structure (symptom -> likely causes -> checks). One thing: call out “propagation lag” earlier. On-call keeps chasing hashing issues when it’s just stale config.

Allison Grant: +1. Also we should add a known-bad for “variant 0 gets 100% despite allocation” when evaluator falls back due to config fetch failure.

Jordan Lee: reading. A few nits + patterns we’ve seen:
- low-entropy stickiness key (defaulting to `ip` or empty string) leads to skew + churn
- mixing `customer_id` vs `session_id` across requests in same logical user (some SDKs)
- route id mismatch (raw path vs template) causing the router to think it’s a different route and not apply the experiment

Rafael Mendes: good call on route id mismatch — can you add example phrasing? not sure how to explain succinctly.

Jordan Lee: something like: “If experiment configured on route template `/v1/chat/{model}` but requests emitted as `/v1/chat/gpt-foo` in assignment logs, you can end up with no match (or match a different policy), and users see ‘control’ only.”

Chloe Martin: For Console folks: we also saw people create experiment on `route_id` X but traffic is hitting the older alias route Y (because of rollout policy redirect). Runbook should say: verify `route_id` on request context / headers matches Console route.

Logan Wright: telemetry angle: missing attribution looks like “assignment wrong” but it’s actually “dimension not attached”. Known-bads:
1) streaming retries path missing request context (fixed in pr-28622 but older builds still around in staging)
2) async span boundaries: experiment tags on parent span but aggregation reads child span attrs only
3) sampling drops high-cardinality tags (esp when debug toggle off)

Allison Grant: can we include the canonical “what to look for” in traces? like which OTel attrs and what the expected values are.

Logan Wright: yep, add a snippet:
- `redwood.route_id`
- `redwood.experiment_id`
- `redwood.experiment_variant_id`
- `redwood.policy_config_version`
- `cloud.region`

Sean Gallagher: Another known-bad: “users flip variants across regions” is often because stickiness key differs by edge path or because cross-region hashing salt mismatch (shouldn’t happen post ADR-047 but if you’re hitting mixed versions…)

Vikram Singh: +1, and add “mixed evaluator versions” after partial rollout. We saw eu-west had old bucketing code for ~30 min during a canary. Symptom: split distribution weird + debug endpoint returns different selected variant vs live.

Rafael Mendes: ok. I’ll add a “mixed versions” section w/ how to verify.

Jordan Lee: Another one: overlapping experiments. Even though Console warns, if two experiments target same route and you force-enable via API, precedence can mask one. Symptom looks like “no traffic to variant B” but it’s because experiment A wins.

Chloe Martin: pls include exact Console warning copy + what to do. Support keeps asking.

Rafael Mendes: yep. I’ll add “If overlap detected, stop one or add eligibility rules to disjoint the populations.”

Allison Grant: Also call out “sticky key drift”:
- customer rotates `x-redwood-sticky-key` each request (UUID)
- OR they send different keys for streaming chunks (??)
This shows as perfect 50/50 overall but individual user churn is 100%.

Jordan Lee: yep. And “missing sticky key” fallback behavior should be explicitly documented. What are we doing now? (I recall we changed default from request_id to something else?)

Chloe Martin: current precedence (from Console docs draft):
1) explicit `x-redwood-sticky-key`
2) `customer_id` (if authenticated)
3) `session_id` (if provided)
4) else: hashed `(ip + user_agent)` with warning banner in Console
We also log `sticky_key_source` in debug endpoint response.

Rafael Mendes: perfect — I’ll copy that into the runbook.

Sean Gallagher: can we add a copy/paste curl for the internal debug endpoint? Helps a ton.

Rafael Mendes: yep, something like:
```bash
curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "x-redwood-route-id: rt_123" \
  -H "x-redwood-sticky-key: acct_456" \
  "https://controlplane.prod.redwood.ai/internal/experiments/debug-assignment?region=us-east" | jq
```

Jordan Lee: include expected fields in response: `bucket`, `hash_input`, `variant_id`, `allocation_snapshot`, `config_version`, `eligibility_passed`, `reason`.

Logan Wright: add the query for attribution drop rate. People ask “why are results empty?”
Something like (pseudo):
- dashboard: “Route Experiments / Attribution Drop Rate”
- if `drop_rate > 2%` over 10m, treat metrics as suspect.

Allison Grant: if we can’t link dashboards in the doc, at least give the metric name. What did we settle on?

Logan Wright: `redwood_experiment_attribution_missing_ratio` (by route_id, region). Also `redwood_experiment_tagged_requests_total` vs `redwood_requests_total`.

Sean Gallagher: Another known-bad is cache-related:
- assignment cache keyed incorrectly (route_id missing) -> users get variant from different route
- config cache stale beyond SLO -> traffic sticks to old split
Runbook should say: check `experiment_config_cache_hit_ratio` and `config_staleness_seconds`.

Vikram Singh: for router side, add: “if experiment applied after fallback” regression. We fixed ordering (apply experiment before fallback), but if you see variant always matches fallback model, it’s likely old router build.

Rafael Mendes: good catch. I’ll add symptom:
- “variant model never selected; always see fallback model_id”
and remediation: verify router build >= pr-28457.

Chloe Martin: also Console list page sometimes shows allocations but the backend validator rejected them (it will auto-normalize). Known-bad: user thinks they set 90/10 but stored config is 100/0 due to rounding/validation. Runbook should include: compare Console UI vs API `GET /experiments/{id}` (source of truth).

Allison Grant: +1 and mention “variant 0 traffic” alert. If `variant_id` missing entirely, our dashboards show all traffic in “unknown”. That’s another tell.

Jordan Lee: can we add a tiny “if all else fails” section to ask for logs to attach to ticket? e.g. debug endpoint output + one trace_id.

Rafael Mendes: yep. Proposed checklist:
- experiment_id, route_id, region
- 2 sample sticky keys (one expected control, one expected treatment)
- debug endpoint output for both
- trace_id from a tagged request + from an untagged request
- current `policy_config_version`

Sean Gallagher: looks good. Please also add: “Do not enable hard-stop while debugging assignment issues unless SRE approves” (we had one false positive cascade).

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll add a caution note + link to kill switch instructions.

Logan Wright: last one: metrics cardinality protections. If someone adds `user_id` as a dimension in custom spans, they can accidentally blow up tagging and we’ll start dropping experiment tags. Mention: keep experiment tags limited + don’t add high-cardinality attrs under `redwood.experiment.*`.

Allison Grant: 👍 include “known bad: results dashboard empty during first 15m” b/c aggregation window. People file bugs immediately.

Chloe Martin: yep, aggregation is 5m rollups but Console caches for ~10m. Document expected delay.

Rafael Mendes: awesome, thanks all. I’ll push an updated version w/ these known-bads and the curl + metric names. Will re-post for final pass tomorrow.

Vikram Singh: for router side, add: “if experiment applied after fallback” regression. We fixed ordering (apply experiment before fallback), but if you see variant always matches fallback model, it’s likely old router build.

Rafael Mendes: good catch. I’ll add symptom:
- “variant model never selected; always see fallback model_id”
and remediation: verify router build >= pr-28457.

Chloe Martin: also Console list page sometimes shows allocations but the backend validator rejected them (it will auto-normalize). Known-bad: user thinks they set 90/10 but stored config is 100/0 due to rounding/validation. Runbook should include: compare Console UI vs API `GET /experiments/{id}` (source of truth).

Allison Grant: +1 and mention “variant 0 traffic” alert. If `variant_id` missing entirely, our dashboards show all traffic in “unknown”. That’s another tell.

Jordan Lee: can we add a tiny “if all else fails” section to ask for logs to attach to ticket? e.g. debug endpoint output + one trace_id.

Rafael Mendes: yep. Proposed checklist:
- experiment_id, route_id, region
- 2 sample sticky keys (one expected control, one expected treatment)
- debug endpoint output for both
- trace_id from a tagged request + from an untagged request
- current `policy_config_version`

Sean Gallagher: looks good. Please also add: “Do not enable hard-stop while debugging assignment issues unless SRE approves” (we had one false positive cascade).

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll add a caution note + link to kill switch instructions.

Logan Wright: last one: metrics cardinality protections. If someone adds `user_id` as a dimension in custom spans, they can accidentally blow up tagging and we’ll start dropping experiment tags. Mention: keep experiment tags limited + don’t add high-cardinality attrs under `redwood.experiment.*`.

Allison Grant: 👍 include “known bad: results dashboard empty during first 15m” b/c aggregation window. People file bugs immediately.

Chloe Martin: yep, aggregation is 5m rollups but Console caches for ~10m. Document expected delay.

Rafael Mendes: awesome, thanks all. I’ll push an updated version w/ these known-bads and the curl + metric names. Will re-post for final pass tomorrow.

title: #eng-sre: 1737419905
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Need to lock SLOs + alerts for the audit log pipeline this week so we can ship dashboards + link from the runbook. What’s the proposal for (1) delivery latency and (2) drop rate? Also: do we differentiate Hosted vs Private?

Kevin Osei: For audit pipeline I’d like 2 things:
- ingestion->sink delivery latency (p95)
- event loss (dropped/failed permanently)

Retries will cause duplicates so “error rate” alone isn’t enough. We should alert on “cannot deliver” and “buffer backlog” too.

Aisha Bello: +1. Draft SLO doc in slo-toolkit PR is up: https://github.com/redwood/slo-toolkit/pull/298
Right now it’s:
- Delivery latency SLO: 99.9% of audit events delivered within 5m (enterprise tiers), 15m (standard)
- Drop rate SLO: < 0.01% permanently failed per 30d window

Open Q: do we measure "delivered" at exporter ack vs stored in our canonical store?

Allison Grant: Canonical definition should be “accepted by ingestion + persisted to audit log store” for internal SLO. Exporter is customer-configurable + can be misconfigured, and we’ll otherwise page ourselves for customer’s bad Splunk creds.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. Separate SLI for exporter health (per destination) with lower urgency and different routing.
Also for drop rate: we need to be clear if it’s “producer dropped” vs “ingestion dropped”. If the SDK does best-effort and drops on local queue full, that’s still a drop.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side, we can calculate expected vs observed event types later for coverage, but SLO should be purely transport.
We do have counters in the ingest service already:
- audit_ingest_received_total
- audit_ingest_persisted_total
- audit_ingest_dropped_total
- audit_ingest_queue_depth
- audit_ingest_persist_latency_ms

If we standardize those names, dashboard is straightforward.

Nadia Rahman: One more: cardinality. Don’t break down alerts by tenant_id. :grimacing: We can break down by deployment_mode (hosted/dedicated/private) and region.

Sean Gallagher: Makes sense. For hosted, we can route to oncall; for private, do we still alert? Private has their own infra and might not ship metrics back.

Kevin Osei: For Private, the installer can ship local metrics but not guaranteed. I’d prefer:
- Hosted/Dedicated: paged alerts
- Private: "best effort" health signals in the package + documented, but our oncall alerts should only cover what we operate (control plane + hosted ingest).

Aisha Bello: Dashboard PR is ready in observability-pack: https://github.com/redwood/observability-pack/pull/433
Includes:
- audit ingest p95 persist latency
- queue depth + consumer lag
- drop rate panel
- exporter failures (by destination type)

Alerts in PR are currently:
- DropRateHigh: dropped / received > 0.1% for 5m
- PersistLatencyHigh: p95 > 60s for 10m
- QueueDepthHigh: depth > 50k for 10m

We should sanity-check thresholds; 0.1% seems too high maybe? but we also don’t want noise.

Rafael Mendes: 0.1% for 5m could still be a lot given volume. I’d rather:
- Page if drop rate > 0.01% for 10m AND dropped_total > N
- Warn if > 0.005% for 15m
Otherwise you’ll page on small denominators.

Allison Grant: +1 to combining ratio + absolute count.
Also: for latency, 60s p95 persist might be okay during minor backpressure, but 10m sustained is bad. Maybe warning at 2m p95 for 15m, page at 5m p95 for 15m.

Sean Gallagher: Let's tie to the SLO boundaries. If SLO is “99.9% < 5m”, then paging at p95 5m seems late. But p95 isn’t the same as 99.9%.
Could we do a burn-rate alert off the SLO instead of raw p95?

Aisha Bello: slo-toolkit supports burn rate alerts. In PR-298 I set up:
- fast burn: 14.4x for 5m
- slow burn: 6x for 1h
for both latency SLO + drop SLO.
We need the SLI query for latency as a histogram + "good" under threshold.

Caleb Johnson: We can implement latency SLI as:
- total = persisted_total
- good = persisted_total with persist_latency_ms <= 300000
But that’s not directly available unless we bucket histograms. We already have persist latency histogram in ingest.

Kevin Osei: Works. For drop SLI, total = received_total, bad = dropped_total. (Need to confirm dropped_total means “permanent fail” not “retry scheduled”.)

Rafael Mendes: Important: define “dropped” precisely in code. Retries shouldn’t count as dropped.
Also we need runbook link in the alert annotations.

Sean Gallagher: Runbook is in progress but we can link to draft location now.
Proposed alert routing:
- AuditIngestSLOViolation -> eng-sre oncall (page)
- AuditExporterFailures -> security-eng (ticket) unless it’s global outage

Thoughts?

Allison Grant: Exporter failures shouldn’t page SRE unless it affects all destinations (e.g., exporter service down). Per-destination auth issues should be non-paging.

Kevin Osei: Yup. For exporter, also include guidance for dedupe on retries using event_id (we keep getting questions from customers).

Observability Bot: PR opened: observability-pack#433 “audit log pipeline dashboards and alerts” (author: @aisha-bello). CI: pending.

Aisha Bello: I’ll update PR-433 with:
- ratio + count logic for drop alert
- SLO burn-rate alert config (latency + drop)
- annotations linking to runbook path

Can someone (Sean/Rafa) confirm the runbook URL we want in alerts?

Rafael Mendes: Use the Confluence runbook path we’re standardizing on: /eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook
Once published we’ll update to the canonical link.

Sean Gallagher: OK. Action items:
1) Kevin to confirm dropped_total semantics (permanent only) w/ ingest team
2) Aisha updates PR-433 thresholds + burn-rate
3) Caleb confirms histogram buckets cover 5m threshold
We’ll re-review tomorrow.

Nadia Rahman: Please add a note in dashboard: “don’t slice by tenant_id” + “deployment_mode only”. I’ve seen people do the wrong thing. :facepalm:

Aisha Bello: Will do. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Let’s keep this thread for tweaks; goal is merge by end of week so we can stop flying blind on audit pipeline.

title: #eng-sre: 1736965120
preview:
Allison Grant: flagging a concern before we merge more experiment tags into OTel/metrics. Cardinality risk is real here.

Allison Grant: Current proposal in ADR-048 + PRs is effectively adding `{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}` to a bunch of metrics. For hosted API this can blow up fast (route_id alone is scary if it’s raw paths).

Sean Gallagher: +1. We’ve been burned by label explosions before. If we do this naively it’s going to page SRE and also cost $$$.

Logan Wright: agreed. our intent was attribution correctness, but we can probably be stricter about what goes to metrics vs traces/logs.

Caleb Johnson: the ingestion side can handle some increase, but not “unbounded per customer route + experiment ids + model ids”. also impacts rollups + storage.

Jordan Lee: route_id should be normalized route template, not raw path. We already have a “route template id” in the router. But yeah, if any team tags `path=/v1/chat/completions?foo=bar` we’re cooked.

Allison Grant: exactly. we need an explicit allowlist of dimensions for *metrics*.

Rafael Mendes: can we split this:
1) low-cardinality metrics with dimensions needed for guardrails
2) high-cardinality only in traces (sampled) + logs for debug endpoint

Sean Gallagher: that’s the pattern we want.

Nadia Rahman: also cost metrics: we’re already emitting token counts and $ estimates; adding high-card labels there makes it worse. we can compute per-variant cost in the analytics rollup rather than TSDB.

Logan Wright: for Console results we don’t need per-request metric series in Grafana with every dimension. we need aggregate by `{experiment_id, variant_id, route_id}` over windows.

Caleb Johnson: right. better to emit a single event/metric w attribution, then have the aggregator build the “results table” keyed by those dims. not tag *every* service metric.

Allison Grant: proposal (tell me if anyone objects):
- Metrics dimension allowlist for experiment feature: `{experiment_id, variant_id}` ONLY on a dedicated small set of metrics.
- `route_id` only if it’s normalized + bounded. ideally use an internal numeric route template id.
- `model_id` and `region` do NOT go on the hot path metrics by default. keep them in traces (sampled) and in the rollup table if needed.

Sean Gallagher: i’d go even further: include `route_id` only on rollup outputs, not raw emitted metrics, unless we have a hard cap.

Jordan Lee: we can provide both `route_template_id` and `route_display_name`. `route_display_name` stays in logs/UI; `route_template_id` for join/aggregation.

Rafael Mendes: what about guardrail dashboards? they want to show “p95 latency by variant” for a given experiment. they don’t necessarily need route_id if the dashboard is already scoped to a specific experiment.

Aisha Bello: +1. For oncall we care about:
- experiment health overall
- variant 0 traffic detection
- attribution drop rate
not “every route and model slice”.

Logan Wright: we can scope Grafana dashboards by `experiment_id` + `variant_id` only. If you need route slice, go to Console results view (from the aggregator) or a queryable dataset.

Caleb Johnson: sampling idea: tag on spans at higher cardinality, but sample aggressively:
- 100% sample for errors
- 1-5% for success paths
and ensure experiment tags are present on *sampled* traces.

Allison Grant: careful: sampling can bias latency histograms if we’re not deliberate. but for traces it’s fine.

Nadia Rahman: for cost: we should not sample cost attribution if we’re using it for billing-ish views. but we can keep cost in the analytics pipeline (not Prom).

Sean Gallagher: also: cap experiments per account + cap variants per experiment for GA. otherwise cardinality is “customer controlled”.

Logan Wright: PR-28489 is adding experiment dims broadly on spans + some metrics. We can adjust before merging or follow-up immediately.

Vikram Singh: data plane side: we can gate emission by “experiment active” and only include tags when experiment_id is present. so baseline traffic doesn’t explode.

Allison Grant: gating by active experiment helps but still needs guardrails. experiments can be many + long-lived.

Sean Gallagher: can we enforce TTLs? (auto-expire experiments after N days unless extended). product might not love it but we can at least warn.

Rafael Mendes: we can start with warnings + limits in Console validation (e.g., max 3 active experiments per route / per account). ties to “safe to operate”.

Logan Wright: ok, concrete ask: let’s document “metrics tagging policy”:
- Metrics: dedicated experiment_* metrics with dims `{experiment_id, variant_id}` (and maybe `route_template_id` if we can prove bounded)
- Traces/logs: include `route_template_id`, `model_id`, `region` (sampled)
- Aggregations: build per `{route_template_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id?, region?}` offline for Console

Caleb Johnson: + add a cardinality budget section: e.g. max series = (active_experiments * variants * regions) per cluster, otherwise we reject.

Allison Grant: yes. also add “do not tag with customer_id/user_id/session_id” to avoid someone getting creative.

Jordan Lee: can we add a unit/integration test to prevent adding new labels? like a “tag allowlist check” in CI for the experiment metrics.

Sean Gallagher: love that. we can make it part of observability standards.

GrafanaBot: :warning: Alert rule preview (staging): projected series count increase for `router_request_latency_ms_bucket` with proposed labels is +18x over baseline.

Allison Grant: lol thanks bot. exactly the problem.

Rafael Mendes: action items?

Allison Grant: action items:
1) Logan/Caleb: update emission plan to use dedicated experiment metrics only + allowlist labels.
2) Jordan/Vikram: confirm route_template_id is stable + bounded, and we can join in rollups.
3) Sean/Aisha/Rafael: define “cardinality budget” + alert thresholds for series growth, and put it in the experiment dashboards spec.
4) I’ll add a short writeup to eng-sre standards + comment on PRs to block adding unbounded labels.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please tag me on the PR review. if we get this wrong we’ll be undoing it during beta.

Logan Wright: will do. I’ll also check with Console team whether route slices are strictly needed in Grafana vs Console.

Nadia Rahman: +1. for cost views, we’ll rely on rollup tables anyway.

Allison Grant: thanks all. let’s keep this tight before we enable broader beta.

title: #eng-sre: 1736455521
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock alert thresholds + escalation policy for route experiments before we expand beta. Specifically: config propagation lag + attribution drop rate. Can we propose defaults today and get buy-in from Platform/Telemetry?

Rafael Mendes: yup. I took a first pass based on what we saw in staging last week. Main thing is we should alert on *lag*, not just “router had no config” since the cache masks it.

Allison Grant: +1. Also reminder from observability review: keep alert signals stable, avoid per-{route,experiment,variant} explosions. Alert should be at service/env level with drilldowns.

Rafael Mendes: Draft thresholds:
- Propagation lag (p95) from control plane write -> router seeing version.
  - Warn: > 60s for 10m
  - Page: > 180s for 5m
- Propagation lag (max) / “staleness bound exceeded”
  - Page: any instance reporting > 10m staleness for 2m (this implies we’re way outside SLO)

Aisha Bello: what’s the SLO we’re anchoring to? Control plane doc says “config visible globally in <2m p99” (IIRC). Paging at 3m p95 seems aggressive if p99 is 2m.

Rafael Mendes: fair. I was trying to catch regressions early. We can align to: p99 < 120s. So paging threshold could be p95 > 120s for 10m OR p99 > 180s for 10m.

Allison Grant: We don’t currently emit p99 at the alerting layer unless we pre-aggregate. We can do: alert on `propagation_lag_seconds` histogram quantile. But keep it simple.

Logan Wright: Telemetry side: we can emit a gauge `experiment_config_propagation_lag_seconds{env,region}` based on “latest seen version timestamp - latest written timestamp”. That gives us a single number per region. For p95/p99 you’d need per-router instance histos.

Caleb Johnson: +1. In otel metrics we already have per-router instance histo (PR-28631). We can aggregate by region. We can alert on p95 over 5m.

Jordan Lee: From policy engine side, one nuance: even with lag, router may choose safe defaults (control variant) so customer impact isn’t always immediate. But if lag is high during active ramp, it screws split accuracy + results.

Sean Gallagher: So we should have two alerts?
1) propagation lag (data freshness)
2) “assignment drift” / split skew? (maybe later)

Rafael Mendes: For GA we said we’d have “variant 0 traffic” detection + attribution drop. Split skew detection is in the dashboards spec but paging is tricky.

Dylan Brooks: Console team would love a crisp definition. When customers complain “results stale”, it’s either aggregation lag or config propagation. We need which one pages who.

Aisha Bello: Attribution drop rate alert proposal:
- Metric: `experiment_attribution_present_rate = attributed_requests / total_requests` (or inverse drop)
- Warn: present_rate < 99.5% for 15m
- Page: present_rate < 98% for 10m

Allison Grant: I’d rather express as drop rate:
- Warn: drop_rate > 0.5% for 15m
- Page: drop_rate > 2% for 10m
Also: exclude low-traffic env/regions or we’ll page on noise. Add a min volume condition.

Caleb Johnson: Please add “min total_requests >= 5k over window” guard. Otherwise a couple streaming retries missing tags will trip it.

Logan Wright: We also need to separate “attribution missing” vs “attribution unknown/empty because no experiment”. For the alert, denominator should be requests *eligible for experiments* (i.e., route has active experiment OR request carried experiment_id expectation). Otherwise it’ll look like we’re dropping when we’re not running anything.

Jordan Lee: router can set `experiment_expected=true` when it evaluates an active experiment but fails to attach variant fields. That’s a good denominator.

Rafael Mendes: ok, so:
- `experiment_expected_requests` vs `experiment_attributed_requests`
- drop_rate = 1 - attributed/expected
Alert only when expected_requests >= 5k/10m.

Sean Gallagher: what about escalation policy? I’d like: warn goes to #eng-sre + #eng-platform (no page). page goes to SRE primary, with auto-cc platform oncall if it’s propagation lag.

Allison Grant: Agree. Also add runbook links directly in alert annotations. (We’ve been bad about this.)

Rafael Mendes: Draft escalation:
- Propagation lag page: SRE primary pages, auto creates incident if > 10m staleness OR multi-region p95 > threshold. Escalate to Platform Control Plane oncall after 15m if not resolved.
- Attribution drop page: SRE primary pages, but first responders likely Telemetry (Logan/Caleb) if it’s ingestion/tagging. Escalate to Router/Policy if correlated with deploy.

Aisha Bello: We need severity mapping:
- Sev2 if active beta customers impacted (any prod region) + lag > 10m OR attribution drop > 5%.
- Otherwise Sev3 (investigate, mitigate, no customer comms unless asked).

Dylan Brooks: can we add a Console banner condition? Like if propagation lag > 5m in region, show “config propagation delayed” in experiment UI. That would cut support tickets.

Sean Gallagher: Good idea but not in this thread :) Focus on alerting.

Rafael Mendes: Linking draft runbook (WIP): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Experiment+Config+Propagation+%26+Attribution+Runbook
Sections include: check `propagation_lag` dashboard, verify config service health, router cache stats, and attribution sanity queries.

Allison Grant: I reviewed it quickly: please add a “known bad pattern” for missing tags on streaming retries (we hit this in staging). Reference the fix PR and the workaround.

Caleb Johnson: also add query snippets:
- `sum(rate(experiment_expected_requests[5m])) by (region)`
- `sum(rate(experiment_attributed_requests[5m])) by (region)`
- and the join with deploy markers.

Logan Wright: Re thresholds: I’d propose slightly looser for beta expansion:
Propagation lag (region gauge):
- Warn: > 120s for 15m
- Page: > 300s for 10m
Attribution drop:
- Warn: > 0.5% for 15m (min 5k expected/10m)
- Page: > 2% for 10m (min 5k expected/10m)

Aisha Bello: +1. Paging at 5m lag seems reasonable. Anything under 2m is “annoying but ok”, esp since auto-stop relies on correct telemetry more than instantaneous config.

Jordan Lee: Heads up: during rollouts, we can see a short spike in lag when config churn is high. Please ensure alert window smooths that (10m is fine).

Allison Grant: Can we add a separate alert for “no config fetch successes” (router can’t reach config service) with faster paging? That’s a true outage.

Rafael Mendes: yes, that’s basically availability, separate from lag:
- `experiment_config_fetch_success_rate < 99% for 5m` warn
- `< 95% for 5m` page
(min attempts >= 1k)

Sean Gallagher: ok I’m good with:
1) Config fetch availability (fast page)
2) Propagation lag (slower page)
3) Attribution drop (slower page)
With volume guards + runbook links.

Dylan Brooks: please post final thresholds in the dashboards spec doc too so Console can reflect guardrails + “data may be delayed” messaging.

Rafael Mendes: will do. I’ll update alert rules PR to match these. @Allison can you sanity check cardinality + labels?

Allison Grant: yup. Keep labels to {env,region,service}. No experiment_id.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Let’s consider this agreed unless someone objects by EOD. :thumbsup:

Caleb Johnson: :thumbsup: I’ll make sure the attribution expected/attributed counters are in prod dashboards.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: Telemetry team ok with this. Ping us when you wire the oncall routing for attribution drop.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: from policy engine side.

Rafael Mendes: :white_check_mark: I’ll incorporate feedback + add the streaming retry known-issue note in the runbook.

Sean Gallagher: We need to lock alert thresholds + escalation policy for route experiments before we expand beta. Specifically: config propagation lag + attribution drop rate. Can we propose defaults today and get buy-in from Platform/Telemetry?

Rafael Mendes: yup. I took a first pass based on what we saw in staging last week. Main thing is we should alert on *lag*, not just “router had no config” since the cache masks it.

Allison Grant: +1. Also reminder from observability review: keep alert signals stable, avoid per-{route,experiment,variant} explosions. Alert should be at service/env level with drilldowns.

Rafael Mendes: Draft thresholds:
- Propagation lag (p95) from control plane write -> router seeing version.
  - Warn: > 60s for 10m
  - Page: > 180s for 5m
- Propagation lag (max) / “staleness bound exceeded”
  - Page: any instance reporting > 10m staleness for 2m (this implies we’re way outside SLO)

Aisha Bello: what’s the SLO we’re anchoring to? Control plane doc says “config visible globally in <2m p99” (IIRC). Paging at 3m p95 seems aggressive if p99 is 2m.

Rafael Mendes: fair. I was trying to catch regressions early. We can align to: p99 < 120s. So paging threshold could be p95 > 120s for 10m OR p99 > 180s for 10m.
